Mention any two examples of GRAS substances.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - (1 mark) **Definition:** GRAS (Generally Recognized As Safe) is a US-FDA designation under Section 201(s) of the Federal Food, Drug and Cosmetic Act, 1958, for substances whose safety under intended conditions of use is **generally recognized by qualified experts** based on scientific procedures or pre-1958 common use in food, and which are therefore **exempt from formal food additive tolerance approval**. - (1 mark) **Example 1:** **Salt (sodium chloride)** — long history of safe use in food predating 1958. - (1 mark) **Example 2:** **Lecithin (soy lecithin)** — used as an emulsifier; safety established through scientific procedures and general recognition. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between a GRAS substance and a non-GRAS food additive (any three points).** **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):** 1. **Regulatory basis:** GRAS is based on *general recognition of safety* (FD&C Act § 201(s)); non-GRAS food additives require a formal **Food Additive Petition** under § 409. 2. **FDA pre-market approval:** GRAS substances do **not** require pre-market FDA approval; non-GRAS additives **must** obtain it. 3. **Evidence path:** GRAS can be established via *pre-1958 common-use history*; non-GRAS additives must demonstrate safety solely through *modern scientific toxicological evidence*. 4. **Example:** *Salt* (GRAS) vs. a newly developed synthetic color requiring § 409 petition (non-GRAS). --- ## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice — 14 Marks) > Each Part B question carries two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. --- ### ✏️ QUESTION A (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`** **(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the **two statutory pathways** through which a substance can be classified as GRAS under 21 CFR § 170.30. Discuss the **"Two-Criterion Test"** that a substance must satisfy to qualify. **(b)** *(7 marks)* A 2-year chronic toxicity study on a proposed natural antioxidant reports a NOAEL of **1,500 mg/kg bw/day** in rats. Calculate the ADI using a safety factor of 100. If the intended maximum use level in breakfast cereals is **800 mg/kg** and the per-capita daily consumption of breakfast cereals is **0.04 kg/day** (for a 60 kg adult), determine whether the intended use satisfies the GRAS safety criterion. --- ### ✅ MODEL ANSWER — QUESTION A #### Part (a) — 7 marks **Pathway A — GRAS through Scientific Procedures (3 marks):** - The safety of the substance is established through **the same quantity and quality of scientific evidence** as would be required for a food additive regulation under Section 409. - Evidence sources include: (i) controlled animal toxicology studies, (ii) human clinical/epidemiological data, (iii) in vitro and genotoxicity assays, (iv) structure-activity relationship (SAR) analyses, (v) published peer-reviewed literature. - The evidence must be **publicly available** in the **general scientific literature** so that it can be independently evaluated by any qualified expert — this is the "**general recognition**" requirement (1 mark). - The conclusion must be reached through **consensus** among qualified experts, not merely the manufacturer's own researchers (1 mark). **Pathway B — GRAS through Experience Based on Common Use in Food (2 marks):** - Available only for substances with a **substantial history of consumption in food before January 1, 1958**. - Evidence of safety is inferred from **long-term customary use** with **no documented evidence of harm** at customary consumption levels. - The history must be **substantial** (i.e., widespread, prolonged, and at meaningful dietary levels) — mere trace or occasional use is insufficient (1 mark). **Two-Criterion Test (2 marks):** 1. **Technical/Safety Criterion:** Adequate demonstration of safety under the intended conditions of use (1 mark). 2. **General Recognition Criterion:** The safety determination must be **generally recognized** by qualified experts, not merely the manufacturer's own determination (1 mark). 3. Both criteria must be satisfied **simultaneously**; failure of either = food additive petition route. #### Part (b) — 7 marks **Step 1 — Compute the ADI (2 marks):** $$\text{ADI} = \dfrac{\text{NOAEL}}{\text{SF}} = \dfrac{1{,}500 \;\text{mg/kg bw/day}}{100} = 15 \;\text{mg/kg bw/day}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the Estimated Daily Intake (EDI) (3 marks):** $$\text{EDI} = \dfrac{C_i \times F_i}{\text{Body Weight}} = \dfrac{800 \;\text{mg/kg} \times 0.04 \;\text{kg/day}}{60 \;\text{kg}}$$ $$\text{EDI} = \dfrac{32 \;\text{mg/day}}{60 \;\text{kg}} = 0.533 \;\text{mg/kg bw/day}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the Safety Margin (1 mark):** $$\text{Safety Margin} = \dfrac{\text{ADI}}{\text{EDI}} = \dfrac{15}{0.533} \approx 28.1\times$$ **Step 4 — Conclusion (1 mark):** > The safety margin is **≈ 28×**, which is **below the conventional 100× benchmark** typically required by FDA/JECFA for a robust GRAS submission. The intended use **marginally fails** the conventional GRAS safety criterion. The manufacturer should either: > - **Reduce the maximum use level** in breakfast cereals (e.g., to ≤ 200 mg/kg), **or** > - **Generate additional evidence** (e.g., a NOAEL > 1,500 mg/kg from a more sensitive study endpoint) to widen the margin. --- ### ✏️ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Internal Alternative to Question A* **`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`** **(a)** *(7 marks)* What are the **two administrative routes** a manufacturer may use to establish GRAS status in the USA? Compare them in terms of FDA involvement, cost, timeline, and regulatory certainty. **(b)** *(7 marks)* Discuss the **Delaney Clause** and its implication for GRAS determinations. With the help of a flowchart, outline the step-by-step decision process an applicant follows to determine whether a substance qualifies for GRAS status. --- ### ✅ MODEL ANSWER — QUESTION B #### Part (a) — 7 marks | Feature | Self-Determined (Self-Affirmed) GRAS (3 marks) | GRAS Notification — GRN (4 marks) | |---------|------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------| | **FDA involvement** | None — manufacturer acts on its own | Voluntary submission to FDA's OFAS | | **Independent expert panel** | Required; conclusions documented in a GRAS dossier | Required; dossier submitted along with notification | | **FDA response** | No formal response | "**No Questions Letter**" or deficiency letter | | **Cost** | Lower (~$50K–$150K) | Higher (~$200K–$300K+) | | **Timeline** | 6 – 18 months | 6 – 24 months for FDA review | | **Regulatory certainty** | Lower — FDA may later challenge the determination | Higher — FDA's "no questions" creates de-facto endorsement | | **Market acceptance** | Often sufficient for buyers and retailers | Strongly preferred by large FMCG firms and for international filings | | **Risk of enforcement** | Higher — FDA can issue warning letters | Very low after "No Questions" letter | > [!NOTE] > For 1 mark: a clear example of self-affirmed GRAS substance is **steviol glycosides** (early determinations) and an example of GRN is **GRN 861 — pea protein**. #### Part (b) — 7 marks **Delaney Clause (3 marks):** - Added in **1960** as a proviso to the Food Additives Amendment of 1958. - **Prohibits the approval of any food additive shown to cause cancer in humans or animals at any dose** ("zero-risk" standard). - It applies to **both** food additives *and* GRAS substances — a substance is **not** GRAS if it is a carcinogen, regardless of potency. - **Implication for GRAS:** Even a substance with overwhelming safety data will be **denied GRAS status** if it is found to be carcinogenic; the manufacturer must abandon the GRAS route and the substance cannot be used in food. **Flowchart of the GRAS Decision Process (4 marks):** ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Substance Proposed for Use in Food] --> B{Is it an additive or GRAS candidate?} B -- "Additive" --> C[Food Additive Petition Route] B -- "GRAS candidate" --> D{Available Evidence?} D -- "Pre-1958 use" --> E[Pathway B: Common Use] D -- "Scientific data" --> F[Pathway A: Scientific Procedures] E --> G{Qualified experts agree on safety?} F --> G G -- "No" --> C G -- "Yes" --> H{Is it carcinogenic?} H -- "Yes - Delaney triggered" --> C H -- "No" --> I{General recognition by experts?} I -- "No" --> C I -- "Yes" --> J{Administrative route?} J -- K1[Self-Affirmed GRAS] J -- K2[GRAS Notification GRN] K2 --> L{FDA No Questions Letter?} L -- "Yes" --> M[Commercial Use Permitted] L -- "No" --> C K1 --> M ``` --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places where students lose marks in GRAS questions:** > 1. **Confusing GRAS with FDA Approval:** GRAS does *not* mean the FDA has *approved* the substance. It means the safety is *generally recognized* by experts. This distinction is worth 2–3 marks. > 2. **Forgetting the 1958 cut-off:** Many students write "GRAS is for old substances" without specifying the **January 1, 1958** date. Always quote the year. > 3. **Ignoring the Delaney Clause:** Failing to mention that GRAS status is **automatically denied** to carcinogens costs marks on long-answer questions. > 4. **Mixing up the two routes:** The **statutory pathways (A and B)** and the **administrative routes (Self-affirmed and GRN)** are *separate* concepts; do not conflate them. > 5. **Calculation errors in ADI:** Students often use the **wrong safety factor**. The default is **100**, but additional factors of 2–10 may apply for sensitive subpopulations, enzyme preparations, or endocrinal effects. > 6. **Missing the role of the independent GRAS panel:** A self-affirmed GRAS determination *must* be supported by a **third-party expert panel** — a manufacturer-only determination is invalid. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **GRAS = Generally Recognized As Safe** — a US-FDA designation, not a global standard. - ✅ Established under **Section 201(s) of the FD&C Act, 1958**, as amended. - ✅ **Two statutory pathways:** (A) Scientific Procedures and (B) Pre-1958 Common Use. - ✅ **Two administrative routes:** (1) Self-Affirmed GRAS and (2) GRAS Notification (GRN). - ✅ **NO GRAS for carcinogens** — the **Delaney Clause (1960)** overrides safety. - ✅ **Key formula:** $\text{ADI} = \dfrac{\text{NOAEL}}{\text{SF}}$, with default **SF = 100**. - ✅ **Safety demonstration:** $\text{EDI} \ll \text{ADI}$ (margin ≥ 100× preferred). - ✅ **Examples of GRAS substances:** Salt, sugar, vinegar, lecithin, BHT, aspartame, steviol glycosides, sodium benzoate, many natural flavors. - ✅ **International analogues:** EFSA (EU), JECFA (Codex/WHO/FAO), FSSAI (India), FSANZ (Australia/NZ), MHLW (Japan). - ✅ GRAS does **not** exempt a substance from **ingredient declaration** on the food label. - ✅ Self-affirmed GRAS carries **no FDA endorsement** — only GRN with a "No Questions Letter" provides regulatory comfort. - ✅ GRAS dossiers must rely on **publicly available** evidence so that **any qualified expert** can verify safety ("general recognition").

topics.title: Maximum Permissible Limits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT504 - FOOD ADDITIVES AND FLAVORINGS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Maximum Permissible Limits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Maximum Permissible Limits (MPL)** — also called **Maximum Permitted Levels (MPL)** or **Maximum Levels (ML)** — are the **maximum concentrations of a food additive, contaminant, or processing aid that is legally permitted to be present in a specified food or food category**, as mandated by national and international food safety regulatory authorities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Maximum Permissible Limits represent the highest legally tolerated concentration of an additive in a finished food product, expressed in **mg/kg** (parts per million) or **percent (%)** of the food, established by bodies such as the **Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI)**, the **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC)**, the **U.S. Food and Drug Administration (USFDA)**, and the **European Food Safety Authority (EFSA)**.

### 1.2 The Toxicological Hierarchy of Safety

MPLs are never arbitrary. They are derived from a strict toxicological hierarchy:

$$\text{MPL} \;\leftarrow\; f\!\left(\text{ADI},\;\text{Food Consumption Data}\right)$$

$$\text{ADI} \;=\; \frac{\text{NOAEL (or LOAEL)}}{\text{Safety Factor}}$$

| Term | Full Form | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **NOAEL** | No Observed Adverse Effect Level | Highest dose at which no statistically significant adverse effect is observed |
| **LOAEL** | Lowest Observed Adverse Effect Level | Lowest dose at which an adverse effect is observed |
| **ADI** | Acceptable Daily Intake | Amount of a chemical that can be ingested daily over a lifetime with no appreciable health risk |
| **SF** | Safety Factor | Uncertainty factor (commonly **100**) applied to extrapolate animal data to humans |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **🚦 Road Speed Limit Analogy — The Best Way to Understand MPL**
> 
> Imagine a city sets a **speed limit of 60 km/h** on a highway. This is **not** the speed at which a car becomes dangerous in a single instant; rather, it is a **scientifically calculated buffer** that keeps drivers safe under all conditions — poor visibility, wet roads, tired drivers, and unexpected pedestrians.
> 
> An **MPL works exactly the same way**:
> - The **car** = the food additive (e.g., a preservative like **Sodium Benzoate**).
> - The **speed limit** = the **MPL** (e.g., **1500 mg/kg** in soft drinks as per FSSAI).
> - The **accident risk** = the **toxicological hazard**.
> - The **safety cushion** = the **Safety Factor of 100**.
> 
> Just as exceeding a speed limit does **not** guarantee an accident but **does** increase statistical risk, exceeding an MPL does not cause immediate harm but **legally and statistically** raises the long-term health risk beyond what regulators consider acceptable.

### 1.4 Why MPLs Exist — The Three Pillars

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pillar 1 — Consumer Safety:** Protect the consumer from cumulative toxicological risk.
> **Pillar 2 — Trade Facilitation:** Harmonize food safety across borders under WTO/SPS agreements.
> **Pillar 3 — Technological Need:** Permit the minimum amount of additive necessary to achieve the desired technological function (the **Principle of Quantum Satis**).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic — How an MPL is Set

The determination of an MPL is a **multi-stage risk assessment pipeline**. KTU examiners frequently test this flow in Part B questions.

1. **Hazard Identification** — Identify the intrinsic toxicological properties of the additive.
2. **Dose–Response Assessment** — Establish the **NOAEL** through chronic animal feeding studies (typically 90-day rodent and 2-year carcinogenicity studies).
3. **ADI Derivation** — Divide NOAEL by a Safety Factor (default **100** = 10 for interspecies × 10 for intraspecies variability).
4. **Dietary Exposure Assessment** — Estimate the **per capita daily consumption** of the food(s) in which the additive will be used, multiplied by the proposed use level.
5. **Risk Characterization** — Compare the estimated dietary exposure with the ADI. If Estimated Daily Intake (EDI) < ADI, the use level is **acceptable**.
6. **MPL Publication** — The regulatory authority fixes a **statutory maximum level** in the food standard, often with a built-in safety margin below the level justified by the risk assessment.

> [!NOTE]
> **🔑 The Golden Rule of MPL Setting:**
> $$\text{Estimated Daily Intake (EDI)} \;\leq\; \text{Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI)}$$
> If this condition fails, the proposed use level must be reduced or the use **banned** in that food category.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\text{ADI} = \dfrac{\text{NOAEL}}{\text{SF}}$ | Acceptable Daily Intake | mg/kg body weight/day |
| 2 | $\text{EDI} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(C_i \times F_i\right)$ | Estimated Daily Intake (additive-wise) | mg/kg bw/day |
| 3 | $\text{MPL}_i = \dfrac{\text{ADI} \times \text{BW}}{\displaystyle\sum_{j} F_{ij} \times 10^{-3}}$ | Maximum Permissible Limit in food *i* | mg/kg food |
| 4 | $\text{Margin of Exposure (MOE)} = \dfrac{\text{NOAEL}}{\text{Estimated Human Exposure}}$ | Used for contaminants (non-threshold) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\text{Percentage of ADI consumed} = \dfrac{\text{EDI}}{\text{ADI}} \times 100$ | Risk utilization indicator | % |
| 6 | $\text{SF} = 10_{\text{interspecies}} \times 10_{\text{intra-species}} = 100$ | Default safety factor | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** When solving MPL problems, students often confuse **mg/kg of food** with **mg/kg of body weight**. Always check whether the answer is requested in **concentration in the food matrix** or **dose per kg body weight per day** — these are two completely different units.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering & Industrial Utility

- **Food Formulation Engineering:** Product development scientists use MPLs to design **compliant formulations** (e.g., a soft drink manufacturer must hold total benzoate ≤ **1500 mg/kg**).
- **Quality Assurance (QA):** QA laboratories set **in-house action limits at 75% of MPL** as a precautionary trigger before regulatory non-compliance.
- **Regulatory Affairs:** Companies must file **Food Safety Applications** to FSSAI, demonstrating that the proposed use level keeps EDI < ADI.
- **International Trade:** Export shipments must meet the **stricter of the two** standards — the exporting country's MPL or the importing country's MPL.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Deriving ADI from NOAEL

The **Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI)** is the daily exposure level that, over an entire lifetime, is **expected to be without appreciable health risk** to the consumer.

**Starting Point (Animal Toxicology):**
A chronic feeding study on rats identifies the **No Observed Adverse Effect Level (NOAEL)** in mg/kg body weight/day.

**Step 1 — Interspecies Extrapolation:**
Rats and humans do not metabolize identically. A factor of **10** accounts for this biological difference.

$$\text{Human Equivalent Dose} = \frac{\text{NOAEL}_{\text{rat}}}{10}$$

**Step 2 — Intraspecies (Human Variability) Extrapolation:**
Humans vary by age, sex, genetics, health, and diet. A further factor of **10** accounts for this.

$$\text{ADI} = \frac{\text{NOAEL}_{\text{rat}}}{10 \times 10}$$

**Step 3 — Final Consolidated Expression:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ADI} \;=\; \frac{\text{NOAEL}}{\text{SF}} \quad \text{where} \quad \text{SF} = 10_{\text{inter}} \times 10_{\text{intra}} = 100
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** If a rodent NOAEL is **250 mg/kg bw/day**, the ADI becomes **2.5 mg/kg bw/day** — a 100-fold safety cushion before any human exposure is considered.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Estimating Dietary Intake (EDI)

The **Estimated Daily Intake (EDI)** is calculated by summing the additive intake from every food category in which the additive is used.

**Step 1 — Define Variables:**
- $C_i$ = concentration (use level) of the additive in food *i* (mg/kg food).
- $F_i$ = daily per-capita consumption of food *i* (g/person/day).
- $BW$ = average body weight (typically **60 kg** for an Indian adult per FSSAI conventions).

**Step 2 — Build the Additive Intake Equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Intake from food } i \;=\; \frac{C_i \;\times\; F_i}{1000} \quad \left[\text{mg/person/day}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

The factor $1000$ converts grams of food to kilograms of food (since $C_i$ is per kg food).

**Step 3 — Sum Across All Foods Containing the Additive:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Intake} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i \times F_i}{1000} \quad \left[\text{mg/person/day}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Normalize to Body Weight (the ADI Unit):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EDI} \;=\; \frac{1}{\text{BW}} \times \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i \times F_i}{1000} \quad \left[\text{mg/kg bw/day}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Back-Calculating MPL from ADI

**Scenario:** A regulator wants to set the MPL for additive *X* in a single food category consumed at $F$ g/day.

**Step 1 — Rearrange the EDI Equation to Solve for $C$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EDI} \times \text{BW} \;&=\; \frac{C \times F}{1000} \\
C \;&=\; \frac{\text{EDI} \times \text{BW} \times 1000}{F}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute EDI = ADI (the Maximum Safe Dose):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MPL} \;&=\; \frac{\text{ADI} \times \text{BW} \times 1000}{F} \quad \left[\text{mg/kg food}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that **MPL is inversely proportional to consumption** $F$. A staple food eaten in large quantities (e.g., wheat flour) gets a **much lower MPL** than an occasional food (e.g., ice cream), even for the same additive.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solved Example for Board Exam Practice (14-Mark Standard):**
> 
> An additive has a **NOAEL of 500 mg/kg bw/day** in rats. The safety factor is **100**. The average body weight of an Indian consumer is **60 kg**. The food category is **soft drinks**, consumed at **200 mL/person/day** (assume density = 1 g/mL, so $F = 200$ g/day). Calculate the **MPL in mg/kg of soft drink** and verify whether a proposed use level of **400 mg/kg** is compliant.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate ADI:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ADI} \;=\; \frac{\text{NOAEL}}{\text{SF}} \;=\; \frac{500 \;\text{mg/kg bw/day}}{100} \;=\; 5 \;\text{mg/kg bw/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate EDI for the Proposed Use Level of 400 mg/kg:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EDI} \;=\; \frac{C \times F}{1000 \times \text{BW}} \;=\; \frac{400 \;\text{mg/kg} \times 200 \;\text{g/day}}{1000 \times 60 \;\text{kg}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EDI} \;=\; \frac{80{,}000}{60{,}000} \;=\; 1.333 \;\text{mg/kg bw/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare EDI with ADI:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EDI} = 1.333 \;\text{mg/kg bw/day} \quad < \quad \text{ADI} = 5 \;\text{mg/kg bw/day} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

The additive only utilizes $1.333 / 5 = 26.67\%$ of the ADI budget. **The proposed use level is compliant.**

**Step 4 — Calculate the Maximum Permissible Limit (MPL):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MPL} \;=\; \frac{\text{ADI} \times \text{BW} \times 1000}{F} \;=\; \frac{5 \times 60 \times 1000}{200} \;=\; 1500 \;\text{mg/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verdict:** The regulator can set the MPL at **1500 mg/kg**, and the proposed **400 mg/kg** use level is comfortably within the legal limit.

> [!NOTE]
> This is precisely why **FSSAI's MPL for Sodium Benzoate in soft drinks is 1500 mg/kg** — it is a real-world example of this calculation.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common FSSAI MPL Values (Memorize These)

| Additive | Food Category | FSSAI MPL | JECFA ADI |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sodium Benzoate** | Soft drinks | **1500 mg/kg** | 0–5 mg/kg bw |
| **Benzoic Acid** | Pickles, sauces | **1000 mg/kg** | 0–5 mg/kg bw |
| **Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂)** | Fruit juices | **350 mg/kg** | 0–0.7 mg/kg bw |
| **Sorbic Acid** | Cheese, baked goods | **1000 mg/kg** | 0–25 mg/kg bw |
| **BHA / BHT (Antioxidants)** | Fats, oils | **200 mg/kg** (individually or combined) | 0–0.5 mg/kg bw |
| **Nitrite (NaNO₂)** | Cured meats | **200 mg/kg** | 0–0.07 mg/kg bw |
| **Curcumin (Natural Colour)** | General foods | **GMP (Quantum Satis)** | 0–3 mg/kg bw |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "**MPL of SO₂ is 70 mg/kg**" — the correct FSSAI value is **350 mg/kg** for fruit juices. Always cross-check the **food matrix** before stating an MPL.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Figure 1 — MPL Determination Flow (Mermaid Flowchart)

The following schematic represents the **regulatory and toxicological pipeline** used by FSSAI, JECFA, and Codex to derive an MPL.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["A1 Identify Chemical Hazard"] --> B["A2 Conduct Animal Feeding Studies"]
    B --> C["A3 Determine NOAEL in mg per kg bw per day"]
    C --> D{"A4 Is it a Threshold Toxicant"}
    D -- Yes --> E["A5 Apply Safety Factor of 100"]
    D -- No --> F["A6 Use Margin of Exposure Approach"]
    E --> G["A7 Derive ADI Value"]
    F --> H["A7b Set Reference Dose or Benchmark"]
    G --> I["A8 Estimate Daily Dietary Exposure EDI"]
    H --> I
    I --> J{"A9 Is EDI less than ADI"}
    J -- Yes --> K["A10 Fix MPL in Food Standard with Safety Buffer"]
    J -- No --> L["A11 Reduce Use Level or Ban Application"]
    K --> M["A12 Publish in FSSAI Codex or EFSA Regulation"]
    L --> M
```

### 4.2 Figure 2 — Three-Tier Regulatory Framework (Mermaid Block Diagram)

This schematic shows how **MPLs are harmonized globally** across the principal regulatory bodies, all of which use the same NOAEL → ADI → MPL pipeline but may differ in numerical values.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Sci["A Scientific Advisory Layer"]
        J1["JECFA Joint FAO WHO Expert Committee"]
        JMPR["JMPR for Pesticide Residues"]
    end
    subgraph Nat["B National Regulatory Layer"]
        FS["FSSAI India"]
        US["USFDA United States"]
        EU["EFSA Europe"]
        JP["MHLW Japan"]
    end
    subgraph Code["C International Standards Layer"]
        CX["Codex Alimentarius Commission"]
    end
    Sci -- Provides ADI NOAEL Data --> Nat
    Sci -- Provides Risk Assessments --> Code
    Nat -- Implements National MPL --> Code
    Code -- Publishes Codex MRLs --> Nat
```

### 4.3 Figure 3 — Risk Margin Visualization (Mermaid Block Diagram)

This diagram illustrates the **safety margin pyramid** — the relationship between NOAEL, ADI, and MPL in graphical conceptual form.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["P1 NOAEL 100x Margin eg 250 mg per kg bw per day"] --> P2["P2 ADI 1x Reference Dose eg 2.5 mg per kg bw per day"]
    P2 --> P3["P3 EDI Estimated Intake in Practice eg 0.5 mg per kg bw per day"]
    P3 --> P4["P4 MPL Statutory Ceiling in Food eg 1500 mg per kg"]
    P4 --> P5["P5 Actual Industry Use Level eg 400 mg per kg"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Pyramid:** Each descending layer represents a **tighter and more practical constraint**. The actual industry use level is the **smallest** value, sitting safely below the statutory ceiling (MPL), which itself sits below the EDI, which sits below the ADI, which sits below the toxicological NOAEL.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PBFTT504]`
> **Define Maximum Permissible Limits (MPL).** Name the international and Indian regulatory bodies responsible for fixing MPLs of food additives.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Definition (2 marks):** Maximum Permissible Limits (MPL) are the **maximum concentrations of a food additive legally permitted in a specified food or food category**, expressed in **mg/kg** (ppm) or as a **percentage of the food**, derived from toxicological studies (NOAEL), divided by a safety factor (typically 100) to obtain the **Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI)**, which is then translated into a maximum permitted level using consumption data.
**Regulatory bodies (1 mark):** **International** — Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC), JECFA (Joint FAO/WHO Expert Committee on Food Additives), EFSA (Europe). **Indian** — Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (**FSSAI**).

---

> **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PBFTT504]`
> **What is the Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI)?** Why is a **safety factor of 100** applied to NOAEL while computing ADI?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Definition (1 mark):** ADI is the **amount of a chemical that can be ingested daily over an entire lifetime without appreciable health risk**, expressed as **mg/kg body weight/day**.
**Safety Factor 100 (2 marks):** The factor of 100 accounts for two uncertainties: (i) a factor of **10** for **interspecies variation** (extrapolating from laboratory animals to humans), and (ii) a factor of **10** for **intraspecies (human) variability** (differences in age, sex, genetics, health, and diet among individuals). Multiplying these gives **10 × 10 = 100**, the default safety factor used by JECFA, FSSAI, and Codex.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **Question A.** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PBFTT504 Module 1]`
> **(a)** Discuss in detail the **scientific basis and toxicological principles** used for setting Maximum Permissible Limits (MPL) for food additives. Include the role of NOAEL, LOAEL, ADI, and the safety factor in your answer. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A preservative has a **NOAEL of 2000 mg/kg bw/day** in rats. The safety factor is **100**. The average body weight of the consumer is **60 kg**. The food in question is a fruit-based beverage consumed at **150 g/person/day**. **Calculate the MPL** in mg/kg of the beverage. If the industry uses it at **800 mg/kg**, calculate the **percentage of ADI consumed** and comment on its safety. **(7 marks)**

### 5.3 Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Scientific Basis and Toxicological Principles of MPL (7 Marks)

The setting of Maximum Permissible Limits is founded on the discipline of **risk assessment**, which combines toxicology, epidemiology, and dietary exposure science.

**(i) Hazard Identification (1 mark):**
The intrinsic ability of the additive to cause adverse health effects is determined through in-vitro tests, structure–activity relationships, and short-term genotoxicity assays. If the substance is a known carcinogen, mutagen, or reproductive toxicant, it is generally **prohibited outright** in foods (e.g., cyclamates, certain nitrosamines).

**(ii) Dose–Response Assessment and NOAEL/LOAEL (2 marks):**
Long-term chronic feeding studies (typically 90 days in rodents and 2 years for carcinogenicity) are conducted. The **highest dose at which no statistically significant adverse effect is observed** is designated the **NOAEL** (No Observed Adverse Effect Level). If NOAEL cannot be determined, the **LOAEL** (Lowest Observed Adverse Effect Level) is used, often with an additional safety factor of 2–10.

**(iii) ADI Calculation Using the Safety Factor (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ADI} \;=\; \frac{\text{NOAEL}}{\text{Safety Factor}} \;=\; \frac{\text{NOAEL}}{100}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **safety factor of 100** has two components — a factor of **10 for interspecies extrapolation** (rats → humans) and a factor of **10 for intraspecies variability** (individual human differences). This produces a **100-fold safety cushion** that ensures lifetime safety even for sensitive subpopulations.

**(iv) Dietary Exposure Assessment and MPL Fixation (2 marks):**
The **Estimated Daily Intake (EDI)** is calculated by multiplying the use level of the additive in each food by the daily consumption of that food, summing over all foods, and dividing by body weight. The regulator then fixes the **MPL** such that EDI ≤ ADI. A built-in safety margin (often 25–50% below the calculated theoretical maximum) is added before the MPL is published in the food standard.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**

| Variable | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| NOAEL | 2000 mg/kg bw/day |
| Safety Factor (SF) | 100 |
| Body Weight (BW) | 60 kg |
| Daily Consumption (F) | 150 g/person/day |
| Industry Use Level (C) | 800 mg/kg |

**Step 1 — Calculate ADI (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ADI} \;=\; \frac{2000}{100} \;=\; 20 \;\text{mg/kg bw/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate MPL (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MPL} \;=\; \frac{\text{ADI} \times \text{BW} \times 1000}{F} \;=\; \frac{20 \times 60 \times 1000}{150}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MPL} \;=\; \frac{1{,}200{,}000}{150} \;=\; 8000 \;\text{mg/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate EDI at 800 mg/kg (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EDI} \;=\; \frac{C \times F}{1000 \times \text{BW}} \;=\; \frac{800 \times 150}{1000 \times 60}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EDI} \;=\; \frac{120{,}000}{60{,}000} \;=\; 2.0 \;\text{mg/kg bw/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Percentage of ADI Consumed (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ADI used} \;=\; \frac{\text{EDI}}{\text{ADI}} \times 100 \;=\; \frac{2.0}{20} \times 100 \;=\; 10\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Safety Comment (1 mark):**
Since the industry is using the additive at **10% of its ADI budget**, the use level of **800 mg/kg** is **well below the calculated MPL of 8000 mg/kg** and is **highly safe** from a toxicological standpoint. There is a 10-fold buffer available if consumption patterns change.

---

> **Question B.** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PBFTT504 Module 1, Internal Choice B]`
> **(a)** Explain the **concept of NOAEL, LOAEL, and ADI** with suitable examples. Discuss the **importance of the safety factor** in toxicological risk assessment. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Compare the **MPL of Sodium Benzoate in carbonated beverages and in pickles** as per FSSAI. Why does the same additive have **different limits in different food matrices**? Illustrate with a numerical example where a lower consumption food is allowed a **higher MPL**. **(7 marks)**

### 5.4 Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — NOAEL, LOAEL, ADI and Safety Factor (7 Marks)

**Definitions with Examples (4 marks):**

| Term | Definition | Example (Aspartame) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **NOAEL** | Highest dose showing no adverse effect | NOAEL = 4000 mg/kg bw/day in rats |
| **LOAEL** | Lowest dose showing an adverse effect | LOAEL = 5000 mg/kg bw/day |
| **ADI** | Daily intake safe over a lifetime | ADI = 4000 / 100 = 40 mg/kg bw/day |

For **Aspartame**, JECFA assigned an ADI of **40 mg/kg bw/day** — among the highest in food additives — reflecting its extremely low toxicity. In contrast, the preservative **Nitrite (NaNO₂)** has an ADI of only **0.07 mg/kg bw/day** because of its conversion potential to **nitrosamines**, which are potent carcinogens.

**Importance of the Safety Factor (3 marks):**
1. **Interspecies protection (factor of 10):** Animal metabolism is faster but not identical to human metabolism. The factor of 10 ensures that human exposure remains below any possible threshold of toxicity.
2. **Intraspecies protection (factor of 10):** Protects sensitive subpopulations — infants, the elderly, pregnant women, the immunocompromised, and individuals with genetic polymorphisms in metabolism.
3. **Default value 100** can be **increased to 1000 or higher** if the data is incomplete, the additive is a suspected endocrine disruptor, or NOAEL is replaced by LOAEL (additional factor of 2–10).
4. The safety factor is the **cornerstone of the precautionary principle** in food additive regulation.

---

#### Part (b) — Comparative MPL and Food-Matrix Dependence (7 Marks)

**FSSAI MPL Comparison (2 marks):**

| Food | Sodium Benzoate MPL (FSSAI) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Carbonated Beverages | **1500 mg/kg** |
| Pickles, sauces, ketchup | **1000 mg/kg** |

**Why MPLs Differ Between Food Matrices (3 marks):**
The MPL of a given additive is **inversely proportional to the per-capita daily consumption** of the food category. Beverages are consumed in **larger volumes** than pickles, so a higher MPL reflects a **lower daily total dose**. Pickles, being a side-consumption condiment, are eaten in **smaller quantities**, justifying a tighter limit. Other factors include:
- **pH of the matrix** (benzoate is most effective at pH < 4.5; the limit reflects the acidified environment).
- **Processing conditions** (heat, salt, sugar all affect microbial challenge).
- **Cumulative exposure** (if benzoate is also present in sauces, syrups, and fruit pulp, the cumulative dietary load must remain below ADI).

**Numerical Illustration (2 marks):**
Suppose the ADI of benzoate is **5 mg/kg bw/day** and BW = **60 kg**.

- **For a beverage consumed at 200 g/day:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MPL} \;=\; \frac{5 \times 60 \times 1000}{200} \;=\; 1500 \;\text{mg/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **For a pickle consumed at 100 g/day:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MPL} \;=\; \frac{5 \times 60 \times 1000}{100} \;=\; 3000 \;\text{mg/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Theoretical illustration only** — in practice, FSSAI may set the pickle MPL lower (1000 mg/kg) for technological reasons (e.g., to avoid flavour impact), but the *toxicological ceiling* would allow 3000 mg/kg. This demonstrates the **inversely proportional relationship** between consumption and MPL.

---

### 5.5 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Where Students Lose Marks in MPL Questions (Read Carefully):**
> 
> 1. **Unit confusion (–2 marks):** Mixing mg/kg of food with mg/kg of body weight. Always convert g/day of food to kg/day by dividing by **1000**.
> 2. **Omitting the safety factor (–1 mark):** A NOAEL alone is not the ADI. You **must** divide by 100 (or the stated SF) to get ADI.
> 3. **Not stating the regulatory authority (–1 mark):** FSSAI, JECFA, and USFDA have **different numerical MPLs**. Always cite the body whose value you quote.
> 4. **Forgetting the % ADI utilization (–1 mark):** A 14-mark question almost always ends with "**comment on safety**" or "**is the use level safe?**" — compute % ADI used and state whether it is < 100%.
> 5. **Conflating MPL with MRL:** **MPL** is for **food additives**; **MRL (Maximum Residue Limit)** is for **pesticide/veterinary drug residues**. Examiners test this distinction.

---

### 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **MPL** = Maximum Permissible Limit, the statutory maximum concentration of a food additive in a defined food matrix.
- **NOAEL** → divided by **Safety Factor (100)** → gives **ADI** → divided by per-capita consumption (in kg/day) → gives **MPL**.
- The **safety factor of 100** = **10 (interspecies) × 10 (intraspecies)**. Increased to 1000 if data is incomplete.
- The **golden rule** is always: **EDI < ADI**; if EDI > ADI, the additive use must be reduced or banned.
- **MPL is inversely proportional to consumption**: a high-consumption food (beverages) gets a low MPL ceiling, while an occasional food (condiments) gets a higher one.
- **Body weight convention** in India = **60 kg** adult; globally JECFA often uses **60 kg**, EFSA uses **70 kg**, USFDA uses **70 kg** — always check the convention stated in the question.
- **Key FSSAI MPLs to memorize:** Sodium Benzoate in beverages = **1500 mg/kg**; SO₂ in fruit juices = **350 mg/kg**; Nitrite in cured meat = **200 mg/kg**; BHA/BHT in fats = **200 mg/kg**.
- **Quantum Satis (QS)** = "as much as needed" — applies to additives that are **non-toxic at any reasonable level** (e.g., curcumin, natural colours); MPL is set by GMP (Good Manufacturing Practice) only.
- **ADI unit is mg/kg bw/day** — never forget the **per kg body weight** and **per day** qualifiers.
- **Risk = Hazard × Exposure**; MPLs exist to **control exposure** even when hazard is non-zero.
- **Difference between MPL, MRL, and ML:** MPL = additives; MRL = pesticides/veterinary drugs; ML = contaminants — all use similar toxicological reasoning but apply to different chemical classes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Risk Assessment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT504 - FOOD ADDITIVES AND FLAVORINGS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Risk Assessment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Risk Assessment** is the scientifically based, systematic process used by regulatory and scientific bodies to evaluate the probability and severity of an adverse health effect resulting from human exposure to a food additive, contaminant, or any chemical substance present in food. It is a four-pillar framework recognized globally by **JECFA (Joint FAO/WHO Expert Committee on Food Additives)**, **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC)**, **EFSA (European Food Safety Authority)**, and **USFDA**.

The four pillars of Risk Assessment are:
1. **Hazard Identification** – Identifying the inherent toxicological potential of a substance.
2. **Hazard Characterization** – Qualitative and quantitative evaluation of the nature of adverse effects.
3. **Exposure Assessment** – Quantitative estimation of the magnitude, frequency, and duration of human exposure.
4. **Risk Characterization** – Integration of hazard characterization and exposure assessment to estimate the probability and severity of adverse effects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Risk Assessment is the cornerstone of regulatory toxicology. Every food additive approved for human consumption must undergo this evaluation before receiving a market authorization number (e.g., E-numbers in EU, INS numbers in India under FSSAI).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car on a busy highway.

- **Hazard Identification** = Knowing that "speed" can cause accidents (identifying the danger itself).
- **Hazard Characterization** = Studying how severe an accident could be at 100 km/h vs. 40 km/h (the dose-response relationship).
- **Exposure Assessment** = Figuring out how often and how long you actually drive at high speeds (your real-world exposure).
- **Risk Characterization** = Combining both to answer: "What is the real chance that *you specifically* will have an accident today?" (final integrated judgment).

Just like a driving instructor does not ban cars (a **hazard**) but sets speed limits (a **risk management** decision based on **risk assessment**), regulatory bodies do not ban all chemicals but establish safe exposure thresholds like the **ADI (Acceptable Daily Intake)**.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bold Constants)

- **NOAEL = No Observed Adverse Effect Level**: Highest dose at which no statistically significant adverse effect is observed.
- **LOAEL = Lowest Observed Adverse Effect Level**: Lowest dose at which an adverse effect is observed.
- **ADI (Acceptable Daily Intake)** = NOAEL ÷ Safety Factor (typically **100**).
- **TDI (Tolerable Daily Intake)**: Used for contaminants (impurities, not intentional additives).
- **RfD (Reference Dose)**: US-EPA terminology equivalent to ADI.
- **MOE (Margin of Exposure)**: Ratio of NOAEL (or BMDL) to estimated human exposure.
- **BMDL (Benchmark Dose Lower Confidence Limit)**: Modern replacement for NOAEL in threshold-of-toxicological-concern (TTC) analysis.
- **JECFA** safety evaluation standard is **0–100 scale** for additives.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Safety Factor = 100** is universally applied. It accounts for **10×** interspecies (animal-to-human) variation and **10×** intraspecies (human-to-human) variation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars — Operational Breakdown

### Pillar 1: Hazard Identification
- **What**: Identify whether the substance has the *intrinsic potential* to cause harm.
- **How**: Through in-vitro tests (Ames test for mutagenicity), in-vivo animal studies (sub-acute, sub-chronic, chronic, reproductive, teratogenicity), epidemiological data, and structure-activity relationship (SAR) analysis.
- **Output**: A "yes/no" determination of whether the substance is a hazard, plus identification of the target organ/system affected.

### Pillar 2: Hazard Characterization
- **What**: Quantify the dose-response relationship.
- **How**: Determine NOAEL or BMDL from the most sensitive species and most sensitive endpoint.
- **Output**: Establishment of the **Reference Point** (NOAEL/BMDL) and qualitative description of the toxic effect.

### Pillar 3: Exposure Assessment
- **What**: Estimate *how much* of the substance consumers actually ingest.
- **How**: Three routes: **dietary**, **occupational**, and **environmental**. For food additives: food consumption data × concentration in food.
- **Output**: Estimated daily intake (EDI) expressed in mg/kg body weight/day.

### Pillar 4: Risk Characterization
- **What**: The final integration step.
- **How**: Compare the Estimated Daily Intake (EDI) to the Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI).
- **Output**: A qualitative statement of risk (negligible, low, moderate, high) and quantitative margin of safety.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why four pillars?** Because separating hazard (intrinsic property) from risk (probabilistic outcome) prevents scientific confusion.
- **Why a Safety Factor of 100?** Because animal models are imperfect predictors (10×), and human populations show genetic, age, and health variability (10×).
- **Why EDI and not actual intake?** Because we cannot measure every person's exact consumption; we use statistical models (deterministic vs probabilistic).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Acceptable Daily Intake | $ADI = \dfrac{NOAEL}{Safety\ Factor}$ | mg/kg bw/day | Threshold for safe lifelong intake |
| Estimated Daily Intake | $EDI = \dfrac{(C \times V)}{bw}$ | mg/kg bw/day | Real consumption estimate |
| Margin of Safety | $MoS = \dfrac{NOAEL}{EDI}$ | dimensionless | If MoS > Safety Factor → Safe |
| Margin of Exposure | $MOE = \dfrac{BMDL_{10}}{Human\ Exposure}$ | dimensionless | Used for genotoxic impurities |
| Concentration in Food | $C_{final} = \dfrac{(C_{stock} \times V_{stock})}{V_{final}}$ | mg/kg or ppm | Preparing standard solutions |
| Hazard Quotient | $HQ = \dfrac{EDI}{ADI}$ | dimensionless | If HQ < 1 → Safe |
| NOAEL Derivation (if not directly observed) | $NOAEL = LOAEL \div k$ (where k ≈ 3–10) | mg/kg bw/day | Statistical interpolation |
| Body Weight Conversion | $Dose_{human} = Dose_{animal} \times \left(\dfrac{bw_{human}}{bw_{animal}}\right)^{0.67}$ | mg/kg bw/day | Allometric scaling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** If the question asks "Is the additive safe?" — your final answer MUST compare EDI with ADI and conclude with **EDI < ADI = SAFE**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering/Scientific Utility

- **FSSAI (India)**: Uses JECFA ADI values for licensing additives under the Food Safety and Standards (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011.
- **Codex Alimentarius**: Sets international standards that influence global trade (WTO/SPS agreement compliance).
- **Food Industry R&D**: Pre-market safety dossier preparation.
- **Shelf-life & Toxicology Studies**: Quantifying degradation product safety.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Calculating ADI from NOAEL

**Given:** A 90-day sub-chronic rat study on preservative "Sodium Benzoate Substitute-X" reports:
- **NOAEL** = 250 mg/kg body weight/day (based on hepatic enzyme elevation in females).
- **Safety Factor** to apply = **100** (standard default).

**To Find:** ADI for humans.

**Step 1:** Write the fundamental equation.

$$ADI = \frac{NOAEL}{SF}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the numerical values.

$$ADI = \frac{250 \text{ mg/kg bw/day}}{100}$$

**Step 3:** Perform the division.

$$ADI = 2.5 \text{ mg/kg bw/day}$$

**Step 4:** Interpret the result.

> This means a 70 kg adult can safely consume up to **175 mg/day** of the additive over an entire lifetime without appreciable health risk.
> Calculation: $70 \text{ kg} \times 2.5 \text{ mg/kg} = 175 \text{ mg/day}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Estimating Dietary Exposure (EDI)

**Given:** A 25 kg child consumes the following foods containing Additive-Y per day:
- 200 g of soft drink (concentration = 150 mg/kg)
- 50 g of confectionery (concentration = 400 mg/kg)

**To Find:** Estimated Daily Intake (EDI) in mg/kg bw/day.

**Step 1:** Calculate total mass of additive ingested per food item.

$$\text{Mass from drink} = 200 \text{ g} \times \frac{150 \text{ mg}}{1000 \text{ g}} = 30 \text{ mg}$$

$$\text{Mass from confectionery} = 50 \text{ g} \times \frac{400 \text{ mg}}{1000 \text{ g}} = 20 \text{ mg}$$

**Step 2:** Sum total additive ingested per day.

$$Total\ Intake = 30 + 20 = 50 \text{ mg/day}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to body-weight basis.

$$EDI = \frac{50 \text{ mg/day}}{25 \text{ kg}}$$

$$EDI = 2.0 \text{ mg/kg bw/day}$$

**Step 4:** Compare with ADI (assume ADI = 5 mg/kg bw/day).

$$HQ = \frac{EDI}{ADI} = \frac{2.0}{5.0} = 0.4$$

Since **HQ = 0.4 < 1**, the exposure is within safe limits.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Allometric Scaling (Animal-to-Human Dose Conversion)

**Given:** LOAEL in mouse = 1000 mg/kg bw/day. Mouse body weight = 0.025 kg. Human body weight = 70 kg.

**Step 1:** Apply allometric scaling formula.

$$Dose_{human} = Dose_{animal} \times \left(\frac{bw_{human}}{bw_{animal}}\right)^{0.67}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$Dose_{human} = 1000 \times \left(\frac{70}{0.025}\right)^{0.67}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the ratio inside the parentheses.

$$\frac{70}{0.025} = 2800$$

**Step 4:** Raise to the power 0.67.

$$(2800)^{0.67} \approx 162.4$$

**Step 5:** Multiply.

$$Dose_{human} = 1000 \times 162.4 = 162{,}400 \text{ mg/kg bw/day}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a raw interspecies dose conversion. Before use in risk assessment, you MUST still divide by the **Safety Factor (100)** to obtain a human ADI.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Scenario | EDI vs ADI | HQ Value | Risk Verdict | Regulatory Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Ideal Case | EDI << ADI | < 0.2 | Negligible | Approve without restriction |
| Acceptable | EDI < ADI | 0.2 – 1.0 | Low | Approve with use-level limits |
| Borderline | EDI ≈ ADI | 0.8 – 1.2 | Moderate | Restrict foods/use levels |
| Unsafe | EDI > ADI | > 1.0 | High | Reject/Ban |
| Genotoxic impurity | Apply MOE | MOE < 10,000 | Unacceptable | Ban (TTC approach) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Risk Assessment Framework — Block-Level Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Chemical Substance Identification] --> B[Pillar 1: Hazard Identification]
    B --> C[Pillar 2: Hazard Characterization]
    C --> D{Pick Reference Point}
    D -->|NOAEL found| E[Use NOAEL]
    D -->|BMDL available| F[Use BMDL]
    D -->|Neither| G[Use LOAEL with extra safety factor]
    E --> H[Apply Safety Factor 100]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Derive ADI mg per kg bw per day]
    J[Pillar 3: Exposure Assessment] --> K[Food Consumption Data]
    K --> L[Concentration in Food]
    L --> M[Calculate EDI mg per kg bw per day]
    I --> N[Pillar 4: Risk Characterization]
    M --> N
    N --> O{Compare EDI vs ADI}
    O -->|EDI less than ADI| P[Safe: Approve]
    O -->|EDI greater than ADI| Q[Unsafe: Reject or Restrict]
    P --> R[Risk Management Decision]
    Q --> R
    R --> S[Communicate to Public]
```

## 4.2 JECFA Evaluation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IntakePhase[Data Gathering]
        A1[Toxicological Studies]
        A2[Use Level Data]
        A3[Consumption Surveys]
    end
    subgraph AnalysisPhase[Scientific Evaluation]
        B1[Determine NOAEL or BMDL]
        B2[Calculate ADI]
        B3[Estimate EDI]
    end
    subgraph DecisionPhase[Outcome]
        C1{Margin of Safety adequate}
        C2[Establish or Maintain ADI]
        C3[Revise or Withdraw ADI]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B3
    A3 --> B3
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    C1 -->|Yes| C2
    C1 -->|No| C3
```

## 4.3 Dose-Response Curve Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low Dose: No Effect Region] -->|Dose increases| B[NOAEL Threshold]
    B -->|Dose increases| C[LOAEL First Adverse Effect]
    C -->|Dose increases| D[Frank Toxicity Region]
    D -->|Dose increases| E[Lethal Dose 50 LD50]
```

## 4.4 Relationship Between Risk Assessment and Risk Management

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Risk Assessment: Scientific Evaluation] --> B[Risk Profile Document]
    B --> C[Risk Managers: Policy Makers]
    C --> D[Risk Management Options]
    D --> E1[Set Maximum Use Levels]
    D --> E2[Labeling Requirements]
    D --> E3[Banning or Withdrawal]
    E1 --> F[Risk Communication to Public]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Public Health Protection]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Risk Assessment. List its four components.
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**Model Answer:**
Risk Assessment is the scientifically based process of evaluating the probability and severity of adverse health effects from human exposure to a food additive or contaminant.
**Four Components:**
1. Hazard Identification
2. Hazard Characterization
3. Exposure Assessment
4. Risk Characterization

---

### Question 2: What is ADI? How is it calculated?
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**Model Answer:**
**ADI (Acceptable Daily Intake)** is the amount of a food additive, expressed in mg/kg body weight/day, that can be ingested daily over a lifetime without appreciable health risk.

$$ADI = \frac{NOAEL}{Safety\ Factor}$$

The standard safety factor is **100** (10× for interspecies variation and 10× for intraspecies variation).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: Risk Assessment Framework in Detail

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the four pillars of Risk Assessment as adopted by JECFA. Explain the role of NOAEL in the process.

**Model Solution:**

The Joint FAO/WHO Expert Committee on Food Additives (JECFA) follows a four-pillar risk assessment paradigm:

**Pillar 1: Hazard Identification** — Determines the intrinsic potential of a substance to cause adverse health effects. Conducted using in-vitro mutagenicity tests, in-vivo animal toxicology studies (acute, sub-chronic, chronic, carcinogenicity, reproductive, developmental), and epidemiological evidence.

**Pillar 2: Hazard Characterization** — Qualitative and quantitative evaluation of the nature of the adverse effect. Establishes the dose-response relationship and identifies the most sensitive endpoint and species. The pivotal output is the **NOAEL** (No Observed Adverse Effect Level).

**Role of NOAEL:** NOAEL is the highest dose at which no statistically or biologically significant increase in adverse effects is observed compared to controls. It serves as the **point of departure** for deriving the human Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI).

**Pillar 3: Exposure Assessment** — Quantifies the magnitude, frequency, and duration of human intake. For food additives, calculated as:

$$EDI = \frac{\Sigma (C_i \times V_i)}{bw}$$

where $C_i$ is concentration in food $i$, $V_i$ is daily consumption of food $i$, and $bw$ is body weight.

**Pillar 4: Risk Characterization** — Integrates outputs from Pillars 2 and 3 to yield a qualitative and quantitative estimate of risk. The Hazard Quotient (HQ = EDI ÷ ADI) is computed; values < 1 indicate acceptable risk.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 60 kg adult consumes 500 g/day of a beverage containing 80 mg/L of artificial sweetener Substitute-Z. The NOAEL from a 2-year rat study is 400 mg/kg bw/day. Calculate the ADI and determine whether the consumption is safe.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate ADI.

$$ADI = \frac{NOAEL}{SF} = \frac{400}{100} = 4.0 \text{ mg/kg bw/day}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the mass of sweetener consumed per day.

$$\text{Volume consumed} = 500 \text{ g} \approx 500 \text{ mL} = 0.5 \text{ L}$$

$$\text{Total dose} = 0.5 \text{ L} \times 80 \text{ mg/L} = 40 \text{ mg/day}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate EDI.

$$EDI = \frac{40 \text{ mg/day}}{60 \text{ kg}} = 0.667 \text{ mg/kg bw/day}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate Hazard Quotient.

$$HQ = \frac{EDI}{ADI} = \frac{0.667}{4.0} = 0.167$$

**Step 5:** Conclusion.

Since **HQ = 0.167 < 1**, the daily consumption is **safe** with a substantial margin of safety (about 6× below the ADI).

---

### Question B: NOAEL, LOAEL, and Safety Factors

**(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between NOAEL and LOAEL. Explain why a safety factor of 100 is used in ADI derivation. What additional factors are applied when using LOAEL instead of NOAEL?

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | NOAEL | LOAEL |
|---|---|---|
| Full Form | No Observed Adverse Effect Level | Lowest Observed Adverse Effect Level |
| Definition | Highest dose with no adverse effect | Lowest dose showing an adverse effect |
| Position on Dose-Response Curve | Below threshold of toxicity | At or just above toxicity threshold |
| Statistical Confidence | Higher | Lower |
| Use in ADI | Direct divisor | Requires extra uncertainty factor |

**Justification for Safety Factor = 100:**
The factor 100 accounts for two 10-fold uncertainties:
- **10× Interspecies variation**: Animal-to-human extrapolation (differences in metabolism, absorption, physiology).
- **10× Intraspecies variation**: Human-to-human variability (age, sex, genetics, disease state, pregnancy, nutrition).

**Additional factors for LOAEL use:**
When only LOAEL is available, additional uncertainty factors of 3–10 are multiplied with the base factor of 100, yielding safety factors of **300 to 1000**. Modern toxicology prefers **BMDL (Benchmark Dose Lower Confidence Limit)** over NOAEL/LOAEL for higher statistical robustness.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The LOAEL for a new flavoring agent Flavor-X in mice is 500 mg/kg bw/day. The estimated human exposure is 0.05 mg/kg bw/day. Using an appropriate safety factor, determine whether the flavoring agent is safe.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Determine the safety factor.
Since only LOAEL is available (not NOAEL), we apply an additional uncertainty factor of 3 (since LOAEL is close to NOAEL on the dose-response curve; if effect is severe, use 10).

$$SF_{total} = 100 \times 3 = 300$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the pseudo-ADI (since we use LOAEL).

$$ADI_{derived} = \frac{LOAEL}{SF_{total}} = \frac{500}{300} = 1.67 \text{ mg/kg bw/day}$$

**Step 3:** Compare with human exposure.

$$EDI = 0.05 \text{ mg/kg bw/day}$$

$$HQ = \frac{EDI}{ADI} = \frac{0.05}{1.67} = 0.030$$

**Step 4:** Conclusion.

Since HQ = 0.03 << 1, **Flavor-X is SAFE** with a very large margin of safety (about 33× below the derived acceptable level).

**Alternative interpretation using MOE:**

$$MOE = \frac{LOAEL}{EDI} = \frac{500}{0.05} = 10{,}000$$

For a non-genotoxic substance, an MOE of ≥ 10,000 is considered of low concern — confirming safety.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Unit Mistake**: Forgetting to convert **mL to L** or **g to kg** when calculating EDI. (Most common deduction: 1–2 marks).
> 2. **Confusing ADI with LOAEL/NOAEL**: ADI is for humans; NOAEL/LOAEL are animal study outputs. Students often mistakenly use NOAEL directly as ADI.
> 3. **Incorrect Safety Factor**: Always 100 by default unless LOAEL is used (then 300–1000). For carcinogens/genotoxins, **NO safe threshold** — use MOE approach.
> 4. **Forgetting body weight**: EDI must always be expressed in mg/kg bw/day.
> 5. **JECFA vs FSSAI vs EFSA**: Cite the correct body; JECFA is international, FSSAI is India, EFSA is EU.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Risk Assessment = Science; Risk Management = Policy.** Never confuse them.
- The **four pillars** are fixed by international convention — memorize them in order.
- **Safety Factor 100** is the bedrock — derived from $10 \times 10$.
- **HQ = EDI ÷ ADI**; safe when HQ < 1.
- **MOE approach** is used for substances with no safe threshold (e.g., genotoxic carcinogens).
- **NOAEL** is preferred over LOAEL; **BMDL** is the modern gold standard.
- Always express doses in **mg/kg bw/day** — this is non-negotiable in KTU answers.
- **JECFA**, **Codex**, **FSSAI**, **EFSA**, and **USFDA** are the bodies to remember for regulatory frameworks.
- **TTC (Threshold of Toxicological Concern)** is used for trace impurities when data is scarce.
- For flavorings specifically, JECFA uses the **"GRAS" (Generally Recognized As Safe)** concept plus structure-activity analysis (e.g., Cramer classes I, II, III).
- Always perform **allometric scaling** when extrapolating animal doses to humans.
- Risk communication is the *final* step — not part of assessment but essential to public health.
- For KTU numericals: show **all steps**, label units clearly, and **box your final answer**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Toxicity Evaluation of Food Additive
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT504 - FOOD ADDITIVES AND FLAVORINGS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Toxicity Evaluation of Food Additive

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Toxicity Evaluation of Food Additive** is the systematic, scientific, and regulatory process of assessing the potential harmful effects of a food additive on biological systems, using standardized toxicological tests, dose-response analysis, and risk assessment protocols to determine the **Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI)** and ensure consumer safety.

In the context of the KTU 2024 scheme (PBFTT504), toxicity evaluation encompasses the **entire chain of evidence generation** — from in vitro biochemical assays, through in vivo animal studies, to epidemiological surveillance — that supports the safety clearance of an additive before it enters the human food chain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 1):** Students must clearly distinguish between *hazard* (inherent property of a substance to cause harm) and *risk* (probability of harm occurring under conditions of exposure). Most exam questions test this distinction.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **new spice** discovered in a remote forest. A tribe has eaten it for centuries with no apparent ill effect. But before a food company can put it in your soup, scientists must prove — to a regulatory body — that it will **not harm you** even if you eat it every day for 70 years.

- **Hazard** = "This spice, in *concentrated* form, can irritate the stomach lining." (It *can* cause harm.)
- **Risk** = "But the amount a person realistically consumes in soup is so small that the *chance* of stomach irritation is virtually zero."

**Toxicity evaluation is the engineered bridge** between these two ideas. It uses **mathematical dose-response relationships**, **animal surrogates**, and **safety multipliers** to translate "can it hurt?" into "is it safe at the dose we will actually eat?"

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Bold-Defined for Quick Recall)

- **Additive**: Any substance (other than the basic foodstuff) intentionally added to food to modify its color, flavor, texture, shelf-life, or nutritional value.
- **Toxin**: A naturally occurring poisonous substance (e.g., aflatoxin from *Aspergillus* mold).
- **Toxicant**: A synthetic or anthropogenic poisonous substance (e.g., pesticide residues).
- **Xenobiotic**: A chemical foreign to a living organism (most food additives qualify).
- **NOAEL**: **No Observed Adverse Effect Level** — the highest dose at which no statistically or biologically significant adverse effect is observed.
- **LOAEL**: **Lowest Observed Adverse Effect Level** — the lowest dose at which an adverse effect is first seen.
- **ADI**: **Acceptable Daily Intake** — the daily intake of a chemical which, during an entire lifetime, appears to be without appreciable risk to the consumer (mg/kg body weight/day).
- **LD₅₀**: **Lethal Dose 50** — the single dose of a substance that kills 50% of the test animal population (typically expressed in mg/kg body weight).
- **TD₅₀**: **Toxic Dose 50** — the dose that produces a toxic effect (other than death) in 50% of the test population.
- **Margin of Safety (MoS)**: Ratio of NOAEL in the most sensitive animal species to the estimated human exposure.
- **GRAS**: **Generally Recognized As Safe** — a US FDA designation for substances with a long, safe history of use.
- **JECFA**: **Joint FAO/WHO Expert Committee on Food Additives** — the international body that evaluates additive safety.
- **FSSAI**: **Food Safety and Standards Authority of India** — India's national regulatory body.
- **BMD**: **Benchmark Dose** — a modern replacement for NOAEL that uses mathematical modeling of the entire dose-response curve.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hazard vs. Risk** is the single most-tested concept in Module 1 exam questions. Memorize: *Hazard = intrinsic property; Risk = hazard × exposure.*

## 1.4 Why Toxicity Evaluation Matters in Food Technology

A B.Tech Food Technology graduate may work in:
- **R&D** of a new additive (e.g., a novel natural preservative)
- **Quality Assurance** in a food processing plant
- **Regulatory affairs** in FSSAI or a private certification lab
- **Toxicology contract research** (CRO)

In each role, the professional must understand how safety dossiers are built, what LD₅₀, NOAEL, and ADI mean, and how to read a Safety Data Sheet (SDS) for a new ingredient.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Used Globally:** Toxicity is measured in **mg of test substance per kg of body weight of the test animal (mg/kg b.w.)**. The "**per kg b.w.**" qualifier is non-negotiable in any KTU answer.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Toxicity Evaluation

The KTU 2024 syllabus structures toxicity evaluation into **four sequential pillars**. Mastering this sequence guarantees marks in any "explain the safety assessment of a food additive" question.

### Pillar 1: Hazard Identification
- "What *can* this substance do to a living system?"
- Uses **structural-activity relationships (SAR)**, **in vitro** tests, and **in vivo** animal studies.
- Identifies the **target organ(s)** — e.g., liver, kidney, nervous system, reproductive system.

### Pillar 2: Dose-Response Assessment
- "At what *dose* does the harm begin, and how does it scale?"
- Establishes the **dose-response curve** — a foundational concept in toxicology.
- Identifies **NOAEL** and **LOAEL** from the curve.

### Pillar 3: Exposure Assessment
- "How much of this substance will humans *actually* eat or drink?"
- Combines food consumption data with additive concentration data.
- Produces an **Estimated Daily Intake (EDI)** in mg/kg b.w./day.

### Pillar 4: Risk Characterization
- "Putting it all together — is the exposure safely below the toxic threshold?"
- Computes the **Margin of Safety (MoS)** and compares EDI with ADI.
- Output: an **ADI value** in mg/kg b.w./day.

## 2.2 Types of Toxicity Studies (Hierarchical Pyramid)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU Module 1 syllabus lists these as "**types of toxicity tests**". Memorize the *duration*, *purpose*, and *typical animal model* for each tier.

| Tier | Test Type | Duration | Primary Purpose | Typical Animal Model |
|------|-----------|----------|-----------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Acute Toxicity Test** | Single dose; observation 14 days | Determine **LD₅₀**, identify target organs | Rats, mice (5–10 per group) |
| 2 | **Sub-acute (28-day) Test** | Repeated dosing for 28 days | Identify NOAEL, target organ toxicity, dose-response | Rats (5 per sex per group) |
| 3 | **Sub-chronic (90-day) Test** | Repeated dosing for 90 days (≈10% of lifespan) | Establish NOAEL for non-cancer endpoints | Rats, dogs, mice (10–20 per group) |
| 4 | **Chronic Toxicity Test** | 6 months to 2+ years (entire lifespan) | Detect carcinogenicity, chronic organ damage | Rats, mice (50+ per group) |
| 5 | **Reproductive & Developmental Toxicity** | Multigeneration | Detect teratogenicity, fertility effects | Rats, rabbits |
| 6 | **Genotoxicity / Mutagenicity Tests** | In vitro + in vivo | Detect DNA damage, mutations, chromosomal aberrations | Salmonella (Ames test), mice |
| 7 | **Carcinogenicity Bioassay** | 2 years (rodent lifespan) | Detect tumor induction | Rats, mice |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All four formulas below are **examinable**. In Part B 14-mark questions, you may be asked to **derive or apply** the ADI formula or interpret LD₅₀ data.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units | When to Use |
|---|-------------------|-------------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | **Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI)** | $\text{ADI} = \dfrac{\text{NOAEL}}{\text{SF}}$ | mg/kg b.w./day | Final risk characterization |
| 2 | **Safety Factor (SF)** | Typically **100** (default), can be 10–1000 | Dimensionless | Accounts for interspecies (×10) and intraspecies (×10) variability |
| 3 | **Margin of Safety (MoS)** | $\text{MoS} = \dfrac{\text{NOAEL (animal)}}{\text{EDI (human)}}$ | Dimensionless | Direct risk comparison |
| 4 | **Lethal Dose 50 (LD₅₀)** | Determined graphically from probit/log-dose plot | mg/kg b.w. | Acute toxicity ranking |
| 5 | **Dose conversion (animal → human)** | $\text{HED} = \text{Animal dose} \times \dfrac{\text{Animal }K_m}{\text{Human }K_m}$ | mg/kg | Allometric scaling |
| 6 | **Estimated Daily Intake (EDI)** | $\text{EDI} = \sum_i \left( C_i \times F_i \right) \div \text{b.w.}$ | mg/kg b.w./day | Exposure assessment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL MARKDOWN RULE (Engine V10):** In the table above, vertical bar symbols (such as those appearing inside `b.w.`) have been **deliberately written with a period**, **NOT** a vertical pipe `|`. This prevents markdown table syntax from breaking.

Where:
- $C_i$ = concentration of additive in food $i$ (mg/kg or mg/L)
- $F_i$ = amount of food $i$ consumed per day (kg/day or L/day)
- $K_m$ = allometric scaling factor (mouse = 3, rat = 6, rabbit = 12, human adult = 37, human child = 25)

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind the Safety Factor of 100

A frequently asked KTU question is: *"Why is the default safety factor 100?"* The answer is built from **two ×10 factors**:

- **Factor of 10 for interspecies variation**: Humans are not 70-kg rats. Differences in metabolism, enzyme activity, absorption, and excretion mean a NOAEL in a rat may not directly apply to humans.
- **Factor of 10 for intraspecies variation**: Even within the human population, sensitivity varies — infants, the elderly, pregnant women, the genetically susceptible, and the diseased may respond differently.

**Optional additional factors (×10 each)** may be added when:
- The data is from sub-chronic (not chronic) studies → **+10**
- The data is from animal studies with no human data → **+10**
- The endpoint is severe (e.g., non-genotoxic carcinogen) → **+10**
- The dose-response is steep → **+10**

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern refinement (BMD approach):** The KTU 2024 syllabus references newer methods where, instead of a flat safety factor of 100, **chemical-specific adjustment factors (CSAF)** derived from toxicokinetic and toxicodynamic data are used. This is a Board-Examiner favorite for 14-mark questions.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Industry:** Before launching a new artificial sweetener, a company files a **GRAS dossier** with the US FDA — the dossier is *built on* the four pillars above.
- **Regulation:** FSSAI in India accepts additives only after **JECFA** (international) evaluation, and the safety factor of 100 is applied to the JECFA NOAEL to set the Indian ADI.
- **Research:** Modern alternatives to animal testing (in silico QSAR, organ-on-chip, omics-based toxicology) are now integrated into the hazard identification step.

## 2.6 The "Threshold of Toxicological Concern (TTC)"

For additives present in very small amounts (e.g., trace contaminants), full toxicology testing is impractical. The **TTC concept** (developed by Kroes et al., 2004) provides a pragmatic alternative:

- If exposure < **1.5 µg/day** → likely safe for all compounds except potent carcinogens.
- If exposure < **0.15 µg/day** → also safe for potent carcinogens.

> This is a high-yield KTU point for "short answer" questions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation: Calculating ADI from Raw Toxicity Data

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A food additive was tested in a 90-day sub-chronic rat study. The NOAEL was determined to be **250 mg/kg b.w./day**. Calculate the **ADI** using the standard safety factor approach.

### Step 1: Identify the safety factor.

For a 90-day sub-chronic study (not a full chronic 2-year study), the default safety factor is **100**, but a **×10 additional factor** may be applied for sub-chronic duration, making it **1000**. For our base case, we use SF = 100.

### Step 2: Apply the ADI formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ADI} &= \dfrac{\text{NOAEL}}{\text{SF}} \\[6pt]
\text{ADI} &= \dfrac{250 \text{ mg/kg b.w./day}}{100} \\[6pt]
\text{ADI} &= 2.5 \text{ mg/kg b.w./day}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Convert to absolute daily intake for an average adult (60 kg).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Absolute ADI} &= \text{ADI} \times \text{body weight} \\[6pt]
&= 2.5 \text{ mg/kg b.w./day} \times 60 \text{ kg} \\[6pt]
&= 150 \text{ mg/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Convert to total over a lifetime (70 years) — for context only.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lifetime total} &= 150 \text{ mg/day} \times 365 \text{ days/year} \times 70 \text{ years} \\[6pt]
&= 3{,}832{,}500 \text{ mg} \approx 3.83 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This is the *maximum* amount a 60-kg adult could consume every day for 70 years with no appreciable health risk.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Sample Problem-Solving Workflow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below codifies the **decision logic** a Food Technologist uses when interpreting a toxicity dossier. This is exactly how the KTU Board Examiner frames 14-mark Part B questions.

| Step | Question Asked | Data Source | Output |
|------|----------------|-------------|--------|
| 1 | What is the acute hazard? | LD₅₀ from acute test | Toxicity category (e.g., "slightly toxic") |
| 2 | What is the chronic NOAEL? | 90-day or 2-year study | NOAEL in mg/kg b.w./day |
| 3 | What is the human exposure? | Food consumption surveys | EDI in mg/kg b.w./day |
| 4 | Is EDI < ADI? | Comparison | Safe / Not safe |
| 5 | What is the MoS? | MoS = NOAEL / EDI | Acceptable if MoS ≥ 100 |
| 6 | Final regulatory decision | All of the above | Approved, restricted, or banned |

## 3.3 Worked Example: Risk Assessment for a Hypothetical Sweetener

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world parallel:** This mirrors the actual safety dossier for aspartame, sucralose, or steviol glycosides.

### Given:
- LD₅₀ in rats = 5000 mg/kg b.w. (practically non-toxic)
- 90-day rat NOAEL = 1000 mg/kg b.w./day
- Average human consumption: 2 L of diet soda per day
- Sweetener concentration in soda: 200 mg/L
- Average adult body weight: 60 kg

### Step 1: Compute human EDI.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Daily intake} &= \text{Concentration} \times \text{Consumption} \\[6pt]
&= 200 \text{ mg/L} \times 2 \text{ L/day} \\[6pt]
&= 400 \text{ mg/day} \\[6pt]
\text{EDI} &= \dfrac{400 \text{ mg/day}}{60 \text{ kg}} \\[6pt]
&= 6.67 \text{ mg/kg b.w./day}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Compute ADI (using default SF = 100).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ADI} &= \dfrac{1000 \text{ mg/kg b.w./day}}{100} \\[6pt]
&= 10 \text{ mg/kg b.w./day}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Compare.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EDI (6.67)} &< \text{ADI (10)} \\[6pt]
\text{Margin of Safety} &= \dfrac{1000}{6.67} \approx 150
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Conclusion.

Since EDI < ADI and MoS ≈ 150 (>100), the sweetener is **safe** at the stated consumption level. ✓

## 3.4 Classification of Chemicals by LD₅₀ (Oral, Rats) — Hodge & Sterner Scale

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus lists this classification table as "**expected to be memorized**". It frequently appears in Part A 3-mark questions.

| Toxicity Class | LD₅₀ (mg/kg b.w., oral rat) | Example |
|----------------|------------------------------|---------|
| Extremely toxic | < 1 | Botulinum toxin, dioxins |
| Highly toxic | 1 – 50 | Nicotine, aflatoxin B1 |
| Moderately toxic | 50 – 500 | Sodium fluoride, methanol |
| Slightly toxic | 500 – 5000 | Sodium chloride (table salt ≈ 3000) |
| Practically non-toxic | 5000 – 15000 | Aspartame, sucralose |
| Relatively harmless | > 15000 | Water (technically not applicable) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight for KTU exam:** Even "safe" substances like common salt and water have an LD₅₀. The dose *makes the poison* (Paracelsus, c. 1538). Salt is a textbook example used by examiners to challenge the "natural = safe" fallacy.

## 3.5 Mathematical Foundation: Probit Analysis for LD₅₀

The LD₅₀ is determined by:
1. Dosing multiple groups of animals (typically 5–10 per group) with logarithmically spaced doses.
2. Recording the **mortality** (number dead / number in group) at each dose.
3. Converting percent mortality to **probit values** (probability units).
4. Plotting probit (y-axis) vs. log dose (x-axis).
5. Drawing a best-fit straight line; the **dose at probit = 5** (= 50% mortality) is the **LD₅₀**.

> This is rarely computed by hand in exams, but you may be asked to **describe the method** (3-mark question) or **interpret a published LD₅₀ table** (14-mark question).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Four-Step Risk Assessment Workflow (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hazard Identification] --> B[Dose-Response Assessment]
    B --> C[Exposure Assessment]
    C --> D[Risk Characterization]
    D --> E{Is EDI less than ADI?}
    E -->|Yes| F[APPROVED with ADI value]
    E -->|No| G[RESTRICTED or BANNED]
    F --> H[Post-market Surveillance]
    G --> I[Reformulation or Withdrawal]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style B fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style C fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style D fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style E fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style F fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style G fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
    style H fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a
    style I fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a
```

## 4.2 Toxicity Testing Pyramid (Hierarchical Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Acute Toxicity Test - Single dose, 14 days]
    L2[Sub-acute 28-day Test]
    L3[Sub-chronic 90-day Test]
    L4[Chronic 2-year Test]
    L5[Genotoxicity Tests Ames Micronucleus Comet]
    L6[Reproductive and Developmental Tests]
    L7[Carcinogenicity Bioassay 2-year rodent]

    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L7
    L3 --> L5
    L4 --> L6

    style L1 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style L2 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style L3 fill:#ffecb3,stroke:#f57f17
    style L4 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style L5 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0
    style L6 fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#ad1457
    style L7 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each tier builds on the previous one. Acute → Sub-acute → Sub-chronic → Chronic. Genotoxicity, reproductive, and carcinogenicity studies are *run in parallel* to the main tier tests.

## 4.3 Dose-Response Curve and Key Reference Points

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[NOAEL - No Observed Adverse Effect Level] -->|Add 1 log dose| B[LOAEL - Lowest Observed Adverse Effect Level]
    B -->|Increase dose| C[TD50 - Toxic Dose 50 percent]
    C -->|Increase dose| D[LD50 - Lethal Dose 50 percent]
    D -->|Increase dose| E[100 percent mortality]

    style A fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```
