# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Define acidulants. List any four commonly used food acidulants with one example application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Acidulants are food-grade acids (organic or inorganic) added to foods primarily to impart sour taste, control pH, and act as preservatives.

**Four commonly used acidulants:**

| Acidulant | Typical Application |
|---|---|
| Citric acid | Soft drinks, jams, confectionery |
| Acetic acid (vinegar) | Pickles, sauces, ketchup |
| Phosphoric acid | Cola beverages |
| Lactic acid | Yogurt, cheese, sourdough bread |

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Explain the role of acidulants as chelating agents in food systems.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chelation** is the process by which a molecule binds a metal ion through two or more coordinate bonds, forming a stable ring structure.

Acidulants such as **citric acid** contain multiple carboxylate (–COO⁻) and hydroxyl (–OH) groups that can bind pro-oxidant metal ions like **Fe²⁺, Fe³⁺, and Cu²⁺**. By sequestering these metals, acidulants:

1. **Prevent Fenton-type reactions** that generate free radicals.
2. **Inhibit lipid oxidation** in fats, oils, and emulsions.
3. **Work synergistically** with primary antioxidants (BHA, BHT, tocopherols).
4. **Stabilize color** in meat products by preventing metmyoglobin formation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model — Module 2 Choice 1]`

**(a) Classify food acidulants in detail and explain any five functions of acidulants in food products.** (7 Marks)

**(b) Discuss the source, chemical properties, and food applications of citric acid and phosphoric acid.** (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Classification of Acidulants:**

**1. Organic Acids:**
- **Monocarboxylic:** Acetic acid, Propionic acid, Sorbic acid (also a preservative)
- **Dicarboxylic:** Adipic, Succinic, Fumaric, Tartaric, Malic, Oxalic
- **Tricarboxylic:** Citric acid
- **Hydroxy acids:** Lactic, Malic, Tartaric, Gluconic (from GDL)

**2. Inorganic Acids:**
- Phosphoric acid (H₃PO₄)

**3. Acid Salts:**
- Sodium citrate, Sodium acetate, Potassium tartrate, Calcium lactate

**4. Lactones:**
- Glucono-δ-lactone (GDL)

**Five Functions:**

1. **Sour taste enhancement:** Provides tartness that balances sweet, salty, and bitter notes.
2. **pH control:** Maintains the food at a desired acidity for stability and safety.
3. **Preservation:** Inhibits microbial growth by lowering pH below 4.5.
4. **Chelation:** Binds metal ions, preventing oxidation and color changes.
5. **Leavening:** Reacts with baking soda to release CO₂ for baked product volume.

*(Additional functions like buffering and gel formation may be added for full marks.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Citric Acid (C₆H₈O₇):**

- **Source:** Citrus fruits (lemon, lime, orange) — historically isolated from Italian lemons.
- **Production:** Industrial fermentation by *Aspergillus niger* using sucrose or molasses.
- **Properties:**
  - White crystalline solid, soluble in water (59 g/100 mL at 20 °C)
  - pKa1 = 3.13, pKa2 = 4.76, pKa3 = 6.40 (triprotic)
  - Three carboxyl groups + one hydroxyl group → strong chelator
  - GRAS status; E330 (European numbering)
- **Food applications:** Soft drinks (0.1–0.3 %), jams (0.1–0.5 %), confectionery (sour candies), frozen desserts, cheese, wine.

**Phosphoric Acid (H₃PO₄):**

- **Source:** Mineral acid — produced from phosphate rock via the wet process.
- **Properties:**
  - Clear, colorless, syrupy liquid
  - pKa1 = 2.15, pKa2 = 7.20, pKa3 = 12.35
  - Stronger than citric acid (lower pKa1) → sharper sourness
  - E338; GRAS at permitted levels
- **Food applications:**
  - Cola beverages (0.05–0.1 %) — provides characteristic sharp taste
  - Cheeses (as melting salt when partially neutralized)
  - Brewing (adjusts mash pH)
  - Sugar refining

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #1:**
> Students commonly lose marks by:
> 1. **Confusing the three pKa values of citric acid** — memorize pKa1 = 3.13, pKa2 = 4.76, pKa3 = 6.40.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention food-grade vs. technical-grade** distinction.
> 3. **Not stating the E-number** when discussing European/Codex regulations.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model — Module 2 Choice 2]`

**(a) Explain the mechanism of action of acidulants as food preservatives, with three suitable examples.** (7 Marks)

**(b) Compare acetic acid, lactic acid, and tartaric acid with respect to source, properties, and food applications.** (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Mechanism of Action as Preservatives:**

1. **pH Reduction:**
   - Most food-borne **bacteria** cannot grow below **pH 4.5**.
   - **Molds and yeasts** are more acid-tolerant but are still inhibited at low pH.
   - Dissociated H⁺ ions disrupt the cytoplasmic membrane potential of microbial cells.

2. **Enzyme Denaturation:**
   - Low pH denatures essential microbial enzymes, halting metabolism.
   - This is **irreversible** above a critical threshold.

3. **Interference with Nutrient Transport:**
   - Protonated weak acids cross the microbial membrane and **acidify the cytoplasm**, collapsing the proton motive force (PMF).

**Three Examples:**

| Acidulant | Example Food | pH Achieved | Target Microbe |
|---|---|---|---|
| Acetic acid (vinegar) | Pickles, chutney | 2.4–3.4 | *Clostridium botulinum* |
| Citric acid | Fruit juices | 2.5–3.5 | *Salmonella*, *E. coli* |
| Lactic acid | Yogurt, cheese | 4.0–4.6 | *Listeria*, spoilage bacteria |

**Synergy with other preservatives:**
- Sodium benzoate is **400× more effective at pH 3.0** than at pH 6.0 because the undissociated form (benzoic acid) is membrane-permeable.
- Hence acidulants are often paired with weak-acid preservatives.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Comparative Table:**

| Property | Acetic Acid (CH₃COOH) | Lactic Acid (CH₃CHOHCOOH) | Tartaric Acid (C₄H₆O₆) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Molecular formula | C₂H₄O₂ | C₃H₆O₃ | C₄H₆O₆ |
| pKa | 4.76 | 3.86 | pKa1 = 3.04, pKa2 = 4.37 |
| Source | Fermentation of ethanol by *Acetobacter* | Fermentation of lactose by *Lactobacillus* | Byproduct of wine industry (argol/cream of tartar) |
| Physical form | Clear liquid with pungent odor | Syrupy liquid or powder | White crystalline solid |
| Taste | Sharp, pungent | Smooth, mild | Strongly tart |
| Chelation | Weak | Moderate | Good (two carboxyl + two hydroxyl groups) |
| Food applications | Vinegar, pickles, ketchup, sauces | Yogurt, cheese, sourdough, cured meats | Baking powder (cream of tartar), wine, candies, fruit jellies |
| E-number | E260 | E270 | E334 |
| Typical concentration | 0.5–5 % | 0.5–2 % | 0.1–0.5 % |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #2:**
> Students commonly lose marks by:
> 1. **Writing only one or two comparative points** instead of the full table.
> 2. **Mixing up sources** — tartaric acid comes from grapes, not apples (that is malic acid).
> 3. **Forgetting the chirality note** — lactic acid exists as L-(+) and D-(−) isomers; only L-lactic acid is GRAS for food.
> 4. **Not mentioning E-numbers** — KTU examiners value international regulatory awareness.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Acidulants**

- ✅ Acidulants are **food acids** that impart sourness, control pH, preserve, chelate, leaven, buffer, and gel.
- ✅ **Citric acid** is the **most widely used** acidulant globally (E330).
- ✅ **Phosphoric acid** is the acidulant of choice in **cola beverages**.
- ✅ **Acetic acid** (vinegar) is the **oldest known** food acidulant, used since antiquity.
- ✅ **Lactic acid** is naturally present in **fermented dairy** products.
- ✅ **Malic acid** gives a smooth, lingering sourness — prominent in **apples**.
- ✅ **Tartaric acid** is the principal acid in **grapes and wine**; cream of tartar is its potassium salt.
- ✅ **Fumaric acid** is used in **dry mixes** because of low hygroscopicity.
- ✅ **GDL** hydrolyzes slowly to gluconic acid — used in **tofu coagulation** and **clean-label baking**.
- ✅ **pKa** dictates acid strength — lower pKa = stronger acid = sharper sourness.
- ✅ **Henderson–Hasselbalch equation** is essential for buffer pH calculations.
- ✅ Most acidulants are **GRAS** but must be used within **permitted limits** (FSSAI / Codex / FDA).
- ✅ Acidulants **enhance preservative action** of weak-acid antimicrobials like benzoates and sorbates.
- ✅ Acidulants **lower water activity (aw)** only slightly; they are not the same as humectants.
- ✅ **Chelation by citric acid** is one of the most important antioxidant mechanisms in lipid-containing foods.
- ✅ Always remember: **three carboxylates + one hydroxyl = citric acid**, the chelator champion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Colouring Agents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT504 - FOOD ADDITIVES AND FLAVORINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Colouring Agents

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **food colouring agent** (also called a *food colorant* or *food dye*) is a substance, either natural or synthetic, that is capable of imparting, restoring, or enhancing the colour of a food product when added to it. According to the **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC)** and the **Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI)**, these agents are classified under the broader category of food additives and are permitted only when they do not pose a hazard to consumer health at the levels of intended use.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Colouring agents are studied as a distinct sub-class of food additives with special emphasis on their **chemical nature, permitted levels, regulatory status, and functional applications** in processed foods.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a painter standing in front of a blank white canvas. The canvas represents uncoloured, raw food (like clear gelatin, plain pasta, or colourless beverages). The **paints** that the artist dips into are the **colouring agents** — they do not change the *structure* of the food, but they radically alter its *visual perception*. Just as some paints come from natural sources (crushed berries, minerals) while others are chemically synthesised in a lab, food colourants too have a **natural** origin (beetroot, turmeric) and a **synthetic** origin (tartrazine, allura red).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why colour matters in food:** Colour is the *first sensory attribute* a consumer evaluates. A faded butter or a dull green pickle is often rejected before tasting — this is called the **"Colour-Flavour Interaction"** phenomenon.

### 1.3 Standard Classification Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Reference |
|---|---|
| **Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI)** | Expressed in **mg/kg body weight/day** |
| **Permitted concentration in food** | Expressed in **ppm (parts per million)** or **mg/kg** |
| **Regulatory body (India)** | FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India) |
| **Global regulatory body** | JECFA (Joint FAO/WHO Expert Committee on Food Additives) |
| **EU numbering system** | E-number (e.g., Tartrazine = E102) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Broad Classification of Food Colouring Agents

Food colourants are classified based on **origin** and **solubility**, as shown below.

#### A. Based on Origin
- **Natural Colouring Agents** — derived from plant, animal, mineral, or microbial sources without chemical modification.
- **Synthetic (Artificial) Colouring Agents** — manufactured via chemical synthesis; mostly derived from **coal-tar (azo dyes)** or **petroleum** precursors.
- **Nature-Identical Colouring Agents** — synthesised chemically but chemically identical to a natural colour (e.g., β-carotene).
- **Inorganic Colouring Agents** — mineral-based pigments (e.g., titanium dioxide, iron oxides).

#### B. Based on Solubility
- **Water-soluble dyes** — dissolve in aqueous phase (e.g., tartrazine, caramel).
- **Fat-soluble (oil-soluble) dyes** — dissolve in lipid phase (e.g., annatto, β-carotene, paprika oleoresin).

#### C. Based on Application
- **Dyes** — fully soluble in the food medium.
- **Lakes** — water-insoluble forms of dyes, prepared by adsorbing the dye onto an alumina hydrate substrate; used in products with low moisture.

### 2.2 Structural Chemistry Behind Colour

The visible colour of a molecule arises from the absorption of specific wavelengths of visible light. The key structural feature is the **chromophore** — a group of atoms (often containing extensive conjugation) responsible for colour.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Chromophore Rule:** A molecule must possess a chromophore (like –N=N–, –NO₂, >C=O, conjugated C=C) and, often, an **auxochrome** (like –OH, –NH₂, –SO₃H) to exhibit intense colour. The longer the conjugated π-system, the longer the wavelength absorbed, and hence the colour observed shifts.

For example, the **azo group (–N=N–)** is the principal chromophore in synthetic dyes such as Tartrazine and Allura Red. The sulphonic acid (–SO₃H) group acts as the auxochrome, improving water solubility.

### 2.3 Properties of an Ideal Food Colouring Agent

1. **Non-toxic** at the levels used.
2. **Chemically stable** under processing conditions (heat, light, pH).
3. **Uniformly dispersible** in the food matrix.
4. **Free from objectionable taste and odour.**
5. **Economical** and easily available.
6. **Approved** by regulatory authorities.
7. **Compatible** with other food ingredients.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Property / Term | Description | Example / Numerical Detail |
|---|---|---|
| **Natural colour** | Plant/animal/microbial origin | Curcumin (turmeric), Betanin (beetroot) |
| **Synthetic colour** | Chemically synthesised azo dye | Tartrazine, Sunset Yellow FCF |
| **Chromophore** | Atomic group producing colour | –N=N–, –NO₂, conjugated C=C |
| **Auxochrome** | Group that intensifies colour & improves solubility | –OH, –SO₃H, –NH₂ |
| **Lake** | Dye adsorbed on alumina hydrate | Aluminium Lake of Indigo Carmine |
| **ADI of Tartrazine** | Acceptable Daily Intake | **7.5 mg/kg body weight/day** (JECFA) |
| **ADI of Sunset Yellow FCF** | Acceptable Daily Intake | **2.5 mg/kg body weight/day** (JECFA) |
| **E-number of Caramel** | EU classification | **E150a–E150d** (4 classes) |
| **Betanin (E162)** | Natural red colour, pH-sensitive | Stable at pH 3.5–7.0 |
| **Curcumin (E100)** | Yellow from turmeric rhizome | Soluble in ethanol, oils |
| **FSSAI maximum level (Caramel)** | Permitted limit in beverages | **GMP** (Generally Recognised as Safe) |
| **HLB (Hydrophilic–Lipophilic Balance)** | Indicator of dye solubility | Water-soluble dyes have HLB $\vert$ value $\vert > 10$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The abbreviation **FCF** stands for *Food, Colour & Forage* — a US certification tag indicating the dye is safe for use in food, drugs, and cosmetics.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Colouring agents are extensively used in the **food processing industry** for:
- **Restoration of colour lost during processing** (e.g., canned peas, strawberry jam).
- **Enhancement of naturally occurring colour** (e.g., deepening the orange of margarine).
- **Standardisation of batch-to-batch colour variation** (critical for brand identity in beverages and confectionery).
- **Consumer appeal** in products like confectionery, beverages, dairy, bakery, and meat products.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Chemistry of Key Colourants

### 3.1 Chemical Structure Analysis of Tartrazine (E102)

**Tartrazine** is a mono-azo dye widely used to impart a **lemon-yellow** colour to foods. Its IUPAC name is *trisodium (4E)-5-oxo-1-(4-sulfonatophenyl)-4-[4-sulfonatophenyl)hydrazono]-1H-pyrazole-3-carboxylate*.

The structural backbone consists of:
- A central **pyrazolone ring** (the chromophoric core).
- Two **phenyl rings** substituted with –SO₃Na groups.
- One **–N=N–** azo linkage (chromophore).

The water solubility is imparted by the three **sulphonic acid (–SO₃H → –SO₃Na)** groups, which dissociate in aqueous solution to give Na⁺ ions and the anionic dye.

$$ \text{Solubility}_{\text{Tartrazine in water}} \approx 200 \text{ g/L at } 25\,^{\circ}\text{C} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The presence of three –SO₃Na groups makes tartrazine **highly water-soluble** but also explains why it may trigger **allergic reactions** (especially in aspirin-sensitive individuals), an aspect often asked in KTU exams.

### 3.2 pH-Sensitivity of Anthocyanins (Natural Red-Blue Pigments)

Anthocyanins are flavonoid pigments responsible for the red, purple, and blue colours in many fruits (grape, blueberry, raspberry, red cabbage). They exhibit **remarkable pH-dependent colour shifts**, making them an important case study.

Consider the simplified equilibrium:

$$ \text{AH}^+ \;(\text{red}) \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{A} \;(\text{purple}) \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{A}^- \;(\text{blue}) \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{chalcone} \;(\text{colourless}) $$

where:
- $AH^+$ = flavylium cation (predominant at **pH 1–3**, red).
- $A$ = carbinol pseudo-base (predominant at **pH 4–5**, purple/violet).
- $A^-$ = quinonoidal base (predominant at **pH 6–7**, blue).
- *Chalcone* = open-ring colourless form (predominant at **pH > 7**, fading to yellow/brown).

**Step-by-step analytical reasoning:**

1. At very low pH (acidic medium), the flavylium cation ($AH^+$) is stabilised by the positive charge on the oxygen, absorbing in the **green region (~520 nm)**, hence appearing red.

2. As pH rises, deprotonation occurs at the hydroxyl group, forming the neutral carbinol pseudo-base ($A$). The absorption maximum shifts towards longer wavelengths, producing **purple/blue tones**.

3. Further deprotonation gives the quinonoidal base ($A^-$), absorbing in the **orange region (~620 nm)**, hence blue appearance.

4. Above pH 7, the pyrylium ring opens to form the colourless **chalcone**, which is why anthocyanins are unsuitable for use in alkaline foods.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often claim that anthocyanins are *unstable* at all pH levels. The correct statement is: **anthocyanins are most stable at pH 1–3 (highly acidic) and progressively lose colour as pH rises above 4.**

### 3.3 Caramel Colour — The Maillard Reaction Derivation

**Caramel colour (E150)** is the world's most widely used food colourant, produced by the **controlled thermal decomposition (caramelisation)** of carbohydrates.

The reaction proceeds through the following mechanistic stages:

**Step 1: Thermal degradation of sucrose**
When sucrose is heated above **160 °C**, the glycosidic bond cleaves, producing **glucose** and **fructose**.

**Step 2: Enolisation and fragmentation**
The resulting monosaccharides undergo enolisation, yielding reactive intermediates like **3-deoxyglucos-2-ene (3-DG)** and **hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF)**.

**Step 3: Polymerisation and condensation**
These intermediates condense, polymerise, and form brown nitrogenous polymers called **melanoidins** (in the presence of amino acids, this is the **Maillard reaction**). The intensity of the brown colour depends on the catalyst used.

Four commercial classes of caramel colour exist:

| Class | Catalyst Used | Net Charge | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **E150a — Plain Caramel** | None / acid | Negative | Spirits, vinegars |
| **E150b — Caustic Sulphite Caramel** | Sulphites + alkali | Negative | Cola beverages, baked goods |
| **E150c — Ammonia Caramel** | Ammonia | Positive | Bakery, soy sauce |
| **E150d — Sulphite Ammonia Caramel** | Ammonia + sulphites | Negative | Cola beverages (most common) |

The colour intensity is measured in **EBC units (European Brewery Convention)** — a value commonly tested in KTU numerical problems.

$$ \text{EBC} = 25 \times A_{430\,\text{nm}} \times \text{Dilution Factor} $$

where $A_{430\,\text{nm}}$ is the absorbance measured at **430 nm** (the wavelength at which brown colour absorbs).

### 3.4 Comparative Stability Matrix of Major Colourants

| Colourant | Heat Stability | Light Stability | pH Stability | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Tartrazine** | Good | Moderate | 3–8 | Confectionery, beverages |
| **Sunset Yellow FCF** | Good | Moderate | 3–8 | Soft drinks, ice cream |
| **Caramel (E150d)** | Excellent | Excellent | Wide range | Cola, bakery |
| **Curcumin** | Moderate | Poor (fades) | 2.5–7 | Curries, mustard, pickles |
| **β-Carotene** | Moderate (oxidises) | Sensitive | Wide | Margarine, cheese, butter |
| **Annatto (E160b)** | Good | Good | 4–8 | Cheese, snacks |
| **Anthocyanins** | Moderate | Moderate | 1–4 (best) | Jams, beverages |
| **Betalains (Betanin)** | Moderate | Moderate | 3–7 | Frozen desserts, confectionery |
| **Chlorophyll** | Poor | Very poor | 6–7 | Vegetables, oils |
| **Titanium Dioxide (E171)** | Excellent | Excellent | Wide | Whiting agent, confectionery coatings |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart of Food Colouring Agents

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Food Colouring Agents"] --> B["By Origin"]
    A --> C["By Solubility"]
    A --> D["By Form"]

    B --> B1["Natural Colours"]
    B --> B2["Synthetic Colours"]
    B --> B3["Nature-Identical Colours"]
    B --> B4["Inorganic Pigments"]

    B1 --> B1a["Plant: Curcumin, Annatto, Betanin"]
    B1 --> B1b["Animal: Cochineal Carmine E120"]
    B1 --> B1c["Microbial: Monascus Red"]
    B1 --> B1d["Mineral: Iron Oxide, TiO2"]

    B2 --> B2a["Azo Dyes: Tartrazine, Sunset Yellow"]
    B2 --> B2b["Triphenylmethane: Brilliant Blue FCF"]
    B2 --> B2c["Xanthene: Erythrosine"]

    B3 --> B3a["Beta Carotene E160a"]
    B3 --> B3b["Riboflavin E101"]

    C --> C1["Water Soluble Dyes"]
    C --> C2["Fat Soluble Dyes"]
    C --> C3["Lakes: Insoluble forms"]

    D --> D1["Dyes: Soluble form"]
    D --> D2["Pigments: Insoluble particles"]
    D --> D3["Lakes: Dye on Alumina"]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture of an Azo Dye (Tartrazine)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph core["Chromophoric Core"]
        N1["Azo Group -N=N-"]:::chromo
        N2["Pyrazolone Ring C3-N2-O"]:::chromo
    end

    subgraph aux["Auxochromic Groups"]
        A1["Sulphonic Acid -SO3H x3"]:::auxo
        A2["Hydroxyl -OH"]:::auxo
    end

    subgraph prop["Resulting Properties"]
        P1["Bright Yellow Hue"]:::prop
        P2["Water Soluble"]:::prop
        P3["pH Stable 3 to 8"]:::prop
        P4["Lambda max 425 nm"]:::prop
    end

    core --> prop
    aux --> prop

    classDef chromo fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#8a6d00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef auxo fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef prop fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 pH–Colour Transition Block Diagram for Anthocyanins

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["pH 1 to 3: Flavylium Cation AH+"] --> P2["pH 4 to 5: Carbinol Pseudo-base A"]
    P2 --> P3["pH 6 to 7: Quinonoidal Base A-"]
    P3 --> P4["pH greater than 7: Chalcone Colourless"]

    P1 --> C1["Red Hue"]
    P2 --> C2["Purple to Violet"]
    P3 --> C3["Blue Hue"]
    P4 --> C4["Yellow to Brown Fading"]

    P1 --- C1
    P2 --- C2
    P3 --- C3
    P4 --- C4
```

### 4.4 Caramel Colour Production Flow (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1["Raw Material: Sucrose or Glucose Syrup"] --> R2["Heating to 160 to 200 C"]
    R2 --> R3["Catalyst Addition"]
    R3 --> R3a["Class I: No Catalyst"]
    R3 --> R3b["Class II: Sulphite plus Alkali"]
    R3 --> R3c["Class III: Ammonia"]
    R3 --> R3d["Class IV: Ammonia plus Sulphite"]
    R3a --> R4["Maillard Reaction and Polymerisation"]
    R3b --> R4
    R3c --> R4
    R3d --> R4
    R4 --> R5["Melanoidin Formation: Brown Colour"]
    R5 --> R6["Quality Control: EBC Absorbance at 430 nm"]
    R6 --> R7["Final Caramel Colour Product"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above architecture maps the **interactions** of the caramelisation process. For a physical laboratory setup, the heating vessel, agitator, and condenser would be added at the engineering scale.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define food colouring agents. Mention any four properties expected of an ideal food colourant.*

**Model Answer:**
Food colouring agents are substances, natural or synthetic, that are added to food to impart, restore, or enhance its colour without affecting its nutritional value. They are considered food additives and are regulated by authorities such as FSSAI and JECFA.

**Four properties of an ideal food colourant:**
1. **Non-toxic** at the permitted levels of use.
2. **Chemically and thermally stable** during food processing and storage.
3. **Free from off-flavours and off-odours.**
4. **Approved** by national and international food safety authorities.
*(Other acceptable answers: economical, uniformly dispersible, compatible with food matrix.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Distinguish between natural and synthetic food colours. Give two examples for each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Natural Colours | Synthetic Colours |
|---|---|---|
| **Source** | Plant, animal, microbial, mineral | Coal-tar, petroleum-derived |
| **Purity** | Variable, depends on source | High, standardised |
| **Stability** | Often poor (heat, pH, light sensitive) | Generally more stable |
| **Cost** | Usually higher | Usually cheaper |
| **Tone** | Muted, earthy | Bright, vivid |
| **Examples** | Curcumin (turmeric), Betanin (beetroot) | Tartrazine (E102), Sunset Yellow FCF (E110) |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify food colouring agents in detail with suitable examples. Explain the chemistry behind the colour produced by azo dyes.

**Model Answer:**

Food colouring agents are broadly classified as follows:

**1. Natural Colouring Agents**
- **Plant origin:** Curcumin (yellow), Betanin (red), Anthocyanins (red–blue), Carotenoids (yellow–orange), Chlorophyll (green), Annatto (yellow-orange).
- **Animal origin:** Cochineal/Carmine (red, from *Dactylopius coccus*).
- **Microbial origin:** Monascus red (from *Monascus purpureus*).
- **Mineral origin:** Iron oxide, Titanium dioxide.

**2. Synthetic Colouring Agents**
- **Azo dyes:** Tartrazine (E102), Sunset Yellow FCF (E110), Allura Red AC (E129), Carmoisine (E122).
- **Triphenylmethane dyes:** Brilliant Blue FCF (E133), Patent Blue V (E131).
- **Xanthene dyes:** Erythrosine (E127).

**3. Nature-Identical Colours:** β-carotene, riboflavin — chemically identical to natural forms but synthesised industrially.

**4. Inorganic Pigments:** Titanium dioxide (whitener), Iron oxides.

**Chemistry of Azo Dyes:**

Azo dyes contain one or more **–N=N– (azo) groups** as the chromophore, linked to aromatic (usually benzene or naphthalene) rings. The azo group absorbs visible light because of its **extended π-electron delocalisation** with the aromatic rings. The absorption maximum ($\lambda_{max}$) shifts to longer wavelengths as the conjugated system lengthens. Auxochromes such as –OH, –SO₃H, and –NR₂ intensify the colour and improve water solubility. The general structure can be represented as:

$$ \text{Ar}_1 - N = N - \text{Ar}_2 $$

where Ar₁ and Ar₂ are aromatic rings bearing auxochromic substituents. In tartrazine, for instance, the azo group is connected to a pyrazolone ring and two sulphonated phenyl rings, producing a bright yellow hue with $\lambda_{max} \approx 425$ nm.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the various classes of caramel colour with their production methods and applications.

**Model Answer:**

Caramel colour is produced by the controlled heating of carbohydrates (sucrose, glucose syrup, invert sugar) in the presence or absence of catalysts. The colour arises from **melanoidin polymers** formed by the Maillard reaction. There are four commercial classes:

**Class I — Plain Caramel (E150a):**
- **Catalyst:** None (or mild acid).
- **Net charge:** Negative.
- **Production:** Heating carbohydrate at 160–180 °C.
- **Application:** Spirits, vinegars, baked goods.

**Class II — Caustic Sulphite Caramel (E150b):**
- **Catalyst:** Sulphites + alkali (NaOH).
- **Net charge:** Negative.
- **Production:** Heating with SO₂ and alkali.
- **Application:** Cola beverages, confectionery.

**Class III — Ammonia Caramel (E150c):**
- **Catalyst:** Ammonia (NH₃).
- **Net charge:** Positive.
- **Production:** Heating with ammonium salts.
- **Application:** Bakery products, soy sauce.

**Class IV — Sulphite Ammonia Caramel (E150d):**
- **Catalyst:** Ammonia + sulphites.
- **Net charge:** Negative.
- **Production:** Most widely produced class; used in cola drinks.
- **Application:** Cola beverages (Coca-Cola, Pepsi), gravies, sauces.

The quality is monitored by measuring **absorbance at 430 nm (EBC units)** and the **colour intensity index**. Caramel colour is preferred for its excellent heat and light stability, making it ideal for beverages subjected to pasteurisation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the structural features responsible for colour in food dyes. Differentiate between dyes and lakes.

**Model Answer:**

**Structural Features Responsible for Colour:**

The colour of a molecule is governed by the interaction of its electrons with visible light (wavelength 380–750 nm). Molecules absorb specific wavelengths, and the complementary colour is perceived. Two structural features are essential:

**1. Chromophore:** A group of atoms that absorbs visible light due to the presence of multiple bonds or extended π-conjugation. Common chromophores in food dyes include:
- **Azo group (–N=N–)** — present in tartrazine, sunset yellow.
- **Carbonyl group (C=O)** — present in curcumin, anthocyanins.
- **Ethylenic group (C=C)** — present in carotenoids.
- **Nitro group (–NO₂)** — present in some dyes.

**2. Auxochrome:** A group that does not absorb visible light by itself but intensifies the colour and improves solubility. Examples: –OH, –NH₂, –SO₃H, –COOH.

**Rule of thumb:** The longer the conjugated π-system, the longer the wavelength absorbed. Hence, increasing conjugation shifts colour from yellow → orange → red → purple → blue.

**Dyes vs Lakes:**

| Feature | Dye | Lake |
|---|---|---|
| **Solubility** | Water-soluble | Water-insoluble |
| **Form** | True solution | Dispersion of solid particles |
| **Preparation** | Direct synthesis | Dye precipitated/adsorbed on alumina hydrate (Al₂O₃·xH₂O) |
| **Use** | Beverages, syrups, jams | Tablets, coated confectionery, low-moisture foods, cosmetics |
| **Stability in pH** | Variable | Generally more stable |
| **Tone** | Transparent, bright | Opaque, vivid |

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write a detailed note on the natural food colourants — anthocyanins, carotenoids, and betalains — with respect to their sources, colour, and stability.

**Model Answer:**

**1. Anthocyanins (E163):**
- **Source:** Grape skin, blueberry, raspberry, red cabbage, blackberry, elderberry.
- **Chemical class:** Flavonoid glycosides; the aglycone is called an *anthocyanidin* (e.g., cyanidin, delphinidin, pelargonidin).
- **Colour produced:** Red (acidic), purple (neutral), blue (alkaline).
- **Stability:** Best at **pH 1–3**; progressively unstable as pH rises; sensitive to heat, light, and oxygen.
- **Applications:** Soft drinks, jams, jellies, confectionery (acidic products).

**2. Carotenoids (E160a–E161g):**
- **Source:** Carrot (β-carotene), tomato (lycopene), annatto (bixin), paprika (capsanthin), saffron (crocin).
- **Chemical class:** Isoprenoid hydrocarbons (C40) with extensive conjugated double bonds.
- **Colour produced:** Yellow, orange, red.
- **Stability:** Reasonably stable to heat but highly sensitive to **light, oxygen, and metals** (Fe, Cu).
- **Applications:** Margarine, butter, cheese, baked goods, oils, beverages.

**3. Betalains (E162):**
- **Source:** Beetroot (betanin), amaranth, prickly pear, dragon fruit.
- **Chemical class:** Nitrogen-containing pigments derived from betalamic acid. Two sub-classes:
  - **Betacyanins** (red-violet) — e.g., betanin.
  - **Betaxanthins** (yellow-orange) — e.g., vulgaxanthin.
- **Colour produced:** Red to violet (betalains) and yellow (betaxanthins).
- **Stability:** Sensitive to heat, light, oxygen, and pH > 7. Best at pH 3–7.
- **Applications:** Frozen desserts, confectionery, dairy products, soups.

**Comparison Summary:**
| Property | Anthocyanins | Carotenoids | Betalains |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Colour** | Red–purple–blue | Yellow–orange–red | Red–violet, yellow |
| **Optimum pH** | 1–3 | 4–8 | 3–7 |
| **Heat stability** | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Nitrogen content** | No | No | Yes (unique feature) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Confusing **anthocyanins with carotenoids** — remember anthocyanins are water-soluble flavonoid pigments, whereas carotenoids are fat-soluble isoprenoid pigments.
> 2. Writing that **all synthetic dyes are unsafe** — many (Tartrazine, Sunset Yellow) are approved by FSSAI and JECFA at permitted levels.
> 3. Forgetting the **E-number** of caramel colour (E150) — this is a high-weightage one-liner in KTU papers.
> 4. Not mentioning the **–N=N– chromophore** when describing azo dyes.
> 5. Confusing **lake** with **pigment** — lakes are dye-adsorbed alumina, whereas pigments are inherently insoluble.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Food colourants** are additives that **impart, restore, or enhance** the colour of food.
- Classification is by **origin** (natural, synthetic, nature-identical, inorganic), **solubility** (water-soluble, fat-soluble, lakes), and **form** (dyes, pigments, lakes).
- **Chromophore** (e.g., –N=N–) is responsible for colour; **auxochrome** (e.g., –SO₃H, –OH) intensifies it and improves solubility.
- **Tartrazine (E102)**, **Sunset Yellow FCF (E110)**, **Allura Red AC (E129)**, **Brilliant Blue FCF (E133)** are the most common synthetic azo dyes.
- **Anthocyanins (E163)** are pH-sensitive flavonoid pigments: red at pH 1–3, purple at 4–5, blue at 6–7, and fade to colourless above pH 7.
- **Carotenoids (E160a)** are fat-soluble C40 isoprenoids; sensitive to light, oxygen, and metals.
- **Betalains (E162)** are nitrogen-containing pigments from beetroot; stable at pH 3–7.
- **Caramel colour (E150)** has four classes (a, b, c, d) based on catalyst use and net charge.
- **Cochineal/Carmine (E120)** is a red animal-origin colour from the *Dactylopius coccus* insect.
- **Chlorophyll** is a porphyrin-ring pigment; degrades to pheophytin (olive-brown) upon heating in acidic conditions.
- **Titanium dioxide (E171)** is an inorganic white pigment (now under regulatory review in the EU).
- **Lakes** are water-insoluble dye forms made by adsorbing dyes on alumina hydrate — used in dry mixes, tablets, and coated confectionery.
- The **ADI** of tartrazine is **7.5 mg/kg body weight/day** (JECFA).
- Regulatory bodies: **FSSAI (India), FDA (USA), EFSA (Europe), JECFA (Joint FAO/WHO).**
- **EBC units** measure caramel colour intensity at **430 nm absorbance**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Emulsifiers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT504 - FOOD ADDITIVES AND FLAVORINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Emulsifiers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Emulsifier (Definition per KTU 2024 / FSSAI Aligned Terminology):**
> An **emulsifier** is a **surface-active agent (surfactant)** that stabilizes an emulsion by **adsorbing at the oil–water interface**, thereby **reducing interfacial tension**, preventing coalescence, and producing a **kinetically stable, homogeneous dispersion** of two immiscible liquids. Emulsifiers are classified as permitted **food additives** under FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India) and INS (International Numbering System) regulations.

Mathematically, emulsifier effectiveness is quantified through the **Hydrophilic–Lipophilic Balance (HLB)** number, an empirical scale developed by **William C. Griffin (1949)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Diplomat" Molecule 🍽️**
> 
> Imagine **oil and water** as two rival nations that refuse to mix — like India and Pakistan refusing to share a cricket field. If you pour oil into water and shake it, tiny droplets form, but within seconds they **clump back together** (coalesce) because oil molecules and water molecules **cannot understand each other's language**.
> 
> Now bring in an **emulsifier molecule**. It has a **"bilingual" personality**:
> - One end is **hydrophilic** (water-loving — like a person fluent in Hindi 🇮🇳)
> - The other end is **lipophilic** (oil-loving — like a person fluent in Urdu 🇵🇰)
> 
> The emulsifier sits exactly at the **boundary (interface)** between oil and water, holding them together like a diplomat at a peace conference. The result? A **stable emulsion** like milk, mayonnaise, or ice cream.
> 
> **Translation for Food Tech Engineers:** Without emulsifiers, your ice cream is just gritty water with floating butter chunks. With emulsifiers, you get the smooth, velvety texture consumers demand.

## 1.3 Standardized Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Quantities (Bolded):**
> - **HLB Scale Range:** **0 to 20** (Griffin's method), **0 to 40** (Davies' method)
> - **Critical Micelle Concentration (CMC):** typically **10⁻⁶ to 10⁻² mol/L** in aqueous systems
> - **Interfacial Tension (γ):** reduced from **~50 mN/m** (oil/water alone) to **~1–10 mN/m** with emulsifier
> - **Standard Temperature:** emulsifier functionality measured at **25 °C** (298.15 K)
> - **FSSAI Permitted INS Codes:** ranges from **INS 322** (Lecithin) to **INS 496** (Polyglycerol esters)
> - **Regulatory Limit:** Generally **GRAS (Generally Recognized As Safe)** up to quantum satis in most foods, except specific limits (e.g., **5000 mg/kg** for polysorbates in some categories per Codex)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanism — Why and How Emulsifiers Work

### Step-by-Step Logic of Emulsification

1. **Mechanical Energy Input:** Two immiscible liquids (oil + water) are subjected to shear (homogenization, high-pressure mixing, ultrasonication).
2. **Droplet Formation:** Bulk oil is broken into fine droplets (typically **0.1–100 µm**) dispersed in the continuous water phase.
3. **Interfacial Instability:** Without emulsifier, the droplets immediately **coalesce** (merge) to minimize interfacial area, due to high **positive interfacial tension (γ ≈ +50 mN/m)**.
4. **Emulsifier Adsorption:** Surfactant molecules **diffuse to the interface** and orient with the **hydrophilic head in water** and the **lipophilic tail in oil**, forming a **monolayer (film)**.
5. **Interfacial Tension Reduction:** The surfactant film reduces γ to near-zero or even transient negative values, **preventing coalescence thermodynamically and kinetically**.
6. **Stabilization Mechanisms:** Three forces stabilize the system:
   - **Electrostatic repulsion** (for ionic emulsifiers like SSL — Sodium Stearoyl Lactylate)
   - **Steric hindrance** (for non-ionic emulsifiers like Polysorbates)
   - **Mechanical film strength** (viscoelastic interfacial layer)

### Bancroft's Rule (1913)

> [!NOTE]
> **Bancroft's Rule:** "The phase in which the emulsifier is **more soluble** tends to become the **continuous (external) phase**."
> - If emulsifier is **water-soluble** → forms **O/W emulsion** (oil-in-water, e.g., milk)
> - If emulsifier is **oil-soluble** → forms **W/O emulsion** (water-in-oil, e.g., butter)

### Oriented Wedge Theory (Harkins, 1917)

The geometric shape of the emulsifier molecule determines curvature:
- Cone-shaped (small head, large tail) → favors **W/O**
- Tapered/wedge-shaped (large head, small tail) → favors **O/W**

## 2.2 HLB System (Hydrophilic-Lipophilic Balance) — The Central Concept

The **HLB number** is the single most important parameter for selecting emulsifiers in the food industry. It is a dimensionless scale quantifying the balance between hydrophilic and lipophilic portions of the surfactant.

### Griffin's Method (for non-ionic surfactants)

$$HLB = 20 \times \left( \frac{M_h}{M_t} \right)$$

where $M_h$ = molecular weight of the hydrophilic portion, $M_t$ = total molecular weight of the molecule.

### Alternative Griffin Formula (using saponification number)

$$HLB = 20 \times \left(1 - \frac{S}{A}\right)$$

where $S$ = saponification number of the ester, $A$ = acid number of the fatty acid.

### Davies' Group Contribution Method (1957)

$$HLB = \sum \left( \text{Hydrophilic group numbers} \right) + \sum \left( \text{Lipophilic group numbers} \right) + 7$$

### HLB Range Interpretation Table

| HLB Range | Function / Application | Example Emulsifier | Typical Food Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 – 3 | Antifoaming agent | Sorbitan tristearate (Span 65) | Cooking oil defoamer |
| 4 – 6 | W/O Emulsifier | Sorbitan monostearate (Span 60) | Butter, margarine, spreads |
| 7 – 9 | Wetting agent | – | Instant drink powders |
| 8 – 18 | O/W Emulsifier | Polysorbate 80 (Tween 80) | Ice cream, milk, cake batter |
| 13 – 15 | Detergent / Cleaning | – | Industrial cleaners |
| 15 – 18 | Solubilizer | Polysorbate 20 (Tween 20) | Flavors, vitamins |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula Name | Equation | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **HLB (Griffin)** | $HLB = 20 \cdot \frac{M_h}{M_t}$ | Select emulsifier | Dimensionless |
| **HLB (Saponification)** | $HLB = 20 \cdot (1 - \frac{S}{A})$ | Ester-based emulsifiers | Dimensionless |
| **HLB (Davies)** | $HLB = \Sigma H_n + \Sigma L_n + 7$ | Group contribution method | Dimensionless |
| **Stokes' Law (Creaming)** | $v = \frac{2 r^2 (\rho_p - \rho_m) g}{9 \eta}$ | Emulsion stability prediction | m/s |
| **Interfacial Tension Reduction** | $\Delta \gamma = \gamma_0 - \gamma_s$ | Efficiency of emulsifier | mN/m |
| **PIT (Phase Inversion T)** | $T_{PIT}$ at HLB = balance | Non-ionic emulsion stability | °C |
| **Laplace Pressure (Droplet)** | $\Delta P = \frac{2 \gamma}{r}$ | Energy for droplet disruption | Pa |
| **CMC** | $\gamma_{CMC}$ point | Micelle formation onset | mol/L |

> Where in Stokes' equation: $r$ = droplet radius, $\rho_p$ = particle density, $\rho_m$ = medium density, $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $\eta$ = viscosity of medium.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The HLB system is **the cornerstone of industrial food formulation**:
- **Product development**: Match HLB of emulsifier blend to required HLB of oil phase
- **Quality control**: Predict emulsion stability from HLB-temperature profile (PIT method)
- **Cost optimization**: Replace expensive emulsifiers with cheaper blends of matching HLB
- **Scale-up**: Translate lab recipes to commercial production using HLB consistency

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: HLB by Griffin's Method — Complete Mathematical Walkthrough

### Problem Setup

A food-grade emulsifier is the ester formed from **sorbitol (hydrophilic)** and **stearic acid (lipophilic)**. The molecular weight of the hydrophilic portion $M_h = 180$ g/mol, and the total molecular weight $M_t = 430$ g/mol. Calculate the HLB.

### Solution Steps

**Step 1: Identify the appropriate formula**

Since this is a non-ionic emulsifier derived from a polyol (sorbitol) esterified with a fatty acid, Griffin's molecular weight method applies:

$$HLB = 20 \times \left( \frac{M_h}{M_t} \right)$$

**Step 2: Substitute the given values**

$$HLB = 20 \times \left( \frac{180}{430} \right)$$

**Step 3: Compute the ratio**

$$HLB = 20 \times 0.4186$$

$$\boxed{HLB \approx 8.37}$$

**Step 4: Interpret the result**

Since $8 \le HLB \le 18$, this emulsifier is suitable as an **O/W (oil-in-water) emulsifier** — ideal for milk, sauces, ice cream, and beverages.

---

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example: Predicting Creaming Velocity

### Problem Statement

A milk sample (O/W emulsion) has fat droplets of average radius $r = 5 \, \mu m = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ m. The fat density $\rho_p = 920$ kg/m³, the skim milk (continuous phase) density $\rho_m = 1035$ kg/m³, and viscosity $\eta = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s. Compute the creaming velocity.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Recall Stokes' Law**

$$v = \frac{2 r^2 (\rho_p - \rho_m) g}{9 \eta}$$

**Step 2: Compute $r^2$**

$$r^2 = (5 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 25 \times 10^{-12} \, m^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-11} \, m^2$$

**Step 3: Compute the density difference**

$$\rho_p - \rho_m = 920 - 1035 = -115 \, kg/m^3$$

The negative sign indicates fat is **less dense** than the surrounding medium → fat droplets **rise** (cream) — hence cream layer forms on top.

**Step 4: Compute the numerator**

$$2 r^2 (\rho_p - \rho_m) g = 2 \times (2.5 \times 10^{-11}) \times (-115) \times 9.81$$

$$= 2 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-11} \times (-1128.15)$$

$$= -5.64 \times 10^{-8} \, (SI)$$

**Step 5: Compute the denominator**

$$9 \eta = 9 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} = 1.35 \times 10^{-2} \, Pa \cdot s$$

**Step 6: Divide**

$$v = \frac{-5.64 \times 10^{-8}}{1.35 \times 10^{-2}} = -4.18 \times 10^{-6} \, m/s$$

$$\boxed{v \approx -4.18 \times 10^{-4} \, cm/s}$$

**Step 7: Engineering Interpretation**

The negative sign confirms **upward creaming**. The magnitude (~4 µm/s) means a cream layer forms in a bottle of unhomogenized milk within a few hours — hence the industry uses **homogenization** (reducing $r$ to ~1 µm) to slow creaming by a factor of **25×** (since $v \propto r^2$).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Required HLB Values for Common Food Oils

| Oil/Fat Source | Required HLB (for O/W) | Required HLB (for W/O) |
|---|---|---|
| Mineral oil (light) | 10.0 | 4.0 |
| Corn oil | 12.0 | 6.0 |
| Soybean oil | 12.0 | 6.0 |
| Olive oil | 11.0 | 5.0 |
| Palm oil | 11.5 | 5.5 |
| Cottonseed oil | 12.0 | 6.0 |
| Butter fat | 9.0 | 4.0 |
| Lard | 10.0 | 5.0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Tip:** When formulating an emulsion, you **blend two emulsifiers** such that the **weighted average HLB** of the blend equals the **required HLB** of the oil phase. This is a frequently asked numerical problem in KTU exams.

**Formula for HLB of a Blend:**

$$HLB_{blend} = \frac{\left( W_A \times HLB_A \right) + \left( W_B \times HLB_B \right)}{W_A + W_B}$$

where $W_A, W_B$ are the weight fractions of emulsifiers A and B.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: HLB Blend Calculation

### Problem

You need to emulsify **cottonseed oil (required HLB = 12)** using a blend of **Span 60 (HLB = 4.7)** and **Tween 80 (HLB = 15.0)**. What weight ratio of Tween 80 : Span 60 gives the target HLB?

### Solution

Let weight of Span 60 = 1 unit, weight of Tween 80 = $x$ units.

$$HLB_{blend} = \frac{(1 \times 4.7) + (x \times 15.0)}{1 + x} = 12$$

Multiply both sides by $(1+x)$:

$$4.7 + 15.0x = 12 + 12x$$

$$15.0x - 12x = 12 - 4.7$$

$$3.0x = 7.3$$

$$\boxed{x \approx 2.43}$$

So the blend ratio is approximately **Tween 80 : Span 60 = 2.43 : 1.00** (i.e., about **71% Tween 80** and **29% Span 60** by weight).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Architecture of an O/W Emulsion with Emulsifier Action

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Oil Droplet Hydrophobic Core] --> B[Lipophilic Tail of Emulsifier Anchored in Oil]
    B --> C[Interfacial Monolayer Film]
    C --> D[Hydrophilic Head of Emulsifier in Water Phase]
    D --> E[Continuous Aqueous Phase Water]
    E --> F[Electrostatic / Steric Repulsion Between Droplets]
    F --> G[Stable O-W Emulsion e.g., Milk]
    
    H[Mechanical Shear Homogenizer] --> A
    I[Surfactant Diffusion to Interface] --> B
    J[Reduction of Interfacial Tension gamma] --> C
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Architecture of Emulsifier Selection Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Oil Phase Composition] --> B[Determine Required HLB from Reference Table]
    B --> C{Check Emulsion Type}
    C -->|O/W Required| D[Select Emulsifier A with HLB 8 to 18]
    C -->|W/O Required| E[Select Emulsifier B with HLB 4 to 6]
    D --> F[Compute Weighted HLB Blend]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Match Target HLB within plus minus 0.5]
    G --> H[Conduct Lab Scale Stability Test]
    H --> I{Passes Centrifuge Test}
    I -->|Yes| J[Scale Up to Production]
    I -->|No| K[Adjust Blend Ratio and Re-test]
    K --> F
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Classification Tree of Food Emulsifiers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Food Emulsifiers] --> B[Natural Source]
    A --> C[Synthetic Source]
    A --> D[Semi Synthetic]
    
    B --> B1[Lecithin from Soy or Egg Yolk INS 322]
    B --> B2[Casein from Milk]
    B --> B3[Gum Arabic]
    B --> B4[Mustard Protein]
    
    C --> C1[Polysorbates Tween Series INS 432 to 436]
    C --> C2[Sorbitan Esters Span Series INS 491 to 495]
    C --> C3[Propylene Glycol Esters INS 477]
    
    D --> D1[Mono and Diglycerides INS 471]
    D --> D2[DATEM INS 472e]
    D --> D3[SSL Sodium Stearoyl Lactylate INS 481]
    D --> D4[Polyglycerol Esters INS 475]
    
    B1 --> E[Function: O/W Stabilizer in Chocolate, Baked Goods]
    C1 --> F[Function: Solubilizer in Ice Cream]
    D1 --> G[Function: W/O Stabilizer in Margarine]
    D2 --> H[Function: Dough Strengthener in Bread]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Mechanism of Emulsion Stability vs. Instability

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Freshly Formed Emulsion] --> B[Short Term Stability hours]
    B --> C[Medium Term Stability days]
    C --> D[Long Term Stability months to years]
    
    A --> A1[Threat 1: Flocculation Droplets Clump]
    B --> B1[Threat 2: Creaming or Sedimentation per Stokes Law]
    C --> C1[Threat 3: Coalescence Droplets Merge]
    D --> D1[Threat 4: Phase Inversion Emulsion Type Flips]
    
    A1 --> E[Counter: Electrostatic Repulsion]
    B1 --> F[Counter: Viscosity Increase via Thickener]
    C1 --> G[Counter: Strong Interfacial Film]
    D1 --> H[Counter: Avoid PIT in Process Range]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The Mermaid flowcharts above illustrate the **functional flow** of emulsifier systems. In a real food factory, you would see HLB computation dashboards, surfactant blending tanks, and high-pressure homogenizers — all governed by the principles shown.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: Define an emulsifier. Mention two natural sources of food emulsifiers.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** An emulsifier is a **surface-active agent (surfactant)** that stabilizes an emulsion by **adsorbing at the oil–water interface**, reducing interfacial tension, and preventing coalescence of dispersed phase droplets. It possesses both **hydrophilic** (polar) and **lipophilic** (non-polar) moieties in the same molecule.
>
> **Two natural sources (1 mark):**
> 1. **Egg yolk** — source of lecithin (phospholipid emulsifier, INS 322)
> 2. **Soybean** — source of commercial lecithin used in chocolate and bakery

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: What is the HLB value? State its significance in food formulation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** HLB (Hydrophilic-Lipophilic Balance) is a **dimensionless numerical scale (0–20)** that quantifies the balance between hydrophilic and lipophilic portions of a surfactant molecule. It was introduced by **Griffin (1949)**.
>
> **Significance (1 mark):** It allows food technologists to **rationally select emulsifiers** for specific applications — e.g., HLB 8–18 for O/W emulsions (milk, ice cream), HLB 4–6 for W/O emulsions (butter, margarine). It eliminates trial-and-error in formulation.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a) Explain the Bancroft's rule and the oriented wedge theory of emulsion formation. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Bancroft's Rule (1913) — 3 marks:**
According to Bancroft, *"the phase in which the emulsifier is more soluble tends to become the continuous phase."*
- If emulsifier is preferentially **water-soluble** → **O/W emulsion** forms
- If emulsifier is preferentially **oil-soluble** → **W/O emulsion** forms

**Example:** Sodium stearate is soluble in water → forms O/W emulsion (e.g., milk).

**Oriented Wedge Theory (Harkins, 1917) — 4 marks:**
This theory states that the **geometric shape and cross-sectional area** of the emulsifier molecule determine the curvature of the interface and thus the type of emulsion.

- **Cone-shaped molecules** (small hydrophilic head, bulky lipophilic tail): The wedge fits into the oil phase, curving the interface **towards water**, forming a **W/O emulsion**.
- **Tapered / wedge-shaped molecules** (large hydrophilic head, small lipophilic tail): The wide head spreads in water, curving the interface **towards oil**, forming an **O/W emulsion**.

**Example:** Potassium oleate (small head, large tail) → favors W/O. Sodium oleate (similar geometry but ionic repulsion at head) → favors O/W.

**(b) Calculate the HLB of the emulsifier blend required for a margarine formulation containing 60% sunflower oil (required HLB = 6.5 for W/O) using a mixture of Span 80 (HLB = 4.3) and Tween 65 (HLB = 10.5). What percentage of each emulsifier is needed? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Apply HLB blend formula**

$$HLB_{blend} = \frac{(W_1 \times HLB_1) + (W_2 \times HLB_2)}{W_1 + W_2} = 6.5$$

**Step 2: Let $W_1 = 1$ unit (Span 80), $W_2 = x$ (Tween 65)**

$$\frac{(1 \times 4.3) + (x \times 10.5)}{1 + x} = 6.5$$

**Step 3: Cross multiply**

$$4.3 + 10.5x = 6.5(1 + x)$$
$$4.3 + 10.5x = 6.5 + 6.5x$$
$$10.5x - 6.5x = 6.5 - 4.3$$
$$4.0x = 2.2$$
$$x = 0.55$$

**Step 4: Compute percentages**

Total blend = $1 + 0.55 = 1.55$

- Span 80 percentage = $\frac{1}{1.55} \times 100 = 64.5\%$
- Tween 65 percentage = $\frac{0.55}{1.55} \times 100 = 35.5\%$

$$\boxed{\text{Blend ratio: Span 80 : Tween 65} \approx 64.5\% : 35.5\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Students often confuse **required HLB of oil** with the **HLB of emulsifier** — they are **NOT the same**.
> - Ensure the unit of weight (g, kg, %) is **consistent** before computing percentages.
> - Do not mix up **Griffin's HLB (0–20)** with **Davies' HLB (0–40)** in the same calculation — a common 1-mark deduction error.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) Discuss in detail the mechanism of action of emulsifiers with suitable diagram. List four natural and four synthetic emulsifiers used in the food industry. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Mechanism of action (4 marks):**
1. **Migration:** Emulsifier molecules diffuse from bulk phase to the oil–water interface.
2. **Adsorption & Orientation:** They orient with hydrophilic head in aqueous phase and lipophilic tail in oil phase, forming a **monolayer film**.
3. **Interfacial tension reduction:** Surface tension drops from ~50 mN/m to ~1–10 mN/m.
4. **Mechanical barrier:** The film provides steric/electrostatic repulsion, preventing droplet coalescence.
5. **Stabilization:** The emulsion becomes kinetically stable for the desired shelf life.

**Natural emulsifiers (1.5 marks):**
1. **Lecithin (INS 322)** — from soybean, egg yolk
2. **Casein** — from milk
3. **Gum Arabic** — from acacia tree
4. **Mustard** — natural emulsifier in mayonnaise-type dressings

**Synthetic emulsifiers (1.5 marks):**
1. **Polysorbates (Tween 20, 40, 60, 80)** — INS 432–436
2. **Sorbitan esters (Span 20, 40, 60, 80)** — INS 491–495
3. **Mono- and diglycerides (INS 471)**
4. **DATEM (INS 472e)**

**(b) What is the Phase Inversion Temperature (PIT)? Explain its significance with respect to non-ionic emulsifiers. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):** The **Phase Inversion Temperature (PIT)** is the specific temperature at which an **O/W emulsion stabilized by a non-ionic emulsifier** (typically polyethoxylated) **inverts to a W/O emulsion**, or vice versa. At PIT, the HLB of the emulsifier shifts due to temperature-dependent dehydration of the polyoxyethylene chain.

**Mechanism (3 marks):**
- Below PIT: Ethylene oxide groups are **hydrated** → emulsifier is hydrophilic → O/W emulsion
- Above PIT: Ethylene oxide groups are **dehydrated** → emulsifier becomes lipophilic → W/O emulsion
- At PIT: HLB balance is exactly neutral → interfacial tension reaches minimum → **droplet size is smallest**, but **stability is poorest**

**Significance (2 marks):**
1. **Storage stability indicator:** Store emulsions at least **20–40 °C below PIT** to prevent phase inversion during temperature abuse (e.g., hot climate shipping of cream liqueurs).
2. **Processing aid:** PIT method produces **fine droplets** if the emulsion is rapidly cooled through PIT — basis of the **PIT method of emulsification** in cosmetics and pharma foods.
3. **Quality control:** PIT helps select emulsifier-oil pairs whose inversion temperature is **outside the product's intended thermal range**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B):**
> - Do **NOT** confuse **PIT with Cloud Point** — Cloud Point is the temperature at which a non-ionic surfactant becomes **insoluble in water** and separates; PIT specifically refers to **emulsion phase inversion**.
> - Students often draw the diagram incorrectly. The correct orientation is: **hydrophilic head → water**; **lipophilic tail → oil**. Drawing it backwards costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Emulsifiers — High-Yield Revision Sheet**
>
> ✅ **Definition:** Surface-active agent that stabilizes oil–water dispersions by reducing interfacial tension.
>
> ✅ **Dual Nature:** Possesses hydrophilic (polar) + lipophilic (non-polar) groups in one molecule.
>
> ✅ **Bancroft's Rule:** Continuous phase = phase where emulsifier is **more soluble**.
>
> ✅ **Oriented Wedge Theory:** Geometry of molecule decides O/W vs W/O — large head = O/W.
>
> ✅ **HLB (Griffin):** Scale 0–20. HLB = $20 \times (M_h / M_t)$ for non-ionic surfactants.
>
> ✅ **HLB Range Memory Trick:**
> - 0–3: Defoamer
> - 4–6: **W/O** emulsifier
> - 7–9: Wetting agent
> - 8–18: **O/W** emulsifier
> - 13–15: Detergent
> - 15–18: Solubilizer
>
> ✅ **Davies' Method:** $HLB = \Sigma H_n + \Sigma L_n + 7$ (group contributions).
>
> ✅ **Blend HLB:** Weighted average of individual HLB values.
>
> ✅ **Natural emulsifiers:** Lecithin (INS 322), casein, gum arabic, mustard.
>
> ✅ **Synthetic emulsifiers:** Polysorbates (Tween series), Sorbitan esters (Span series), DATEM, SSL, mono/diglycerides.
>
> ✅ **Stokes' Law:** Creaming velocity $v = \frac{2 r^2 (\rho_p - \rho_m) g}{9 \eta}$ — slower creaming achieved by **reducing droplet radius** (homogenization).
>
> ✅ **PIT (Phase Inversion Temperature):** Temperature at which non-ionic emulsions flip from O/W to W/O. Store products **well below PIT**.
>
> ✅ **Stability Threats:** Flocculation → Creaming → Coalescence → Phase Inversion (sequence of emulsion breakdown).
>
> ✅ **Counter-measures:** Electrostatic repulsion, steric hindrance, viscosity increase (xanthan/guar gum), robust interfacial film.
>
> ✅ **FSSAI Compliance:** Emulsifiers must be from the **positive list**, used within **permitted limits**, and labeled by **INS number**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sweeteners and Thickening Agents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT504 - FOOD ADDITIVES AND FLAVORINGS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sweeteners and Thickening Agents

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Sweeteners — Formal Academic Definition

A **sweetener** is a food additive (natural or synthetic) that imparts a sweet taste to a food product without contributing significantly to its nutritional value (in the case of non-nutritive sweeteners) or by providing a sweetening effect with caloric contribution (in the case of nutritive sweeteners). According to the **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC)** and the **Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI)**, sweeteners are regulated as food additives that activate the **T1R2/T1R3 sweet taste receptors** on the human tongue.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (verbatim taxonomy):**
> Sweeteners are classified into **(a) Nutritive Sweeteners (Natural caloric)** — providing ≥2 kcal/g and **(b) Non-Nutritive / High-Intensity Sweeteners (HIS)** — providing essentially zero calories but delivering sweetness at fractional usage levels.

### 1.2 Thickening Agents — Formal Academic Definition

**Thickeners**, more scientifically termed **Hydrocolloids**, are high-molecular-weight, long-chain polymers (often polysaccharides or proteins) capable of binding large quantities of water to produce viscous solutions or gels. They function by:
1. **Hydration** of polar/hydrophilic sites
2. **Chain entanglement** in solution
3. **Three-dimensional gel network** formation (via hydrogen bonds, ionic cross-links, or junction zones)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Tag:** Hydrocolloids are categorized as food additives under **INS Numbers 400–499** by the **Codex Alimentarius International Numbering System (INS)**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Sweetener Analogy:** Think of sweeteners as the "volume knob" of taste. A single grain of table salt (NaCl) makes food salty, but just one molecule of a high-intensity sweetener (HIS) can make a whole glass of water taste sweet. They are the "**concentrated essence**" of sweetness — used in microgram quantities to achieve the same sensory effect as teaspoons of sugar.

**Thickener Analogy:** Imagine a bucket of loose marbles (water molecules) — they flow freely. Now, add a web of gummy worms (hydrocolloid polymer chains) into the bucket. The worms trap the marbles, restrict their movement, and the entire system becomes a "**slow-flowing gel**." That restriction of free movement = **viscosity**; a fully interconnected worm-web that traps ALL marbles = a **gel**.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used Throughout This Module

- **Sweetness Potency (SP)** = Relative sweetness of a sweetener with respect to **sucrose (set as 1.0x baseline)** on a weight basis.
- **Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI)** = mg of additive per **kg body weight per day**, as set by **JECFA (Joint FAO/WHO Expert Committee on Food Additives)**.
- **Viscosity (η)** = Resistance of a fluid to flow, measured in **Pascal·seconds (Pa·s)** or **centipoise (cP)** where 1 Pa·s = 1000 cP.
- **Gel Strength** = Force (in grams) required to break a standard gel, measured using a **Bloom Gelometer** (1% gel, 4 h maturation at 10°C).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Sweeteners

Sweeteners divide into two main classes based on their **energy contribution** and **sweetness intensity**.

#### A. Nutritive Sweeteners (Caloric, Bulk Sweeteners)

These provide **4 kcal/g** (carbohydrate-based) or **2.4 kcal/g** (polyol-based). They also contribute body, texture, and browning (Maillard reaction) in addition to sweetness.

**Sub-class A1 — Monosaccharides:**
- **Glucose (Dextrose):** SP = 0.7x, found in corn syrup, fruits.
- **Fructose (Levulose):** SP = 1.2–1.8x (sweetest natural sugar), found in fruits, honey.

**Sub-class A2 — Disaccharides:**
- **Sucrose:** SP = 1.0x (REFERENCE STANDARD), from sugarcane/beet.
- **Maltose:** SP = 0.4x, malt products.
- **Lactose:** SP = 0.16x (least sweet), milk sugar.

**Sub-class A3 — Sugar Alcohols (Polyols):**
- **Sorbitol (E420):** SP = 0.5–0.6x, ADI = "Not specified" (JECFA).
- **Mannitol (E421):** SP = 0.5–0.7x, ADI = "Not specified".
- **Xylitol (E967):** SP = 1.0x, ADI = "Not specified", anti-cariogenic.
- **Erythritol (E968):** SP = 0.6–0.8x, ADI = "Not specified", zero calories (absorbed but excreted unchanged).
- **Isomalt (E953):** SP = 0.45–0.6x, ADI = 0–50 mg/kg.

#### B. Non-Nutritive / High-Intensity Sweeteners (HIS)

Provide essentially **zero calories**; used at very low concentrations. Sweeter than sucrose by 30× to 13,000×.

| Sweetener | INS No. | Discovery Year | Sweetness Potency (vs Sucrose) | ADI (mg/kg bw/day) | Caloric Value | Regulatory Status (India – FSSAI) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Saccharin** | E954 | 1879 | 300–500× | 0–5 | 0 | Permitted |
| **Aspartame** | E951 | 1965 | 180–200× | 0–40 | 4 kcal/g (negligible at use levels) | Permitted |
| **Acesulfame-K (Ace-K)** | E950 | 1967 | 200× | 0–15 | 0 | Permitted |
| **Sucralose** | E955 | 1976 | 600× | 0–15 | 0 | Permitted |
| **Neotame** | E961 | 1993 | 7,000–13,000× | 0–2 | 0 | Permitted |
| **Steviol Glycosides** | E960 | 1931 (isolated) | 200–300× | 0–4 (expressed as steviol equivalents) | 0 | Permitted |
| **Cyclamate** | E952 | 1937 | 30–50× | 0–7 | 0 | Banned in US; permitted in 130+ countries |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acesulfame-K is often used synergistically with Aspartame** (1:1 ratio) to mask each other's aftertaste — a high-yield KTU question topic.

---

### 2.2 Classification of Thickening Agents (Hydrocolloids)

Thickeners are classified based on **origin** and **chemical nature**.

#### A. Plant-Sourced Polysaccharides
- **Starch (E1400–E1450 series):** From corn, potato, tapioca. Provides high viscosity, opaque pastes.
- **Pectin (E440):** From citrus peel/apple pomace. Forms gels with sugar + acid (high-methoxyl) or with Ca²⁺ (low-methoxyl).
- **Guar Gum (E412):** From *Cyamopsis tetragonoloba* seeds. Cold-water-soluble, non-ionic.
- **Locust Bean Gum / LBG (E410):** From *Ceratonia siliqua* seeds. Synergistic with xanthan and carrageenan.
- **Gum Arabic / Acacia Gum (E414):** From *Acacia senegal*. Highly branched, low-viscosity emulsifier.

#### B. Seaweed-Sourced (Phycocolloids)
- **Alginates (E401–E404):** From brown algae (*Phaeophyceae*). Form gels with Ca²⁺ (egg-box model).
- **Carrageenan (E407):** From red algae (*Rhodophyceae*). Three types — κ (kappa), ι (iota), λ (lambda).
- **Agar (E406):** From red algae *Gelidium*. Strongest natural gel.

#### C. Microbial-Sourced
- **Xanthan Gum (E415):** From *Xanthomonas campestris* fermentation. Pseudoplastic, stable over wide pH/temperature range.
- **Gellan Gum (E418):** From *Sphingomonas elodea*. Forms clear, thermostable gels.

#### D. Animal-Sourced Proteins
- **Gelatin (E441):** From bovine/porcine collagen. Thermoreversible gel; bloom strength 50–300 g.

#### E. Cellulose Derivatives (Modified Plant)
- **Carboxymethyl Cellulose / CMC (E466), Methyl Cellulose (E461), HPMC (E464), Microcrystalline Cellulose (E460).**

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Formula / Parameter | Standard Value / Unit | Application Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sweetness Potency | $SP = \dfrac{\text{Threshold conc. of sucrose}}{\text{Threshold conc. of sweetener}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | Compares sweetness strength |
| Mass substitution | $M_{sweetener} = \dfrac{M_{sucrose}}{SP}$ | grams | Recipe reformulation |
| ADI Calculation | $ADI_{daily} = ADI \times m_{body}$ | mg/day | Safety dosage for a person |
| Dilute-solution viscosity (Einstein) | $\eta = \eta_0 (1 + 2.5 \phi)$ | Pa·s | Very dilute suspension |
| Power-law fluid model | $\tau = K \cdot \gamma^{n-1}$ | Pa | Shear-thinning (pseudoplastic) fluids (xanthan) |
| Gel point condition | $G' = G''$ (storage = loss modulus) | Pa | Sol-gel transition |
| Bloom Strength | $B = \dfrac{F_{break}}{A_{probe}}$ | g (grams) | Gel firmness (gelatin) |
| Degree of Methylation (Pectin) | $DM = \dfrac{\text{Esterified COOH}}{\text{Total COOH}} \times 100$ | % | HM-pectin > 50%, LM-pectin < 50% |
| Water binding capacity | $WBC = \dfrac{m_{water\,absorbed}}{m_{dry\,gum}}$ | g/g | Hydrocolloid hydration |
| Relative sweetness (molecular) | $R_s = \dfrac{k \cdot C_s^{n_s}}{k \cdot C_{ref}^{n_{ref}}}$ | Ratio | Hill equation model of taste |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Sweeteners:** Reformulation of diabetic foods, low-calorie beverages, tabletop sweeteners, chewing gum (xylitol), pharmaceutical syrups.
> - **Thickeners:** Ketchup (xanthan + starch), ice cream (carrageenan + LBG to prevent ice crystal growth), jams (pectin), salad dressings (xanthan for pourability), confectionery (agar/gelatin), gluten-free baking (xanthan + guar).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Sweetness Potency Calculation (Worked Example)

**Problem:** A beverage currently uses 10 g of sucrose per 100 mL. Reformulate using **sucralose** (SP = 600×) without changing the perceived sweetness.

**Step 1 — Define Sweetness Potency mathematically**

By definition:
$$SP = \dfrac{\text{Sweetness of 1 g sweetener}}{\text{Sweetness of 1 g sucrose}}$$

So, 1 g of sucralose = 600 g of sucrose in sweetening power.

**Step 2 — Apply the mass-substitution principle**

$$M_{sucralose} = \dfrac{M_{sucrose}}{SP}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values**

$$M_{sucralose} = \dfrac{10\ \text{g}}{600}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate**

$$M_{sucralose} = 0.01667\ \text{g} \approx 16.67\ \text{mg per 100 mL}$$

**Step 5 — Energy saving calculation**

$$\text{Energy saved} = M_{sucrose} \times 4\ \text{kcal/g} - M_{sucralose} \times 0\ \text{kcal/g}$$

$$\text{Energy saved} = 10 \times 4 = 40\ \text{kcal per 100 mL}$$

> **Inference:** A 250 mL can of the reformulated beverage saves **100 kcal** vs the sucrose version.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: ADI Calculation for a Human Consumer

**Problem:** A 70 kg adult consumes 2 L of beverage sweetened with **Aspartame** at 600 mg/L. Check if intake is within the **JECFA ADI of 40 mg/kg bw/day**.

**Step 1 — Daily aspartame intake (I)**

$$I = C \times V_{consumed}$$

$$I = 600\ \text{mg/L} \times 2\ \text{L} = 1200\ \text{mg/day}$$

**Step 2 — Daily intake per kg body weight**

$$I_{bw} = \dfrac{I}{m_{body}}$$

$$I_{bw} = \dfrac{1200\ \text{mg}}{70\ \text{kg}} = 17.14\ \text{mg/kg bw/day}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with ADI**

$$\text{Safety Margin} = \dfrac{ADI}{I_{bw}} = \dfrac{40}{17.14} = 2.33$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion**

Since $I_{bw} = 17.14 < 40\ \text{mg/kg bw/day}$, the intake is **SAFE** (within ADI) with a 2.33× safety margin.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Aspartame:** Contains the dipeptide L-Aspartyl-L-Phenylalanine methyl ester. **Contraindicated in Phenylketonuria (PKU) patients** because they cannot metabolize phenylalanine.

---

### 3.5 Mechanism of Thickening — Three Theoretical Steps

**Step 1 — Dispersion:** Dry hydrocolloid powder is dispersed in water; particles swell.

**Step 2 — Hydration:** Polar groups (–OH, –COO⁻, –SO₃⁻) attract water via **hydrogen bonding**; chain unfolds.

$$\text{Polymer-OH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{Polymer-OH} \cdots \text{OH}_2$$

**Step 3 — Network Formation:** Chains entangle → **viscosity increase**, or cross-link (ionic, H-bond, junction zones) → **gel formation**.

For alginate, the "**egg-box model**" describes Ca²⁺ coordination between guluronate blocks of adjacent chains:

$$\text{2 (Alg-G)}^{-} + \text{Ca}^{2+} \rightarrow \text{Alg-G} \cdot \text{Ca}^{2+} \cdot \text{G-Alg}$$
