# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Natural, Nature-Identical, and Artificial Flavors with one example each. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~120 words):**

- **Natural Flavor:** A flavoring substance obtained exclusively from plant, animal, or microbiological sources through physical, enzymatic, or microbiological processes. *Example: Vanillin extracted from cured vanilla bean pods.*
- **Nature-Identical Flavor:** A flavoring substance chemically identical to a natural flavor compound but produced synthetically (chemically or via fermentation). *Example: Synthetic vanillin produced from guaiacol via Reimer-Tiemann reaction.*
- **Artificial Flavor:** A flavoring substance that has not been identified in any natural food material and exists only through chemical synthesis. *Example: Ethyl vanillin (3-ethoxy-4-hydroxybenzaldehyde, FEMA GRAS 2464).**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Do NOT write "natural means safer." All three categories undergo identical GRAS toxicological evaluation. Marks are deducted for confusing source with safety.

---

### Question 2: What is FEMA GRAS? Why is it important in flavor regulation? **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~100 words):**

**FEMA GRAS** stands for **Flavor and Extract Manufacturers Association – Generally Recognized As Safe**. It is a self-regulatory industry panel of expert toxicologists, pharmacologists, and chemists that evaluates flavoring substances for safe use in food.

**Importance:**
1. Provides internationally recognized safety benchmarks for >3,000 flavoring compounds.
2. Each compound is assigned a unique FEMA GRAS number (e.g., Vanillin = GRAS 3107).
3. Acts as a reference list adopted by FDA, EFSA, and FSSAI for regulatory approvals.
4. Without FEMA GRAS approval, a flavor cannot legally be used in food products in the US market.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — With Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Classify the three categories of flavoring substances as per the KTU / EU regulatory framework. Compare them in terms of source, chemical identity, cost, and labeling requirements. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the industrial synthesis of **Vanillin** from guaiacol via the Reimer-Tiemann reaction. Include the balanced chemical equation, mechanism steps, and a brief mass-balance calculation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL ANSWER — Question A (Part a)

The three categories of flavoring substances defined by **EU Regulation (EC) No 1334/2008** and adopted by FSSAI (India) and KTU 2024 syllabus are:

**(i) Natural Flavouring Substances:**
Obtained by physical, enzymatic, or microbiological processes exclusively from plant, animal, or microbiological origin. *Examples: orange oil, vanilla extract, menthol from peppermint.* Labeling term: **"Natural ___ flavor"**.

**(ii) Nature-Identical Flavouring Substances:**
Chemically identical to a natural flavor compound but manufactured by chemical synthesis. *Examples: synthetic vanillin from guaiacol, synthetic benzaldehyde.* Labeling term: **"___ flavor"** (without "natural" prefix).

**(iii) Artificial Flavouring Substances:**
Chemically synthesized compounds NOT yet identified in any natural food source. *Examples: ethyl vanillin, methylcyclopentenolone.* Labeling term: **"Artificial ___ flavor"**.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Natural | Nature-Identical | Artificial |
|-----------|---------|------------------|------------|
| Source | Plant/animal/microbial | Chemical synthesis | Chemical synthesis |
| Occurs in nature? | Yes | Yes (identical to natural) | No |
| Cost index | 50x–400x | 1x | 1x–2x |
| Labeling | Natural flavor | Flavor | Artificial flavor |

*(7 marks — full marks for table + 3 categories explained)*

---

#### MODEL ANSWER — Question A (Part b)

**Step 1:** Guaiacol (2-methoxyphenol, C₇H₈O₂, MW 124.14 g/mol) is dissolved in excess aqueous NaOH to form sodium guaiacolate.

**Step 2:** Chloroform (CHCl₃) is added slowly. NaOH deprotonates CHCl₃ to generate **dichlorocarbene (:CCl₂)**, the reactive electrophile.

**Step 3:** Sodium guaiacolate attacks :CCl₂. Hydrolysis follows, introducing the **-CHO group** at the ortho position to the phenolic -OH.

**Balanced Equation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_7\text{H}_8\text{O}_2 + \text{CHCl}_3 + 4\,\text{NaOH} \longrightarrow \\
\text{C}_8\text{H}_8\text{O}_3 + 3\,\text{NaCl} + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Acidification with H₂SO₄ (pH 4–5) precipitates crude vanillin, which is filtered, washed, and recrystallized from hot water.

**Mass Balance Calculation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Theoretical Yield} &= \dfrac{1000\,\text{g guaiacol}}{124.14\,\text{g/mol}} \times 152.15\,\text{g/mol} \\
&= 1225.6\,\text{g vanillin} \\
\%
\text{Yield (industrial)} &\approx 73\% \\
\text{Actual Yield} &= 1225.6 \times 0.734 = 899.6\,\text{g vanillin}
\end{aligned}$$

*(7 marks — 2 marks for balanced equation, 2 marks for mechanism explanation, 2 marks for mass balance, 1 mark for industrial context)*

---

### QUESTION B: **[Alternative Choice — KTU Style]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Odor Activity Value (OAV)** with a suitable numerical example. Discuss how OAV is used by flavorists to design commercial flavor formulations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of labeled diagrams, describe the **classification hierarchy** of flavoring substances. Compare nature-identical vanillin with ethyl vanillin in terms of occurrence, cost, flavor strength, and regulatory labeling. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL ANSWER — Question B (Part a)

**Definition:**
The **Odor Activity Value (OAV)** is the ratio of the concentration of a flavor compound in a food matrix to its odor detection threshold (the lowest concentration at which 50% of a sensory panel can perceive the aroma).

$$\boxed{OAV = \dfrac{C_{\text{compound}}}{OT_{\text{compound}}}}$$

Where $C_{\text{compound}}$ = concentration of compound in food, and $OT_{\text{compound}}$ = odor threshold in the same matrix.

**Numerical Example:**
Suppose a mango-flavored beverage contains 12 mg/L of ethyl butyrate, and its odor threshold in water is 0.4 µg/L.

$$\begin{aligned}
C &= 12\,\text{mg/L} = 12{,}000\,\mu\text{g/L} \\
OT &= 0.4\,\mu\text{g/L} \\
OAV &= \dfrac{12{,}000}{0.4} = 30{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

Since **OAV ≫ 1**, ethyl butyrate is a **major aroma-active compound** in this product.

**Application by Flavorists:**
- Compounds with **OAV ≥ 1** are called **aroma-active** and contribute directly to the perceived flavor profile.
- Compounds with **OAV < 1** are **sub-threshold** but may act as **flavor enhancers** or **modifiers** through synergy.
- Commercial flavor formulations target an OAV spectrum that mimics the natural reference (e.g., fresh mango) within a ±20% tolerance.

*(7 marks — 2 for definition, 2 for numerical example, 3 for industrial application)*

---

#### MODEL ANSWER — Question B (Part b)

**Classification Hierarchy (Refer to Mermaid Diagram in Section 4.1):**

Flavoring substances are hierarchically divided into:
1. **Natural Flavors** → Essential oils, Plant extracts, Oleoresins, Process flavors, Hydrolysates.
2. **Nature-Identical Flavors** → Synthetic aromatic chemicals, Biotech/fermentation-derived, Enzyme-catalyzed, Non-food natural isolates.
3. **Artificial Flavors** → Novel synthetic molecules, Modified natural templates, Combinatorial chemistry designs.

**Comparison Table: Nature-Identical Vanillin vs. Ethyl Vanillin**

| Parameter | Nature-Identical Vanillin | Ethyl Vanillin |
|-----------|---------------------------|----------------|
| Chemical Name | 4-Hydroxy-3-methoxybenzaldehyde | 3-Ethoxy-4-hydroxybenzaldehyde |
| Molecular Formula | C₈H₈O₃ | C₉H₁₀O₃ |
| Molecular Weight | 152.15 g/mol | 166.18 g/mol |
| Occurs in Nature? | Yes (in vanilla beans) | No (synthetic only) |
| FEMA GRAS No. | 3107 | 2464 |
| Flavor Strength | 1x (reference) | ~2.0x–2.5x stronger |
| Cost | USD 10–15/kg | USD 12–18/kg (more cost-effective per unit flavor) |
| Regulatory Label | "Natural flavor" or "Flavor" | "Artificial flavor" |

*(7 marks — 3 for hierarchy + 4 for comparison)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Marks-Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Confusing "natural" with "safe"** — All three categories are equally regulated for safety. (Lose 1–2 marks)
> 2. **Writing "natural means healthier"** — There is NO scientific basis; consumer perception ≠ regulatory status.
> 3. **Forgetting the balanced equation** in synthesis questions. Always write mass-balanced chemical equations with state symbols.
> 4. **Wrong units** — Convert mg/L to µg/L BEFORE calculating OAV, or lose 2 marks.
> 5. **Not mentioning FEMA GRAS** — A compulsory regulatory term in every Module 3 answer.
> 6. **Missing the source-of-origin clause** — Natural vs. Nature-Identical differs ONLY in source, NOT chemistry.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam Day:**

- ✅ The **three categories** of flavoring substances are Natural, Nature-Identical, and Artificial — defined by **source**, NOT chemical structure.
- ✅ **Vanillin** = Nature-identical (FEMA GRAS 3107, MW 152.15, C₈H₈O₃).
- ✅ **Ethyl Vanillin** = Artificial (FEMA GRAS 2464, MW 166.18, C₉H₁₀O₃, ~2x stronger than vanillin).
- ✅ **FEMA GRAS** is the master reference list with >3,000 approved flavor compounds.
- ✅ **EU Regulation (EC) No 1334/2008** is the most rigorous legal classification framework.
- ✅ The **Reimer-Tiemann reaction** is the standard industrial route for synthetic vanillin from guaiacol.
- ✅ **OAV > 1** → aroma-active compound; **OAV < 1** → sub-threshold / flavor enhancer.
- ✅ **Cost ratio:** Natural vanilla extract is **100x–400x** costlier than synthetic vanillin.
- ✅ Nature-identical compounds are **chemically, spectroscopically, and sensorially identical** to their natural counterparts.
- ✅ All three categories undergo **identical toxicological evaluation** — natural ≠ safer.
- ✅ Industrial biotechnological routes (e.g., *Pseudomonas putida* on ferulic acid) are gaining traction as "**natural-equivalent**" production routes.
- ✅ For labeling: **"Natural flavor"** = from natural source; **"Flavor"** = nature-identical; **"Artificial flavor"** = not found in nature.
- ✅ The food industry's **cost-saving driver** is the shift from natural → nature-identical → artificial, without compromising sensory quality.
- ✅ Detection threshold (DT) and Odor Activity Value (OAV) are the **two most important numerical concepts** for KTU Module 3 calculations.
- ✅ Module 3 carries **15–20%** weightage in the KTU 2024 End Semester Examination for PBFTT504.

---

**End of Premium Notes — Module 3: Natural Identical and Artificial Flavour**
*Prepared in alignment with KTU 2024 NEP-aligned Outcome-Based Education framework.*

topics.title: Methods of Flavour Isolation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBFTT504 - FOOD ADDITIVES AND FLAVORINGS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Methods of Flavour Isolation # MODULE 3: METHODS OF FLAVOUR ISOLATION ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PBFTT504 / Module 3):** > **Flavour Isolation** is defined as the systematic, physico-chemical separation of volatile and non-volatile aromatic constituents (flavour-active compounds) from a complex food or biological matrix using selective techniques such as distillation, solvent extraction, supercritical fluid extraction, and headspace trapping, while preserving their native organoleptic integrity for subsequent analytical or industrial use. In the language of the KTU 2024 Scheme, flavour isolation forms the **upstream bridge** between raw agricultural/biotechnological produce and downstream flavour formulation. Without isolation, we cannot identify, quantify, or recreate the sensory profile of a food product. ### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Coffee Brewing" Analogy > [!NOTE] > Imagine you walk into a kitchen where the chef has freshly ground coffee beans. The delightful smell of coffee is essentially **volatiles escaping** from the bean matrix. To **isolate** those smell molecules, you pour hot water (a solvent) over them — water dissolves some, carries them up as steam, and you collect the aromatic condensate in a cup. Flavour isolation in the food industry works on the **exact same principle**, just with much more sophisticated solvents (supercritical CO₂, ethanol, hexane), temperatures, and pressures. The only difference is the **scale, precision, and selectivity** — instead of one cup of coffee, a flavour chemist in Kerala's spice industry may isolate vanillin from 1,000 kg of cured vanilla beans using **ethanol reflux extraction** to capture every microgram of the active molecule. ### 1.2 Why Is Isolation Necessary? - **Concentration**: Flavours exist in ppm–ppb ranges; isolation concentrates them by **100×–100,000×**. - **Identification**: Gas Chromatography-Mass Spectrometry (GC-MS) requires purified isolates. - **Stability**: Isolated flavours are easier to standardize and microencapsulate. - **Commercial Production**: Natural flavours (e.g., cardamom oil, pepper oleoresin) are sold globally as isolates. - **Standard Mark**: Industry yield is benchmarked at **recovery >85%** for most volatile isolation methods. ### 1.3 Classification of Flavour Isolation Methods > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU 2024 syllabus groups flavour isolation into **Classical (Conventional)** and **Modern (Advanced)** techniques: | Category | Methods Covered | Principle | |---|---|---| | **Classical** | Steam Distillation, Hydrodistillation, Solvent Extraction, Expression, Enfleurage | Thermal or solubility-based separation | | **Modern** | Supercritical Fluid Extraction (SCFE), Molecular Distillation, Headspace (HS), SPME, SDE | Pressure, vacuum, or sorption-based separation | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classical Isolation Methods — Detailed Logic ### 2.1.1 Steam Distillation (SD) **Principle**: Co-distillation of volatile flavour compounds with steam at temperatures **below 100 °C**, exploiting the fact that immiscible liquids depress each other's partial pressure. **Step-by-Step Logic**: - The flavour matrix (spices, herbs, peels) is placed in a still. - Saturated steam is injected or generated in situ. - Volatile aroma molecules (terpenes, aldehydes, esters) co-distil with steam. - The vapour mixture is condensed; the aqueous and organic layers separate in a **Florentine flask** (because water and oil are immiscible). - The essential oil is decanted and dried over anhydrous **Na₂SO₄**. **Why It Works**: Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures for immiscible systems ensures the total vapour pressure exceeds atmospheric pressure at temperatures **far below the boiling point** of the individual flavour compounds. > [!NOTE] > **Industrial Example**: Production of **Kerala cardamom oil** (Elettaria cardamomum) — yields of **6–8% v/w** by steam distillation. ### 2.1.2 Hydrodistillation (HD) **Principle**: Direct immersion of the plant matrix in boiling water. - Simpler than SD (no external steam generator). - Risk: **hydrolysis** of sensitive esters (e.g., linalyl acetate in lavender) due to prolonged contact with hot water. - Used widely for **rose water** and **kewra water** production. ### 2.1.3 Solvent Extraction (Liquid–Liquid & Solid–Liquid) **Principle**: Partitioning of flavour compounds between two immiscible phases governed by the **Nernst Distribution Law**. **Operational Steps**: - **Solid-Liquid**: Plant tissue is macerated and contacted with a non-polar solvent (hexane, dichloromethane, petroleum ether). - **Liquid-Liquid**: An aqueous flavour broth is shaken with an organic solvent in a **separating funnel**. - The solvent is evaporated under vacuum → **concrete** (wax-containing) or **absolute** (alcohol-washed) extracts. **Solvent Selection Criteria**: Polarity match, boiling point (low for easy removal), non-toxicity (food-grade: ethanol, ethyl acetate). ### 2.1.4 Cold Press Expression **Principle**: Mechanical rupture of oil glands in citrus peels by pressing or scraping. - **No heat applied** → preserves heat-labile compounds (no terpenoid degradation). - Used for **orange, lemon, bergamot** peel oils. - Yield is lower (≈0.5% v/w) but the **olfactive quality is superior**. ### 2.1.5 Enfleurage **Principle**: Absorption of delicate floral volatiles into a **purified, odourless fat** (lard or beef tallow) at room temperature. - Used historically for **jasmine, tuberose, gardenia** — flowers that continue to emit fragrance even after harvesting. - The fat saturated with volatiles = **"pomade"**, which is then washed with alcohol to yield the **"absolute"**. - **Time-consuming** (3–7 days) — now largely replaced by SCFE and headspace techniques. ## 2.2 Modern Isolation Methods ### 2.2.1 Supercritical Fluid Extraction (SCFE) **Principle**: A fluid above its **critical temperature (Tc)** and **critical pressure (Pc)** behaves as a hybrid — gas-like diffusivity + liquid-like solvating power. - **CO₂ is the solvent of choice**: Tc = **31.1 °C**, Pc = **73.8 bar**. - Selective extraction by tuning pressure (40–300 bar). - **Supercritical CO₂** is gaseous at ambient conditions → solvent-free extract! ### 2.2.2 Molecular Distillation (Short-Path Distillation) **Principle**: Separation under **high vacuum (10⁻³ to 10⁻⁶ mbar)** where the mean free path of molecules equals the evaporator-to-condenser distance. - Used for **heat-sensitive, high-boiling** flavour molecules (e.g., lactones, sesquiterpenes, omega-3 esters). - Operating temperature reduced by **150–200 °C** compared to conventional distillation. ### 2.2.3 Headspace Analysis (Static & Dynamic) **Principle**: Analysis of volatiles in the gas phase **above** a food sample without disturbing the matrix. - **Static HS**: Sample sealed in a vial; volatiles equilibrate and an aliquot injected. - **Dynamic HS (Purge-and-Trap)**: Inert gas sweeps volatiles onto a sorbent trap (Tenax, Porapak Q); trapped compounds are thermally desorbed. ### 2.2.4 Solid-Phase Microextraction (SPME) **Principle**: A fused-silica fibre coated with a polymeric stationary phase (**PDMS, DVB, CAR**) absorbs/adsorbs analytes from the sample. - **Solvent-free**, fast, sensitive (detection limits at **ng/L** level). - Compatible with **GC-MS** direct injection. ### 2.2.5 Simultaneous Distillation–Extraction (SDE — Likens–Nickerson) **Principle**: Steam distillation of the sample occurs concurrently with solvent extraction in a single apparatus. - Combines the advantages of both techniques. - Excellent for **trace aroma compounds** in complex matrices (wine, coffee). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table consolidates **all critical equations** required for KTU 2024 ESE numericals and derivations on flavour isolation. | # | Equation / Relationship | Description | Variables & Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $K_d = \dfrac{C_{\text{org}}}{C_{\text{aq}}}$ | Nernst Distribution Coefficient | $C_{\text{org}}$ = solute conc. in organic phase; $C_{\text{aq}}$ = conc. in aqueous phase | | 2 | $E\,\% = \dfrac{C_{\text{org}}\,V_{\text{org}}}{C_{\text{org}}\,V_{\text{org}} + C_{\text{aq}}\,V_{\text{aq}}} \times 100$ | Extraction Efficiency (%) | $V$ = phase volume (mL or L) | | 3 | $R\,\% = \dfrac{\text{Mass of isolated flavour}}{\text{Total flavour in raw matrix}} \times 100$ | Recovery Percentage | Mass in g or kg | | 4 | $P_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{water}}^{\circ} + P_{\text{oil}}^{\circ}$ | Dalton's Law (immiscible mixture) | $P$ in kPa or mmHg | | 5 | $\dfrac{n_{\text{water}}}{n_{\text{oil}}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{water}}^{\circ} \cdot M_{\text{oil}}}{P_{\text{oil}}^{\circ} \cdot M_{\text{water}}}$ | Steam Distillate Composition | $n$ = moles, $M$ = molar mass (g/mol) | | 6 | $T_b^{\text{mixture}} < T_b^{\text{oil}}$ | Boiling point depression rule | $T_b$ in °C or K | | 7 | $\eta_{\text{SCFE}} = f(P,\,T,\, \rho_{\text{CO}_2})$ | SCFE Solvent Power (density-driven) | $P$ in bar, $\rho$ in g/cm³ | | 8 | $\text{LLE Recovery} = 1 - \left(\dfrac{V_{\text{aq}}}{V_{\text{aq}} + K_d V_{\text{org}}}\right)^n$ | Multi-stage LLE (n extractions) | $n$ = number of extractions | | 9 | $\lambda = \dfrac{h}{P \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{2\pi m k_B T}}$ | Mean Free Path (molecular distillation) | $h$ = Planck const., $k_B$ = Boltzmann const. | | 10 | $t_{\text{eq}} \approx \dfrac{r^2}{D_s}$ | SPME Equilibrium Time | $r$ = fibre radius, $D_s$ = diffusion coeff. | > [!WARNING] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE FOR KTU**: When writing absolute value expressions or distribution ratios in table cells, always use $\vert$ or $\mid$ (e.g., $C_{\text{org}}\vert V_{\text{org}}$) — **never** the raw vertical pipe $\vert$ inside a markdown table cell, as it breaks table parsing. ## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields > [!NOTE] > - **Food Engineering**: SCFE is the **gold standard** for decaffeinated coffee and hop extract (beer industry). > - **Pharmaceutical**: Enantiomer isolation via molecular distillation for chiral APIs. > - **Cosmetic**: Enfleurage absolutes for high-end perfumery. > - **Kerala Spice Board**: Steam distillation units in **Idukki and Wayanad** for cardamom, clove, nutmeg. > - **Environmental**: Headspace–GC used to monitor off-flavours in packaged drinking water. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Dalton's Law Applied to Steam Distillation of Essential Oils ### Statement For an immiscible mixture of water and a high-boiling essential oil, the total vapour pressure equals the sum of the individual vapour pressures at the same temperature. This explains why essential oils **distil far below their atmospheric boiling point**. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1**: Start with Raoult's Law for two immiscible liquids A (water) and B (oil). Since they are immiscible, each component escapes independently, behaving as if the other is absent: $$P_A = X_A \cdot P_A^{\circ} \quad \text{and} \quad P_B = X_B \cdot P_B^{\circ}$$ Because the two liquids are essentially insoluble in each other in the liquid phase, we have $X_A + X_B \to 1$ as each approaches purity in its own phase. **Step 2**: Apply Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures to the vapour phase: $$P_{\text{total}} = P_A + P_B$$ **Step 3**: Substituting and noting that each pure component contributes its saturation vapour pressure: $$P_{\text{total}} = P_A^{\circ} + P_B^{\circ}$$ **Step 4**: The mixture boils when $P_{\text{total}}$ equals atmospheric pressure $P_{\text{atm}}$. Therefore: $$P_A^{\circ} + P_B^{\circ} = P_{\text{atm}}$$ **Step 5**: For water at 100 °C, $P_{\text{water}}^{\circ} = 760$ mmHg. If the oil has $P_{\text{oil}}^{\circ} = 50$ mmHg at 100 °C, then it distils along with water even though its own boiling point may be **>250 °C**. $$T_b^{\text{mixture}} \ll T_b^{\text{oil}}$$ **Final Result**: $$\boxed{P_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{water}}^{\circ}(T) + P_{\text{oil}}^{\circ}(T) = P_{\text{atm}}}$$ This is the **thermodynamic backbone** of every industrial steam distillation unit in Kerala's spice processing hubs. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Extraction Efficiency in Single-Stage Liquid–Liquid Extraction ### Statement Derive the equation for the percentage of solute recovered from an aqueous phase into an organic phase in a single extraction step. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1**: Define the distribution coefficient (Nernst's Law): $$K_d = \dfrac{C_{\text{org}}}{C_{\text{aq}}}$$ **Step 2**: Let initial mass of solute = $m_0$ (in aqueous phase, volume $V_{\text{aq}}$). After extraction with volume $V_{\text{org}}$, let mass remaining in aqueous = $m_1$. **Step 3**: Mass balance — mass recovered in organic phase = $m_0 - m_1$: $$m_0 - m_1 = C_{\text{org}} \cdot V_{\text{org}}$$ **Step 4**: Express concentrations: $$C_{\text{aq}} = \dfrac{m_1}{V_{\text{aq}}}, \qquad C_{\text{org}} = \dfrac{m_0 - m_1}{V_{\text{org}}}$$ **Step 5**: Substitute into $K_d$: $$K_d = \dfrac{(m_0 - m_1)/V_{\text{org}}}{m_1/V_{\text{aq}}}$$ **Step 6**: Solve for $m_1$: $$K_d \, V_{\text{aq}} \, m_1 = V_{\text{aq}} \, (m_0 - m_1)$$ $$m_1 (K_d V_{\text{org}} + V_{\text{aq}}) = m_0 V_{\text{aq}}$$ $$m_1 = \dfrac{m_0 V_{\text{aq}}}{K_d V_{\text{org}} + V_{\text{aq}}}$$ **Step 7**: The fraction extracted = $1 - \dfrac{m_1}{m_0}$: $$\dfrac{m_0 - m_1}{m_0} = 1 - \dfrac{V_{\text{aq}}}{K_d V_{\text{org}} + V_{\text{aq}}}$$ $$\boxed{E\,\% = \left[1 - \dfrac{V_{\text{aq}}}{K_d V_{\text{org}} + V_{\text{aq}}}\right] \times 100}$$ **Engineering Insight**: A larger $K_d$ (more solute-soluble in organic phase) and larger $V_{\text{org}}$ increase extraction efficiency. This justifies the practice of **multiple small-volume extractions** rather than one large one — proven by the **multi-stage recovery formula**: $$\text{Total Recovery} = 1 - \left(\dfrac{V_{\text{aq}}}{V_{\text{aq}} + K_d V_{\text{org}}}\right)^n$$ where $n$ = number of extraction stages. This is highly testable in KTU ESE. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Composition of Steam Distillate ### Statement Determine the mass ratio of oil to water in the condensate during steam distillation. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1**: In the vapour phase, mole fractions are governed by partial pressures: $$y_{\text{water}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{water}}^{\circ}}{P_{\text{total}}}, \qquad y_{\text{oil}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{oil}}^{\circ}}{P_{\text{total}}}$$ **Step 2**: Ratio of moles in vapour: $$\dfrac{n_{\text{water}}}{n_{\text{oil}}} = \dfrac{y_{\text{water}}}{y_{\text{oil}}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{water}}^{\circ}}{P_{\text{oil}}^{\circ}}$$ **Step 3**: Convert mole ratio to mass ratio using $m = n \cdot M$: $$\dfrac{m_{\text{water}}}{m_{\text{oil}}} = \dfrac{n_{\text{water}} \cdot M_{\text{water}}}{n_{\text{oil}} \cdot M_{\text{oil}}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{water}}^{\circ} \cdot M_{\text{water}}}{P_{\text{oil}}^{\circ} \cdot M_{\text{oil}}}$$ **Step 4**: Solve for mass of oil obtained per kg of water condensed: $$\boxed{m_{\text{oil}} = m_{\text{water}} \cdot \dfrac{P_{\text{oil}}^{\circ} \cdot M_{\text{oil}}}{P_{\text{water}}^{\circ} \cdot M_{\text{water}}}}$$ **Numerical Example**: Distil lemongrass oil ($M = 154$ g/mol) at 100 °C, where $P_{\text{oil}}^{\circ} = 25$ mmHg. For 1 kg water condensed ($M = 18$ g/mol): $$m_{\text{oil}} = 1000 \times \dfrac{25 \times 154}{760 \times 18} \approx 281.5 \text{ g of oil}$$ This matches industrial yields from **citronella processing plants in Kannur, Kerala**. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Isolation Methods | Method | Working Principle | Temp. Range | Pressure | Solvent | Recovery (%) | Best For | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Steam Distillation | Co-distillation with steam | 90–100 °C | 1 atm | Water | 80–95 | Citrus, spices | | Hydrodistillation | Direct boiling in water | 100 °C | 1 atm | Water | 70–90 | Rose, kewra | | Solvent Extraction | Partition by solubility | 25–80 °C | 1 atm | Hexane, ethanol | 85–98 | Oleoresins, absolutes | | Cold Press Expression | Mechanical rupture | 20–25 °C | 1 atm | None | 60–75 | Citrus peels | | Enfleurage | Fat absorption | 20–25 °C | 1 atm | Lard/tallow | 90–95 | Jasmine, tuberose | | SCFE | Supercritical solvation | 35–60 °C | 80–300 bar | CO₂ | 92–99 | Decaf, hop, paprika | | Molecular Distillation | High-vacuum short path | 50–200 °C | 10⁻³–10⁻⁶ mbar | None | 75–90 | Lactones, DHA | | Headspace (Dynamic) | Volatile trapping | 25–80 °C | 1 atm | None (trap) | Qualitative | Aroma profiling | | SPME | Fibre sorption | 25–80 °C | 1 atm | None (fibre) | 95+ | Trace analysis | | SDE (Likens–Nickerson) | Concurrent SD + LLE | 100 °C | 1 atm | Pentane, ether | 85–95 | Wine, coffee | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Steam Distillation Plant ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Plant Matrix
Spices or Herbs] --> B[Steam Generator
Boiler Unit] B --> C[Saturated Steam
Inlet] A --> D[Distillation Vessel

Steam plus Volatiles] E --> F[Condenser
Liebig Coil] F --> G[Decanter or
Florentine Flask] G --> H[Aqueous Layer
Hydrosol] G --> I[Organic Layer

Anhydrous Na2SO4] J --> K[Filtered Pure
Essential Oil] H --> L[Recycle to
Boiler] classDef process fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px classDef output fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px classDef utility fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px class A,D,F,G process class I,J,K output class B,C,H,L utility ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Supercritical Fluid Extraction (SCFE) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PUMP_LOOP [High Pressure Circuit] A1[Liquid CO2 Cylinder] --> B1[Cooling Unit

Defatted Solid] classDef equipment fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px classDef product fill:#dcedc8,stroke:#33691e,stroke-width:2px classDef waste fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px class A1,B1,C1,D1,E1,F1,G1,H1 equipment class P1,P2 product class S1 waste ``` ## 4.3 Decision Tree — Selection of Flavour Isolation Method ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Start: Raw Matrix
and Target Flavour] --> Q1{Thermolabile

or Dynamic HS] Q2 -- No --> SCFE[Supercritical CO2

or SCFE] classDef decision fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px classDef process fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px classDef start fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f,stroke-width:2px class START start class Q1,Q2,Q3,Q4 decision class SD,HS,SCFE,SDE,CP,ENF process ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram — Likens-Nickerson SDE Apparatus ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph DISTILL_SIDE [Distillation Limb] M1[Sample Flask

KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > These questions are calibrated to KTU ESE Part-A pattern: **direct recall, definitions, or short derivations of 2–4 lines**. --- ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define flavour isolation. List any four classical methods used for isolating flavours from food matrices.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Flavour isolation is the **systematic separation of volatile and non-volatile aroma compounds** from a complex food matrix while preserving their chemical identity and organoleptic properties for analytical characterization or industrial formulation (1 mark). Four classical methods (2 marks — ½ mark each): 1. **Steam Distillation** — co-distillation with saturated steam. 2. **Hydrodistillation** — direct boiling of matrix in water. 3. **Solvent Extraction** — partitioning using organic solvents (hexane, ethanol). 4. **Cold Press Expression** — mechanical rupture of oil glands in citrus peels. *(Acceptable alternatives: Enfleurage, Maceration)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **State and explain the principle of simultaneous distillation–extraction (SDE). Mention its main advantage.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Principle (2 marks):** SDE, also called the **Likens-Nickerson apparatus**, combines steam distillation of the aqueous sample with continuous solvent extraction of the rising vapours in a single closed apparatus. Volatile flavour compounds are co-distilled with steam on one limb and simultaneously extracted into a boiling organic solvent (pentane/diethyl ether) on the other limb. The mixed vapours condense together, the organic and aqueous phases separate by density difference, and the organic phase (now containing the flavour isolate) is recycled for further extraction. **Main Advantage (1 mark):** The method provides **high recovery (>85%) of trace aroma compounds** with minimal artefact formation because the residence time at high temperature is short and the solvent continuously removes volatiles from the aqueous phase. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > Following the **KTU ESE Part B Internal Choice pattern**, two full alternative questions are given. Each question has sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a) Describe the principle, working, and industrial applications of Supercritical Fluid Extraction (SCFE) for flavour isolation. Highlight why supercritical CO₂ is preferred over conventional solvents.** *(7 Marks)* #### **Model Solution** **(i) Principle (2 marks):** A fluid maintained **above its critical temperature (Tc) and critical pressure (Pc)** exists in a supercritical state — exhibiting **liquid-like density and solvating power** along with **gas-like diffusivity and viscosity**. This hybrid character makes supercritical fluids **exceptional solvents** for selective extraction of flavour compounds from solid or semi-solid food matrices. **(ii) Working (3 marks):** - The plant/herbal matrix is loaded into a **high-pressure extraction vessel**. - Liquid CO₂ is cooled below its Tc (31.1 °C), pressurized by a **high-pressure pump to 80–300 bar**, and heated to 35–60 °C to attain supercritical state. - Supercritical CO₂ penetrates the matrix, dissolves the target flavour compounds. - The loaded fluid passes through a **series of separators** where stepwise pressure reduction causes CO₂ to revert to gas, **precipitating the extracted compounds**. - CO₂ gas is **recycled**; the extracted flavours are collected in fractionated form. **(iii) Why CO₂ is preferred (1 mark):** - Tc = **31.1 °C**, Pc = **73.8 bar** → mild, food-safe conditions. - **Non-toxic, non-flammable, GRAS status**. - **Solvent-free product** (CO₂ evaporates completely). - Tunable selectivity by pressure programming. **(iv) Industrial Applications (1 mark):** - Decaffeination of coffee and tea. - Extraction of **hops** for brewing (alpha-acids, essential oils). - **Spice oleoresins**: cardamom, pepper, ginger (Kerala industries). - Nutraceuticals: lycopene, beta-carotene. --- **(b) With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a steam distillation unit. State Dalton's law as applied to immiscible mixtures and discuss why essential oils distil below 100 °C.** *(7 Marks)* #### **Model Solution** **(i) Block Diagram (2 marks):** A neatly labelled schematic showing: **Plant Matrix → Distillation Vessel ← Steam Generator → Vapour Mixture → Condenser → Florentine Flask (Separator) → Essential Oil (organic layer) + Hydrosol (aqueous layer) → Drying over Na₂SO₄ → Pure Oil**. **(ii) Working (2 marks):** - The spice/herb matrix is packed in the distillation vessel. - Saturated steam is injected from the boiler, permeates through the matrix. - Volatile flavour compounds (essential oils) co-distil with steam because the **total vapour pressure of the immiscible mixture exceeds atmospheric pressure at temperatures below 100 °C**. - The mixed vapours enter the condenser, are cooled, and collected in the **Florentine flask** where aqueous and oily layers separate by gravity. **(iii) Dalton's Law Application (2 marks):** $$P_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{water}}^{\circ} + P_{\text{oil}}^{\circ}$$ For an immiscible mixture, both components contribute their full saturation vapour pressures. The mixture boils when $P_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{atm}} = 760$ mmHg. **(iv) Reasoning — why below 100 °C (1 mark):** Even at 95 °C, $P_{\text{water}}^{\circ} \approx 640$ mmHg. If the oil exerts $P_{\text{oil}}^{\circ} \approx 120$ mmHg at 95 °C, then $P_{\text{total}} = 760$ mmHg → the mixture boils at 95 °C, **far below the individual boiling point of the oil (typically 200–300 °C)**. This protects heat-sensitive aroma compounds. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme Alternative]** **(a) Explain the Nernst Distribution Law. Derive an expression for the extraction efficiency when a single solute is partitioned between two immiscible liquids. Comment on why multiple extractions are more efficient than one.** *(7 Marks)* #### **Model Solution** **(i) Nernst Distribution Law (2 marks):** When a solute is distributed between two immiscible solvents at constant temperature, it attains a constant ratio of concentrations, provided the **solute exists in the same molecular form** in both phases: $$K_d = \dfrac{C_{\text{org}}}{C_{\text{aq}}} = \text{constant at fixed } T$$ **(ii) Derivation (3 marks):** *(See Section 3.2 of these notes for the full derivation)* Starting from $K_d = \dfrac{C_{\text{org}}}{C_{\text{aq}}}$, mass balance $m_0 = m_1 + m_{\text{org}}$, and definitions of concentration, we obtain: $$\boxed{E\,\% = \left[1 - \dfrac{V_{\text{aq}}}{K_d V_{\text{org}} + V_{\text{aq}}}\right] \times 100}$$ For $n$ successive extractions each using fresh volume $V_{\text{org}}$: $$\text{Total Recovery} = 1 - \left(\dfrac{V_{\text{aq}}}{V_{\text{aq}} + K_d V_{\text{org}}}\right)^n$$ **(iii) Why multiple extractions are better (2 marks):** Mathematically, the unrecovered fraction after $n$ extractions is raised to the power $n$. For example, if single extraction leaves 20% of solute behind ($V_{\text{aq}}/V_{\text{aq}}+K_d V_{\text{org}} = 0.2$), then three successive extractions reduce unrecovered solute to $(0.2)^3 = 0.008 = 0.8\%$. Hence **three small extractions recover 99.2%**, whereas one extraction recovers only 80%. This principle is fundamental in industrial-scale solvent extraction of oleoresins. --- **(b) Compare classical and modern methods of flavour isolation with specific examples. Discuss the role of headspace techniques in flavour research.** *(7 Marks)* #### **Model Solution** **(i) Comparison Table (3 marks):** | Feature | Classical Methods | Modern Methods | |---|---|---| | Energy input | High (heat-based) | Low to moderate | | Solvent residue | Possible (organic solvents) | Mostly none (SCFE, HS) | | Selectivity | Limited | High (tunable) | | Capital cost | Low | High | | Examples | Steam distillation, enfleurage, solvent extraction | SCFE, SPME, molecular distillation, HS | **(ii) Specific Examples (2 marks):** - Classical: **Steam distillation of cardamom** (Kerala, Idukki) → 6–8% v/w oil recovery. - Modern: **SCFE of hops** (Germany, USA) using CO₂ at 250 bar, 50 °C → solvent-free hop extract. **(iii) Role of Headspace Techniques (2 marks):** - **Static Headspace**: Direct injection of equilibrium gas above the sample → best for **highly volatile compounds** (e.g., ethanol in wine). - **Dynamic Headspace (Purge-and-Trap)**: Continuous sweeping of volatiles onto a sorbent (Tenax TA) → enables **trace-level detection (ppb)** of off-flavours, oxidation markers. - **SPME**: Solvent-free fibre extraction → the **workhorse of modern aroma research**, ideal for **GC-MS coupling** in flavoromics. - Headspace techniques are **non-destructive** — the original food sample remains intact, making them ideal for quality control of finished products. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Flavour Isolation Questions** > > 1. **Confusing steam distillation with hydrodistillation**: Students frequently write "steam distillation" when the question asks about hydrodistillation. **Steam distillation** uses **external generated steam**; **hydrodistillation** boils the matrix in direct contact with water. Examiners deduct ½–1 mark for this mix-up. > 2. **Forgetting units**: Always state $P$ in mmHg or kPa, $V$ in mL or L, $K_d$ as dimensionless. A formula without units = lost mark. > 3. **Incorrect Dalton's Law statement**: Students often write $P_{\text{total}} = P_A \cdot X_A + P_B \cdot X_B$ (Raoult's for miscible). For immiscible, it is the **sum of pure vapour pressures**, NOT mole-fraction weighted. > 4. **Skipping the recycling line in SCFE diagrams**: Examiners specifically look for the **CO₂ recycle loop**. Missing arrow = -1 mark. > 5. **No mention of GRAS status**: When justifying CO₂ as solvent, write "GRAS — Generally Recognized As Safe". Skipping this loses ½ mark. > 6. **Skipping the "n" in multi-stage extraction**: Always specify the number of extraction stages in derivations. > 7. **Drawing Clevenger apparatus by hand**: KTU prefers **functional block diagrams** (flowcharts) over physical drawings which are often illegible. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-DENSITY SUMMARY** — Revise these points before every KTU ESE attempt on Module 3. ### 🔑 Core Definitions to Memorize - **Flavour Isolation**: Separation of aroma compounds from food matrix without altering chemistry. - **Nernst Distribution Coefficient ($K_d$)**: Ratio of solute concentration in two immiscible solvents. - **Supercritical Fluid**: Substance above its Tc and Pc, exhibiting dual liquid-gas behaviour. - **Dalton's Law (Immiscible)**: $P_{\text{total}} = P_A^{\circ} + P_B^{\circ}$ — sum of pure vapour pressures. - **Enfleurage**: Cold fat absorption for delicate floral volatiles. - **Pomade**: Fat saturated with absorbed floral volatiles. - **Absolute**: Alcohol-soluble, wax-free solvent extract. - **Concrete**: Waxy, semi-solid initial solvent extract. ### 🧪 Methods — Quick Identifier - **High-pressure work** → SCFE. - **High-vacuum work** → Molecular Distillation. - **Fat absorption at 25 °C** → Enfleurage. - **Mechanical citrus peel rupture** → Cold Press. - **Likens-Nickerson apparatus** → SDE. - **Fibre sorption + GC-MS** → SPME. - **Two limbs, mixed vapours, recycling solvent** → SDE. ### 📐 Equations to Memorize 1. $K_d = \dfrac{C_{\text{org}}}{C_{\text{aq}}}$ 2. $E\,\% = \left[1 - \dfrac{V_{\text{aq}}}{K_d V_{\text{org}} + V_{\text{aq}}}\right] \times 100$ 3. Multi-stage: $\text{Recovery} = 1 - \left(\dfrac{V_{\text{aq}}}{V_{\text{aq}} + K_d V_{\text{org}}}\right)^n$ 4. $P_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{water}}^{\circ} + P_{\text{oil}}^{\circ}$ 5. $\dfrac{m_{\text{oil}}}{m_{\text{water}}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{oil}}^{\circ} \cdot M_{\text{oil}}}{P_{\text{water}}^{\circ} \cdot M_{\text{water}}}$ ### 🌿 Real-World Connect (Kerala Context) - **Cardamom oil** (Idukki) → Steam distillation. - **Pepper oleoresin** → Solvent extraction with ethanol/acetone. - **Lemongrass oil** (Kannur) → Steam distillation, Citral-rich. - **Clove oil** → Hydrodistillation, eugenol recovery. - **Cocoa flavour** → SCFE for fat-and-aroma fractionation. ### ⚠️ High-Yield Comparison Points (Likely to Appear in ESE) - SCFE vs Solvent Extraction → residue, energy, selectivity. - Steam vs Hydrodistillation → contact, hydrolysis, application. - Static vs Dynamic Headspace → sensitivity, instrumentation. - Molecular vs Conventional Distillation → vacuum, thermal damage. ### 📊 Numerical Problem Pattern - Always: write formula → substitute values → state final numerical answer with units. - Show all intermediate steps. - Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ for any absolute value expression in tables.

topics.title: Different Flavour Products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT504 - FOOD ADDITIVES AND FLAVORINGS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Different Flavour Products

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Flavour Products (KTU 2024 Definition):** Flavour products are *engineered, standardized, and commercially manufactured aromatic preparations* — either single-compound isolates or complex multi-component blends — designed to impart, modify, enhance, or mask the organoleptic (taste + aroma) profile of a food system during processing, storage, or final consumption.

According to the **Codex Alimentarius (CAC/GL 66-2008)** and **FEMA (Flavor and Extract Manufacturers Association)** classifications adopted in the KTU 2024 Food Additives syllabus, a *flavour product* is functionally distinct from a *food ingredient*. A food ingredient provides bulk, calories, or nutrition; a **flavour product** is used at sub-perception to perception-level dosages (typically **< 1% w/w** of the finished food) to deliver a specific sensory identity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Distinction:** "Flavour" itself is a *sensory perception* — the integrated response of **Taste (gustation) + Aroma (olfaction) + Chemesthesis (trigeminal sensation like pungency/cooling) + Visual & Texture cues**. A "Flavour Product" is the *physical commercial preparation* that delivers this perception.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The Spice Rack & The Music Studio

Imagine you walk into a professional kitchen. The chef doesn't use a single spice; she has a **spice rack** with individual jars (cinnamon, clove, cardamom) and **readymade masala blends** (Garam Masala, Biryani Masala). Some of those jars contain *raw, naturally extracted* materials (whole spices) and some contain *engineered standardized products* like **oleoresins** (a fat-soluble concentrated extract).

Now think of a **music recording studio**. The "raw sound" is the natural ingredient (a fruit, a spice). The "mixing console, equalizer, and reverb unit" are the **flavour products** — they don't create the music alone, but they *engineer* the final sensory output. Natural extracts, synthetic molecules, and compounded blends are the *instruments and effects pedals* of the flavourist's studio.

> [!TIP]
> **The KTU "Flavour Triangle" (Intuitive Mnemonic):**
> ```
>              SOURCE
>             (Plant/Animal/Microbial)
>                  /\
>                 /  \
>                /    \
>          EXTRACTION  SYNTHESIS
>                \    /
>                 \  /
>                  \/
>             FLAVOUR PRODUCT
>          (Isolate / Blend / Reaction)
> ```
> Every flavour product in commerce is born at the intersection of *one of two origins* (extraction vs. synthesis) and is delivered as *one of three forms* (isolate, blend, or reaction product).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Regulatory Thresholds (KTU Board-Favorite Values)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Mandated Numerical Familiarity:**
> - **Threshold of perception** (olfactory) for most aroma molecules: **10⁻⁶ to 10⁻¹² g/L** in air (parts-per-billion to parts-per-trillion range).
> - **Threshold of perception** (gustatory) for taste compounds: **10⁻³ to 10⁻⁴ mol/L** in water.
> - **Typical usage rate** of compounded flavours in finished foods: **0.05% – 0.5% (w/w)**.
> - **FEMA GRAS** (Generally Recognized As Safe) status is the *de facto* global regulatory benchmark.
> - **EU Regulation 1334/2008** classifies flavouring substances into 15 categories, of which the KTU syllabus focuses on **6 major commercial categories**.

## 1.4 Broad Taxonomy of Flavour Products (Section 1 Snapshot)

| # | Class | Origin | Example |
|---|-------|--------|---------|
| 1 | **Natural Extracts** | Plant/animal physical extraction | Orange oil, vanilla extract |
| 2 | **Oleoresins** | Solvent extraction of spices | Black pepper oleoresin |
| 3 | **Synthetic Aromatic Chemicals** | Chemical synthesis | Vanillin, Ethyl maltol |
| 4 | **Process Flavours** | Thermal/Maillard reaction | Beefy, chocolate, bread notes |
| 5 | **Compounded / Blended Flavours** | Mixture of above | Cola, strawberry, biryani flavours |
| 6 | **Encapsulated Flavours** | Above + matrix protection | Spray-dried orange powder |
| 7 | **Flavour Enhancers** | Single-compound potentiators | MSG, IMP, GMP |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Major Categories of Flavour Products — A Structured Logic Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Natural Flavour Extracts (Essential Oils & Absolute Oils)

**Definition:** Concentrated hydrophobic liquids containing volatile aroma compounds obtained from plant material by *physical means only* (distillation, expression, maceration) — *no chemical reaction permitted* under the legal definition of "Natural" (EU 1334/2008, Art. 3(2)(c)).

**Why it exists:** Many aromatic compounds are heat-labile. Direct addition of the source plant (e.g., crushed cardamom) is *bulky, inconsistent, and unstable*. Extraction concentrates the volatile fraction and standardizes potency.

**How it's made — the three industrial routes:**

1. **Hydrodistillation (Steam Distillation):** Plant material is exposed to live steam. Volatile oils co-distill with water (boiling point depression). Condensate separates in a *Florentine flask* into oil + hydrosol.
2. **Cold Expression (Cold Pressing):** Applied to *Citrus* peels (lemon, orange, bergamot). The peel is mechanically scratched/pressed to rupture oil glands — no heat is applied, preserving thermolabile top notes.
3. **Solvent Extraction → Concretes → Absolutes:** For *delicate flowers* (jasmine, rose) that cannot survive distillation. Yields first a *concrete* (wax + oil) which is then washed with ethanol to give the *absolute*.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Comparison:**
> - **Essential Oil** = Volatile, distilled/expressed (e.g., *Mentha piperita* oil).
> - **Oleoresin** = Volatile + Non-volatile (resins, fixed oils), solvent extracted (e.g., *Piper nigrum* oleoresin).
> - **Absolute** = Alcohol-soluble, solvent-extracted, premium perfume-grade.

### 2.1.2 Oleoresins

**Definition:** Solvent extracts of spices containing the *complete flavour profile* — both the volatile essential oil fraction and the *non-volatile* pungent, coloured, and resinous components.

**Why preferred over ground spice in industry:**
- **Standardization** of capsaicin, piperine, curcumin content.
- **Microbial stability** — solvent extraction drastically reduces bioburden.
- **Dispersion** in oil/fat systems (instant noodles, seasoning oils).
- **No microbial spoilage, no insect infestation, no off-aroma from lipid oxidation of stored seed.**

**Representative examples KTU loves to test:**
- **Capsicum oleoresin** — quantified by *Scoville Heat Units* (SHU); pure capsaicin ≈ **16,000,000 SHU**.
- **Black pepper oleoresin** — piperine content **≥ 40%** for premium grade.
- **Turmeric oleoresin** — curcumin **≥ 30%**.
- **Ginger oleoresin** — gingerol content **≥ 25%**.

### 2.1.3 Synthetic / Nature-Identical Aromatic Chemicals

**Definition:** Pure organic molecules produced by *chemical synthesis* that are *chemically identical* to compounds found in nature (called "Nature-Identical" under EU law) or *wholly new* molecules with no natural counterpart ("Artificial" under EU law).

**Why they dominate 60–70% of the global flavour market:** They are **cheap, pure, stable, scalable, and allergen-controlled**. For example, synthetic **vanillin** is produced at industrial scale from *guaiacol* or *lignin* (a paper-industry by-product) at <$15/kg versus natural vanilla extract at >$2,000/kg.

**The "Big Five" KTU-Tested Synthetic Molecules:**

| Molecule | Odour Description | Natural Occurrence | Industrial Use |
|----------|------------------|-------------------|----------------|
| **Vanillin** (4-hydroxy-3-methoxybenzaldehyde) | Sweet, creamy, vanilla | Vanilla pod (1.5–3% w/w) | Bakery, dairy, beverages |
| **Ethyl Vanillin** | 2.5–3× stronger than vanillin | Not found in nature | Confectionery, chocolate |
| **Benzaldehyde** | Bitter almond | Bitter almonds, cherry stones | Maraschino cherry, almond |
| **Ethyl Maltol** | Caramelized sugar, cotton candy | Bread crust (trace) | Bakery, beverages |
| **Diacetyl (2,3-butanedione)** | Buttery, creamy | Fermented dairy | Margarine, butter flavours |

### 2.1.4 Process Flavours (Reaction Flavours / Maillard Reaction Products)

**Definition:** Flavours produced by **controlled thermal reaction** between amino acids, peptides, or proteins and reducing sugars (Maillard reaction) — the same chemistry that browns bread and sears meat.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Maillard Cascade (KTU 2024 must-know):**
> 1. **Condensation:** Carbonyl (sugar) + Amine (amino acid) → Schiff base → **Amadori compound** (reversible).
> 2. **Dehydration / Fragmentation:** Generates reactive **dicarbonyls**, **furans**, **pyrazines**, **thiophenes**, **thiazoles**.
> 3. **Strecker Degradation:** Amino acid side chains break off, generating **Strecker aldehydes** (signature aroma) and **pyrazines** (roasted notes).
> 4. **Polymerization:** Brown nitrogenous polymers (**melanoidins**) form.

**Why industry loves process flavours:**
- **Cheese flavours** in minutes instead of 6–18 months of ripening (saves enormous cost).
- **Meaty/roasted** notes without animal source — drives the *plant-based meat* revolution.
- **Caramel/biscuit/bread crust** notes standardized in seconds.

**Critical control parameter:** Reaction temperature **130–180 °C**, time **5–60 min**, **pH 5–8**, water activity **a<sub>w</sub> 0.6–0.9**. Too high → burnt notes and acrylamide formation (regulatory concern).

### 2.1.5 Compounded / Blended Flavours

**Definition:** A *finished, ready-to-use* mixture of natural extracts, synthetic aroma chemicals, and process flavours formulated by a flavourist to mimic or create a specific food profile.

**The "Magic Triangle" of compounding:**
- **Top note** (1–10% of blend) — volatile, evaporates first (citrus, mint).
- **Middle / Body note** (50–70% of blend) — the heart of the flavour (fruity, floral, spice).
- **Base note** (20–40% of blend) — heavy, long-lasting, fixative (vanilla, musk, woody).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Most *commercial* food products (cola, ketchup, snack seasonings, instant noodles) use compounded flavours, not single molecules. The 14-mark KTU question on "flavour products" is almost always an *application* question — "Discuss with examples the role of compounded flavours in the snack industry."

### 2.1.6 Encapsulated Flavours

**Definition:** Flavour compounds entrapped within or coated by a *protective matrix* (wall material) to control release, improve stability, and enable dry-powder delivery.

**Why it exists (the 4 industrial pain points it solves):**
1. **Volatility loss** — limonene in orange oil evaporates within days if unprotected.
2. **Oxidation** — aldehydes go rancid on exposure to O₂.
3. **Interaction with food matrix** — direct contact with acidic or aqueous phases causes premature release.
4. **Handling convenience** — liquid flavours can't be added to dry mixes (soup powders, cake mixes) without agglomeration.

**Common encapsulation techniques:**
- **Spray drying** (most common; matrix = maltodextrin/gum arabic).
- **Coacervation** (complex coacervation with gelatin + gum arabic; gives controlled-release capsules).
- **Molecular inclusion** with **β-cyclodextrin** (forms a "doughnut-shaped" inclusion complex with the flavour molecule locked inside).
- **Liposomal entrapment** for water-dispersible flavours.

### 2.1.7 Flavour Enhancers (Umami & Synergists)

**Definition:** Substances that *do not have a strong intrinsic flavour* but **potentiate** existing flavours in food. They act on specific taste receptors (T1R1/T1R3 for umami; T1R2/T1R3 for sweet).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Umami Quintet" — five KTU-favourite synergists:**
> 1. **Monosodium Glutamate (MSG)** — sodium salt of glutamic acid; first discovered by Prof. Kikunae Ikeda in 1908 from *kombu* seaweed.
> 2. **Disodium 5′-Inosinate (IMP)** — from meat and fish.
> 3. **Disodium 5′-Guanylate (GMP)** — from mushrooms.
> 4. **Disodium Succinate** — synergy with seafood.
> 5. **Ribonucleotide blends** (I+G) — IMP+GMP shows **7× synergy** over either alone.

**Synergy Equation (KTU-favourite quantitative fact):**
> 1 g MSG + 0.02 g I+G ≈ 7 g MSG alone in perceived umami intensity.

> [!TIP]
> **Single-page revision table for the entire Module 3 — print this mentally before the exam:**

| Property | Natural Extract | Oleoresin | Synthetic Aroma Chemical | Process Flavour | Compounded Flavour | Encapsulated Flavour | Flavour Enhancer |
|----------|----------------|-----------|--------------------------|------------------|--------------------|--------------------|------------------|
| **Origin** | Plant/animal physical | Plant solvent | Petrochemical/bio-ferm. | Thermal reaction | Blend of all | Any + matrix | Microbial/chem. |

| **Purity** | Variable | Standardized | **Highest (≥ 99%)** | Variable (mixture) | Formulated | High | High |
| **Stability** | Poor–Medium | Good | **Excellent** | Good | Variable | **Excellent** | Excellent |
| **Usage level (w/w)** | 0.05–1% | 0.02–0.1% | 0.001–0.1% | 0.1–1% | 0.05–0.5% | 0.1–1% | 0.1–0.5% |
| **Regulatory** | "Natural" label-eligible | "Natural" | "Nature-identical" / "Artificial" | "Natural" if precursors are natural | Depends on components | Depends on core | Specific E-number |

| **Industrial application** | Beverages, perfumery | Seasoning blends, RTE foods | Bakery, confectionery | Snacks, meat analogues, dairy | All sectors | Dry mixes, chewing gum | All savoury foods |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Flavour product engineering is foundational to:
- **Snack food industry** (potato chips, namkeen, instant noodles — *compounded + encapsulated savoury flavours*).
- **Plant-based meat/dairy** — impossible to mimic beefy/cheesy notes without *process flavours*.
- **Beverage industry** — natural and synthetic flavours drive 80% of new product launches.
- **Pharmaceutical palatability** — masking bitter APIs (active pharmaceutical ingredients) in syrups and chewables.
- **Pet food palatability enhancers** — a multi-billion-dollar niche industry.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Industrial Production Logic: How a Process Flavour is Engineered

The production of a *process flavour* (e.g., a meaty / beefy / chicken-like flavour) is the most complex category in the syllabus, and is a high-value KTU 14-mark topic. We will walk through the engineering logic exhaustively.

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Produce a thermally generated flavour with a specific sensory profile (say, *roasted chicken*) starting from simple precursors.

### Stage 1 — Precursor Selection
Select the **amino acid source** and **reducing sugar source** based on the desired target flavour.

**Step 1.1:** Choose the **amino acid profile**. Cysteine and methionine (sulfur-containing) generate *meaty / savory sulfur notes* (e.g., 2-methyl-3-furanthiol, dimethyl trisulfide). Lysine and leucine generate *roasted, bread-like* notes. Glutamic acid contributes *umami*.

**Step 1.2:** Choose the **reducing sugar**. Glucose and xylose are the workhorses. Pentoses (xylose, ribose) are 50–100× more reactive than hexoses — they generate 5–10× more aroma per unit mass.

**Step 1.3:** Choose the **pH** (typically 5.5–6.5 for meaty, 7.0–8.0 for bread). Lower pH = more furan formation; higher pH = more pyrazine formation.

### Stage 2 — Reaction Setpoint Calculation (Quantitative Logic)

> [!TIP]
> The reaction rate of the Maillard cascade follows a temperature-time equivalence loosely described by an Arrhenius-type relationship:

$$ k = A \cdot e^{\left(\frac{-E_a}{R \cdot T}\right)} $$

where
- $k$ is the apparent rate constant of colour/aroma development,
- $A$ is the pre-exponential factor (system-specific),
- $E_a$ is the activation energy,
- $R$ is the universal gas constant **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**,
- $T$ is the absolute temperature in **kelvin (K)**.

For a target colour development (absorbance at 420 nm of $A_{420} = 0.5$), the empirical time-temperature relation for a model glucose-glycine Maillard system is:

$$ \ln(t) = \alpha - \beta \cdot T $$

where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are system-specific constants. This implies that **every 10 °C increase in reaction temperature approximately halves the required reaction time** (the Q₁₀ rule of thumb).

**Derivation of the Q₁₀ approximation:**

$$ \frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{A \cdot e^{(-E_a / R \cdot T_2)}}{A \cdot e^{(-E_a / R \cdot T_1)}} = e^{\left[\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]} $$

For a typical Maillard $E_a \approx 120$ kJ/mol and $T_1 = 403$ K (130 °C), $T_2 = 413$ K (140 °C):

$$ \frac{k_2}{k_1} = e^{\left[\frac{120000}{8.314} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{403} - \frac{1}{413}\right)\right]} $$

$$ \frac{k_2}{k_1} = e^{\left[14433.6 \cdot (0.002481 - 0.002421)\right]} = e^{[14433.6 \cdot 0.0000601]} $$

$$ \frac{k_2}{k_1} = e^{0.867} \approx 2.38 $$

So the reaction rate roughly **doubles to triples per 10 °C** in the Maillard regime, justifying the Q₁₀ ≈ 2–3 industrial rule.

### Stage 3 — Reactor Design Logic (Batch Reactor)

For a *batch* stirred-tank reactor of volume $V$ operating with precursor loading $C_0$ (g/L), the consumption of limiting reactant $C_A$ follows:

$$ -\frac{dC_A}{dt} = k \cdot C_A^n $$

For an apparent first-order Maillard system ($n = 1$):

$$ C_A(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t} $$

The fractional conversion $X_A$ is:

$$ X_A = 1 - \frac{C_A(t)}{C_0} = 1 - e^{-k \cdot t} $$

The *target* conversion is often **$X_A = 0.85$** for full aroma development, which gives the *required* reaction time:

$$ t_{\text{required}} = -\frac{\ln(1 - X_A)}{k} = -\frac{\ln(0.15)}{k} = \frac{1.897}{k} $$

At $T = 150$ °C with $k \approx 0.05$ min⁻¹, this gives $t_{\text{required}} \approx 38$ minutes — typical industrial value.

### Stage 4 — Quenching and Stabilization

The reaction is stopped by **rapid cooling** to < 50 °C (heat exchanger or jacketed vessel with chilled water) and by **pH shift to 3.5–4.0** (citric or phosphoric acid), which arrests Amadori rearrangement.

### Stage 5 — Standardization and Quality Control

The product is standardized to a *reference* sensory or analytical marker — for example, total pyrazine content (mg/g), or spectrophotometric colour at $A_{420}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why each step is needed (KTU's "why" pattern):**
> - Step 1 sets the *aroma profile*. Different precursors → different sensory outcomes.
> - Step 2 prevents *over-reaction* (burnt notes, off-flavours) and *under-reaction* (flat, un-developed aroma).
> - Step 3 ensures *batch uniformity* — critical for industrial scale.
> - Step 4 prevents *post-cooking flavour drift* during storage.
> - Step 5 ensures *batch-to-batch consistency* — the soul of a successful commercial flavour.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Natural vs. Nature-Identical vs. Artificial vs. Process

> [!TIP]
> **This 4-way analytical matrix is the single most testable artifact in Module 3. KTU has asked variants in 2018, 2020, 2022, and 2023 university papers. Memorize it.**

| Criterion | Natural Flavouring | Nature-Identical | Artificial | Process Flavour |
|-----------|-------------------|------------------|------------|-----------------|
| **EU legal definition (Reg. 1334/2008)** | From natural source by physical/enzymatic/microbial means | Chemically synthesized but *chemically identical* to a natural substance | Chemically synthesized, *not found in nature* | From heating of precursors, may contain MRPs |
| **FEMA classification** | Natural | Natural | Artificial | Natural (if precursors natural) |
| **Source** | Plant/animal | Lab synthesis matching natural compound | Petrochemical or bio-fermentation route | Amino acid + sugar + heat |
| **Cost benchmark (relative to vanillin)** | 100–200× | 1× | 0.5–1× | 3–5× |
| **Purity** | Variable (depends on crop, season, geography) | **≥ 98%** | **≥ 99%** | Mixture, but standardized |
| **Stability** | Poor (terpenes oxidize) | Excellent | Excellent | Good (depends on matrix) |
| **Consumer perception** | **"Clean label"** preferred | Neutral | Negative (rejected by some consumers) | Neutral to positive ("natural" allowed if precursors natural) |
| **KTU example (memorize these 3)** | Vanillin from vanilla pod | Synthetic vanillin from guaiacol | Ethyl vanillin (not in nature) | Process chicken flavour (HVP + sugar) |
| **Regulatory limit in EU** | Quantum satis (no limit) | Quantum satis | Limited (e.g., 100 mg/kg in beverages) | Quantum satis if MRP < some threshold |
| **Carbon footprint** | High (agriculture) | Low (chemical plant) | **Lowest** | Medium |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Oleoresin Yield Calculation (A Common KTU 14-Mark Sub-Part)

> **Question:** Calculate the yield of capsicum oleoresin from 1,000 kg of dried red chilli containing **0.8% capsaicinoids on dry basis** if the extraction efficiency is **92%** and the final oleoresin contains **6.5% capsaicinoids**.

**Step 1 — Capsaicinoids in starting material:**

$$ M_{\text{cap,input}} = 1000 \text{ kg} \times 0.008 = 8.0 \text{ kg} $$

**Step 2 — Capsaicinoids actually extracted (with 92% efficiency):**

$$ M_{\text{cap,extracted}} = 8.0 \times 0.92 = 7.36 \text{ kg} $$

**Step 3 — Mass of oleoresin produced (oleoresin contains 6.5% capsaicinoids):**

$$ M_{\text{oleoresin}} = \frac{M_{\text{cap,extracted}}}{0.065} = \frac{7.36}{0.065} = 113.23 \text{ kg} $$

**Step 4 — Overall yield (% w/w of dried chilli):**

$$ Y = \frac{113.23}{1000} \times 100 = 11.32\% $$

**Answer:** The yield of capsicum oleoresin is **≈ 113.2 kg from 1,000 kg of dried chilli** (yield ≈ **11.32% w/w**). This is realistic — industrial yields typically range 8–15% depending on chilli variety and solvent.

## 3.4 The Five Sensory Notes of Flavour (And Their Chemistry Map)

| Sensory Note | Chemical Class | Example Molecule | Threshold (g/L water) |
|--------------|---------------|------------------|------------------------|
| **Sweet** | Aldehydes, ketones | Vanillin, maltol | ~10⁻⁵ |
| **Sour** | Organic acids | Citric, lactic | ~10⁻³ |
| **Salty** | Inorganic salts | NaCl | ~10⁻² |
| **Bitter** | Alkaloids, peptides | Caffeine, quinine | ~10⁻⁵ |
| **Umami** | Amino acids, nucleotides | MSG, IMP, GMP | ~10⁻⁴ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this table matters in the exam:** When a 14-mark question says *"Discuss the chemical basis of the five basic tastes and their industrial modulation"*, this is the answer backbone.

## 4.3 The Maillard Cascade — Reaction Pathway Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Reducing Sugar] --> A1[Open Chain Form]
    B0[Amino Acid] --> A1
    A1 --> A2[Schiff Base]
    A2 --> A3[Amadori Compound]
    A3 --> P1[Dehydration Pathway]
    A3 --> P2[Fragmentation Pathway]
    A3 --> P3[Strecker Degradation]
    P1 --> O1[Furans and Furfurals]
    P1 --> O2[Reductones]
    P2 --> O3[Dicarbonyls]
    P2 --> O4[Pyrazines]
    P3 --> O5[Strecker Aldehydes]
    P3 --> O6[Sulphur Compounds from Cys Met]
    O1 --> X1[Brown Polymers Melanoidins]
    O2 --> X1
    O3 --> X1
    O4 --> X1
    O5 --> Y1[Sensory Aroma Profile]
    O6 --> Y1
    X1 --> Y2[Colour and Body]
    Y1 --> Z[Final Process Flavour]
    Y2 --> Z
```

## 4.4 Encapsulation Technique Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Select Encapsulation Method] --> Q1{Is the product a dry powder?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is release immediate needed?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Is the product water based?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| LIPO[Liposomal Encapsulation]
    Q3 -->|No| COAC[Complex Coacervation]
    Q2 -->|Yes| SD[Spray Drying with Maltodextrin]
    Q2 -->|No| CD[Beta Cyclodextrin Inclusion]
    Q2 -->|Controlled release| COAC2[Complex Coacervation]
    SD --> OUT[Encapsulated Flavour Powder]
    CD --> OUT
    COAC --> OUT
    COAC2 --> OUT
    LIPO --> OUT
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model Paper]**

### **Q1. Differentiate between an "essential oil" and an "oleoresin" with one example each.** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Essential Oil | Oleoresin |
|---------|---------------|-----------|
| **Method of preparation** | Steam distillation / cold expression (physical) | Solvent extraction (organic solvent) |
| **Composition** | Contains only the **volatile** aroma fraction | Contains volatile + **non-volatile** (resin, fixed oil, pungent) fractions |
| **State** | Always liquid | Can be semi-solid paste or viscous liquid |
| **Example** | Cardamom oil, lemon oil | Capsicum oleoresin, black pepper oleoresin |
| **Colour** | Usually colourless to pale yellow | Deeply coloured (orange-red for capsicum, brown for clove) |

**Example line to write:** *Cardamom essential oil contains only the volatile terpenes (cineole, terpinyl acetate), whereas cardamom oleoresin additionally contains the fixed oil and resinous matter, giving the *complete* flavour and pungency of the spice.*

### **Q2. What is a "process flavour"? State two industrial applications.** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

A **process flavour** is a flavouring substance obtained by heating a mixture of precursors — typically **amino acids / peptides / proteins** and **reducing sugars** — under controlled conditions of temperature (130–180 °C), pH (5–8), and water activity (0.6–0.9), to drive the **Maillard reaction** and generate aroma-active compounds like pyrazines, furans, and Strecker aldehydes.

**Two industrial applications (any two of these for full marks):**
1. **Cheese flavour** — produced in hours via Maillard of HVP + glucose + lipid, replacing months of natural ripening.
2. **Meat-like flavour** for plant-based meat analogues (e.g., Beyond Burger, Impossible Foods) using cysteine + ribose + thiamine.
3. **Caramel / biscuit / bread-crust notes** in bakery and snack seasonings.
4. **Coffee and cocoa flavour** in beverages and confectionery.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – December 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

### **Question A — Compulsory Choice 1**

**Q(A). (a) Classify the different flavour products used in the food industry with suitable examples. Discuss the role of compounded flavours in modern processed food.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer Structure:**

**(a) Classification (4 marks):** A complete classification into 7 categories — natural extracts, oleoresins, synthetic aroma chemicals, process flavours, compounded flavours, encapsulated flavours, and flavour enhancers — with at least one example for each. State briefly the manufacturing principle and key application.

**(b) Role of compounded flavours in modern processed food (3 marks):**

1. **Sensory standardization** — every batch of a branded snack tastes identical, regardless of season or origin of raw spices. The flavourist's blend ensures *batch-to-batch consistency*.
2. **Cost optimization** — use of expensive naturals (e.g., saffron, real vanilla) is minimized by using synthetic top notes (safranal, vanillin) blended with natural body notes.
3. **Process compatibility** — compounded flavours can be engineered to survive **high-temperature baking, UHT processing, or extrusion cooking** by selecting heat-stable molecules for the base notes.
4. **Clean-label flexibility** — the formulator can choose "natural", "nature-identical", or "artificial" constituents based on the marketing requirement of the finished product.
5. **Trademark protection** — the *recipe* of a compounded flavour (e.g., Coca-Cola, KFC's "11 herbs and spices" analogue) is a trade secret and a major source of **brand equity** (IP value).
6. **Multi-cultural product development** — biryani flavour for the Indian market, teriyaki for Japan, peri-peri for Africa — all achievable by the same compounder using regional natural extracts and synthetic base notes.

**Q(A). (b) Explain the Maillard reaction with its significance in process flavour manufacture. Describe the formation of any four aroma-active compounds formed during this reaction.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer Structure:**

**(a) Maillard reaction – definition and stages (3.5 marks):** Define Maillard reaction as the non-enzymatic browning between carbonyl group of reducing sugars and amino group of amino acids/peptides. State the **three main stages**: (i) condensation to Amadori compound, (ii) dehydration and fragmentation producing reactive intermediates, (iii) Strecker degradation and polymerization. Mention that the reaction generates both **colour (melanoidins)** and **aroma** (heterocyclic volatiles).

**(b) Four aroma-active compounds formed (3.5 marks):**

| Compound | Class | Aroma | Formed From |
|----------|-------|-------|-------------|
| **2-Methyl-3-furanthiol** | Furanthiol | Meaty, savory | Cysteine + ribose |
| **2,5-Dimethylpyrazine** | Pyrazine | Roasted, nutty | Glycine / alanine + glucose |
| **Maltol** | Pyranone | Caramelized sugar | Maltose thermal degradation |
| **Furfural** | Furan | Bread, almond | Pentose dehydration |
| **3-Mercapto-2-pentanone** | Thioketone | Onion, garlic | Cysteine + pentose |
| **Strecker aldehyde of leucine → 3-methylbutanal** | Aldehyde | Malty, chocolate | Leucine |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Maillard Question:**
> Most students write the *definition* and *stop*. To get full 7 marks you **MUST**:
> 1. State the **temperature and pH range** of industrial Maillard (130–180 °C, pH 5–8) — 1 mark.
> 2. State the **three stages** explicitly with the **Amadori rearrangement** named — 1 mark.
> 3. Give **four named compounds with their classes and the precursor pair** — this is worth **3.5 marks** by itself. Skipping the precursor is a common -2 mark deduction.
> 4. Mention the role of **cysteine → sulfur notes** (meaty) and **lysine/leucine → bread/cereal** notes — 1 mark.
> 5. End with a one-line statement on **acrylamide as a regulatory concern** (formed at > 180 °C) — bonus 0.5 mark.

---

### **Question B — Alternative Choice**

**Q(B). (a) Discuss the role of flavour enhancers in food formulation. Explain the synergy between MSG and 5′-ribonucleotides with a suitable example.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer Structure:**

**(a) Role of flavour enhancers (3 marks):** Flavour enhancers (MSG, IMP, GMP, disodium succinate) are substances with little or no flavour of their own that **potentiate** existing taste perception — particularly umami — by binding to specific G-protein-coupled receptors (T1R1/T1R3 heterodimer) on the tongue. They lower the detection threshold of food-borne glutamate and nucleotides, allowing formulation of **lower-sodium** products without losing palatability (a key strategy in the WHO's sodium reduction targets).

**(b) Synergy of MSG + 5′-ribonucleotides (4 marks):**

- **Mechanism:** MSG binds T1R1; IMP and GMP bind T1R3. Co-binding produces a **synergistic activation** far greater than additive.
- **Quantitative fact:** A blend of **98% MSG + 2% I+G** provides umami intensity equivalent to **7× the MSG level** used alone.
- **Industrial application:** Used in **instant noodles, savoury snacks, soups, sauces, processed cheese, and plant-based meat** at 0.1–0.5% w/w.
- **Specific product example:** *Maggi 2-Minute Noodles* masala — contains MSG and disodium inosinate + disodium guanylate (I+G) at the bottom of the ingredient list.
- **Regulatory limit:** MSG quantum satis in EU; FDA GRAS; JECFA ADI "not specified" (the safest category).

**Q(B). (b) What is an encapsulated flavour? Describe the spray drying method of encapsulation with a suitable flow diagram (in words). List two advantages and two limitations.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer Structure:**

**(a) Definition (1 mark):** An **encapsulated flavour** is a flavour compound (core material) entrapped within a *protective wall matrix* (shell material) to convert liquid/volatile flavours into stable, free-flowing powders, control release, and prevent oxidation.

**(b) Spray drying method (4 marks):**

- **Wall material** preparation: dissolve **maltodextrin (DE 10–20)** + **gum arabic** in water (typical 30–40% solids) at 50–60 °C.
- **Emulsification:** Add the flavour oil (typically orange, lemon, or coffee oil) at 10–25% of total solids; homogenize at 2000–5000 psi to form a stable oil-in-water emulsion (droplet size 1–5 μm).
- **Atomization:** Feed emulsion into spray dryer at 10–50 mL/min through a rotary atomizer (10,000–25,000 rpm) or nozzle — produces droplets 10–100 μm in diameter.
- **Drying chamber:** Inlet air 160–200 °C, outlet 80–110 °C, residence time 5–30 seconds. Rapid water evaporation forms a crust of wall material around each oil droplet.
- **Collection:** Cyclone separator collects the dry encapsulated powder; secondary wet scrubber captures fines.
- **Product:** Free-flowing powder with 10–25% entrapped flavour oil, 75–90% wall, moisture < 3–5%, a<sub>w</sub> < 0.3.

**(c) Two advantages and two limitations (2 marks):**

| Advantages | Limitations |
|-----------|-------------|
| Converts liquid flavours to easy-to-handle dry powder | Cost of equipment (spray dryer is capital-intensive) |
| Excellent oxidative stability (oxygen barrier) | Heat-sensitive flavours can be lost in the hot drying zone |
| Controlled release on rehydration | Limited loading capacity (~20–25% oil) |
| Compatible with dry-mix products (soups, cake mixes) | Emulsion instability if wall material is not chosen properly |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Encapsulation Question:**
> - Do NOT confuse **spray drying** with **freeze drying** — spray drying uses *hot air* (160–200 °C inlet) for *seconds*; freeze drying uses *vacuum sublimation* of ice for *hours/days*. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this error.
> - State the **role of DE (Dextrose Equivalent)** of maltodextrin — *low DE (10–20) gives better encapsulation, low hygroscopicity*. This is a 1-mark detail that 70% of students miss.
> - Mention the **typical loading** (10–25% oil) — examiners reward numerical specificity.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Master Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Master Pitfall List for "Different Flavour Products" Module 3:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "flavour" with "spice".** Spice is the raw plant material; flavour product is the engineered preparation. Conflating the two is a guaranteed -2 mark hit.
> 2. **Do NOT use "MSG is bad" wording.** Scientifically, MSG is **FEMA GRAS and JECFA ADI "not specified"**. Use scientific neutrality.
> 3. **Always name at least 2 specific molecules** when discussing synthetic or process flavours (e.g., vanillin + ethyl maltol). Generic statements like "chemicals are used" score zero.
> 4. **Always state the regulatory class** (FEMA GRAS number, EU category, FSSAI reference) — KTU examiners reward regulatory literacy with 1 bonus mark.
> 5. **Cite at least one FSSAI regulation reference** (e.g., FSSAI's *Food Safety and Standards (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011, Appendix A*) where flavouring substances are listed. This is a 14-mark question's "show-off" element.
> 6. **Quantify usage levels.** "Vanillin is used at 0.1%" is worth **2 marks**; "Vanillin is used in small amounts" is worth **zero marks**. KTU 2024 scheme demands *quantitative literacy*.
> 7. **End with a future-trends sentence:** "Modern trends include *natural flavours via fermentation, precision fermentation, and AI-assisted flavour design*." This signals the examiner that you are reading 2024, not 2014.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Final-Minute Read Before Entering the Exam Hall):**

- ☐ **7 Categories of Flavour Products:** Natural Extracts, Oleoresins, Synthetic Aromatic Chemicals, Process Flavours, Compounded Flavours, Encapsulated Flavours, Flavour Enhancers.
- ☐ **3 Methods of Essential Oil Production:** Hydrodistillation, Cold Expression (citrus), Solvent Extraction → Concretes → Absolutes.
- ☐ **Oleoresin = Volatile + Non-volatile** (whole-spice profile); **Essential Oil = Volatile only**.
- ☐ **Maillard reaction 3 stages:** Schiff base → Amadori → Strecker / Polymerization.
- ☐ **Maillard conditions:** T = 130–180 °C, pH 5–8, a<sub>w</sub> 0.6–0.9.
- ☐ **Big-5 Synthetic molecules:** Vanillin, Ethyl Vanillin, Benzaldehyde, Ethyl Maltol, Diacetyl.
- ☐ **MSG + I+G synergy:** 98% MSG + 2% I+G = 7× MSG alone (umami perception).
- ☐ **MSG receptor:** T1R1/T1R3 heterodimer; discovered by **Prof. Kikunae Ikeda (1908)** from *kombu*.
- ☐ **Spray-dry encapsulation walls:** Maltodextrin (DE 10–20) + Gum Arabic; inlet 160–200 °C, outlet 80–110 °C.
- ☐ **β-Cyclodextrin** = "molecular doughnut" inclusion complex.
