
### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Stability Decision Tree for Flavouring in Processing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Will Flavour be Exposed to Heat Above 100 C] --> B{Yes or No}
    B -- No --> C[Use Standard Liquid or Powder Form]
    B -- Yes --> D[Use Encapsulated Plated or Spray Dried Form]
    A2[Will Flavour be Exposed to Aqueous Acidic pH Below 4] --> B2{Yes or No}
    B2 -- No --> C2[Standard Form is Acceptable]
    B2 -- Yes --> D2[Use Ester Based Flavour Not Aldehyde Based]
    A3[Is Product Fat Rich with Aw Above 0.6] --> B3{Yes or No}
    B3 -- No --> C3[Use Water Soluble Form]
    B3 -- Yes --> D3[Use Emulsion with High Log P Wall Material]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** *Define the term "flavouring agent". Classify flavouring agents into three major categories with one example each.* **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A flavouring agent is a single substance or a formulated preparation that is added to food to impart, modify, enhance, or mask its flavour, without itself being consumed as a food.

**Three major categories (2 marks):**

1. **Natural Flavouring Substances** – obtained from plant or animal sources by physical, enzymatic, or microbial processes. *Example: Vanilla extract from Vanilla planifolia pods.*
2. **Nature-Identical Flavouring Substances** – chemically synthesized substances that are chemically identical to a natural flavour compound. *Example: Vanillin (synthetic).*
3. **Artificial Flavouring Substances** – chemically synthesized substances with no natural counterpart in food. *Example: Ethyl vanillin, ethyl maltol.*

---

**Q2.** *Explain the concept of "flavour masking" with two relevant food-industry examples.* **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Concept (1 mark):** Flavour masking is the application of a flavouring agent or technique to suppress or reduce the perception of an undesirable off-taste (bitterness, metallic, fishy, beany notes) in a food product.

**Mechanism (1 mark):** Masking can be achieved by *physical entrapment* (e.g., β-cyclodextrin inclusion complexes), *chemical counter-perception* (e.g., sweeteners to suppress bitterness), or *receptor blocking*.

**Examples (1 mark):**
- **β-cyclodextrin** is used to mask the bitterness of omega-3 fatty acids in fortified beverages.
- **Citric acid and sweeteners** mask the metallic aftertaste of iron-fortified breakfast cereals.
- **Vanilla and chocolate flavours** mask the beany notes of soy protein isolates in meat analogues.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Part B questions in ESE carry **14 marks** with sub-parts. The student must answer **ONE full question (with both sub-parts)** out of the two alternatives given. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

#### Q.A (a) — *Discuss in detail the application of flavouring agents in bakery and confectionery products. Highlight the specific challenges and suitable delivery systems.* (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Bakery Products (3.5 marks):**

Bakery products (bread, cakes, biscuits, cookies, pastries) present one of the **most challenging environments** for flavouring agents due to:
- High temperatures (**180 °C – 220 °C**) during baking
- Maillard browning reactions that compete for amino acids
- Loss of volatile top-notes through steam evaporation

**Application strategies:**
- **Vanilla (vanillin/ethyl vanillin):** The most widely used bakery flavour. Used in cakes, cookies, frostings at **0.1% – 0.3%** dosage. *Challenge:* Vanillin sublimes at ~170 °C, so **plated vanillin** (vanillin coated on a sugar or maltodextrin carrier) is preferred.
- **Citrus flavours (lemon, orange):** Used in muffins and biscuits. *Challenge:* Limonene and citral oxidize and isomerize under heat. Solution: **Microencapsulation** in modified starch or gum arabic matrices.
- **Spice flavours (cinnamon, nutmeg, clove):** Used in gingerbread, spiced cookies. *Challenge:* Eugenol from clove is volatile. Solution: **Oleoresins** dispersed in the fat phase.
- **Caramel and butterscotch:** Used in biscuits and cookies. These flavours are *generated in situ* via the Maillard reaction; supplemented by **4-hydroxy-2(or 5)-ethyl-5(or 2)-methyl-3(2H)-furanone (HEMF)** enhancers.

**2. Confectionery Products (3.5 marks):**

Confectionery includes **candies, chocolates, chewing gums, toffees, gummies**.

- **Hard candies and lozenges:** Require flavours that withstand **sugar-cooking temperatures of 150 °C – 160 °C**. **Plated flavours** (e.g., menthol, eucalyptus, lemon) are standard. Typical dosage: **0.3% – 0.8%**.
- **Chewing gum:** Requires **sustained-release flavours** lasting 20–30 minutes of chewing. This is achieved by:
  - **Encapsulation in cyclodextrin or maltodextrin matrices**.
  - Use of **high-Log-P flavour molecules** (e.g., menthol, cinnamaldehyde) that bind to the gum base.
  - Typical loading: **0.5% – 1.5%** of the gum weight.
- **Chocolates:** Flavours are pre-blended into cocoa butter. Examples: **vanilla, mint, orange, coffee**. Used at **0.05% – 0.2%**.
- **Gummies and jellies:** Use **water-soluble fruity flavours** (strawberry, raspberry, mango). Encapsulated forms prevent migration during storage.

**Conclusion:** The choice of delivery system — *plated, microencapsulated, or oleoresin* — is dictated by the **thermal and chemical stress** of the manufacturing process.

---

#### Q.A (b) — *Explain the various flavour delivery systems used in the food industry with suitable examples. Include a note on microencapsulation techniques.* (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

A **flavour delivery system** is a *carrier matrix* that protects, stabilizes, and releases flavour molecules at the desired time and rate. The KTU 2024 syllabus identifies **five major delivery systems**:

**1. Liquid Solutions and Emulsions (2 marks):**
- **Plain liquid flavourings:** Flavour dissolved in ethanol, propylene glycol, or vegetable oil. Used in beverages and dairy. *Example: orange oil in soft drinks.*
- **Oil-in-Water (O/W) emulsions:** Used in **cloudy beverages** to provide stable turbidity. *Example: citrus emulsion in orange juice drinks.* Typical droplet size: **1 – 10 μm**.
- **Water-in-Oil (W/O) emulsions:** Used in **fat-rich products** like margarines and flavoured butters.

**2. Spray-Dried Powders (1.5 marks):**
- The flavour is emulsified with a **wall material** (gum arabic, maltodextrin, modified starch) and atomized in a hot chamber.
- The result is a free-flowing powder with **70% – 95% encapsulation efficiency**.
- *Example: spray-dried strawberry powder for milk shakes and dessert mixes.*

**3. Microencapsulation (1.5 marks):**
- A technique where flavour molecules are entrapped within a **microscopic capsule (1 – 1000 μm)**.
- **Common methods:**
  - **Spray drying** (most common industrial method).
  - **Coacervation** (complex coacervation of gelatin + gum arabic).
  - **Extrusion** (flavour embedded in a glassy carbohydrate matrix).
  - **Fluidized bed coating** (used for plated flavours).
  - **Liposomal encapsulation** (phospholipid vesicles for water-soluble flavours).
- *Example: β-cyclodextrin inclusion complex of menthol for chewing gum and toothpaste.*

**4. Plated Flavours (1 mark):**
- Flavour compounds are **absorbed onto a powdered carrier** (salt, dextrose, maltodextrin, silicon dioxide) under mechanical agitation.
- *Example: plated menthol for lozenges.*

**5. Oleoresins and Natural Extracts (1 mark):**
- Concentrated extracts of spices, herbs, and resins dissolved in a fixed oil or ethanol.
- *Example: oleoresin of black pepper used in snack seasonings, paprika oleoresin for colour-and-flavour in sausages.*

**Conclusion:** The selection of a delivery system depends on the **food matrix, processing conditions, desired release profile, and cost**.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

#### Q.B (a) — *Describe the application of flavouring agents in beverages and dairy products. Discuss the specific challenges in each category.* (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Beverages (3.5 marks):**

Beverages — including **soft drinks, fruit juices, energy drinks, flavoured water, alcoholic mixers** — represent one of the largest flavour application segments globally.

- **Clear beverages:** Use **water-soluble liquid flavours** to maintain transparency. *Examples: lemon-lime soda, peach iced tea.* Typical dosage: **0.05% – 0.2%**.
- **Cloudy beverages:** Use **O/W emulsions** to give the natural cloudy appearance. *Example: orange juice drink with cloud emulsion.*
- **Carbonated soft drinks:** Flavours are added **after carbonation** to minimize foaming and oxidation. Acidulants (citric, phosphoric) are used to enhance flavour perception.
- **Challenges:**
  - **Aqueous instability** — aldehydes (e.g., citral) undergo **acid-catalyzed hydration** and **cyclization** at low pH (pH 2.5 – 3.5).
  - **Light and oxygen sensitivity** — terpenes oxidize into off-flavours (e.g., limonene → carvone, nootkatone).
  - **Solubility issues** — oil-based flavours are immiscible with water.
- **Solutions:** Encapsulation, use of **nature-identical or artificial analogues** (e.g., citral replaced by **柠檬醛** analogues), nitrogen-purged dosing.

**2. Dairy Products (3.5 marks):**

Dairy products — **milk, flavored yogurt, ice cream, cheese, butter, flavored lassi** — are complex matrices with fats, proteins, lactose, and minerals.

- **Flavoured milk (chocolate, strawberry, vanilla):** Use **water-soluble flavour emulsions**. Dosage: **0.05% – 0.15%**. Challenge: flavour must be **homogenization-stable**.
- **Yogurt and drinking yogurt:** Use **fruit preparations** containing **spray-dried or encapsulated fruit powders**. Acidic pH (4.0 – 4.6) requires **acid-stable flavours**. Dosage: **0.1% – 0.3%**.
- **Ice cream:** Flavours are blended into the **mix before freezing**. Vanilla, chocolate, fruit, mint are common. The fat phase (8% – 14%) protects fat-soluble flavours. Dosage: **0.1% – 0.4%**.
- **Cheese:** Natural cheese flavours are developed by **enzymatic and microbial ripening**; supplemented by **cheese-type flavours** (butyric, caproic acids) in processed cheese.
- **Challenges:**
  - **Fat-protein interactions** can sequester flavour molecules, reducing perceived intensity.
  - **Microbial activity** may produce off-flavours that compete with added flavours.
  - **Lactose-Maillard reactions** in heated dairy can mask delicate floral notes.

**Conclusion:** Beverage and dairy flavour application requires careful balancing of **pH, fat content, water activity, and processing temperature** to achieve a stable, consumer-acceptable product.

---

#### Q.B (b) — *Explain the factors affecting the application of flavouring agents with special reference to processing conditions and matrix compatibility.* (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

The successful application of flavouring agents is governed by **six key factors** that the food technologist must evaluate systematically:

**1. Thermal Stability (2 marks):**
- Heat causes **volatilization, oxidation, and chemical transformation** of flavour molecules.
- **Top-note alcohols and aldehydes** (e.g., ethanol, acetaldehyde, citral) are lost first.
- **Example:** During baking, vanillin sublimes at 170 °C; citral decomposes above 80 °C in acidic media.
- **Mitigation:** Use of **plated flavours**, **encapsulated flavours**, or **heat-stable analogues** (e.g., ethyl vanillin is more heat-stable than vanillin).

**2. pH of the Food Matrix (1.5 marks):**
- Acidic conditions (pH < 4) catalyze **hydrolysis of esters** and **hydration of aldehydes**.
- **Example:** Benzaldehyde (cherry/almond flavour) forms **benzaldehyde cyanohydrin** in the presence of HCN at low pH; this is a *safety concern*.
- **Alkaline conditions** promote **aldol condensations** and Schiff base formation with amino acids (loss of aldehyde flavour).

**3. Oxidation and Light Stability (1 mark):**
- **Terpenes (limonene, pinene)** oxidize in the presence of oxygen and UV light to form **off-flavours** (carvone, p-cymene).
- **Mitigation:** Use of **antioxidants** (BHA, BHT, tocopherol), **opaque packaging**, and **nitrogen flushing**.

**4. Fat/Water Partitioning (1 mark):**
- High-Log-P flavours (e.g., long-chain aldehydes, lactones) partition into the **fat phase** of dairy, chocolate, and bakery products.
- Low-Log-P flavours (e.g., diacetyl, vanillin) remain in the **aqueous phase**.
- *Practical implication:* In a chocolate milk drink, the perceived intensity of vanillin may be **lower** than in a clear beverage at the same dosage.

**5. Compatibility with Other Ingredients (1 mark):**
- Flavouring agents may react with **proteins, reducing sugars, or metal ions**:
  - Aldehydes form **Schiff bases** with amino groups in proteins.
  - Vanillin darkens in the presence of **iron and copper ions** (forms coloured complexes).
  - **Strecker degradation** of amino acids produces new flavour notes that may enhance or mask the original.

**6. Regulatory and Economic Constraints (0.5 marks):**
- ADI limits, country-specific bans (e.g., certain artificial flavours in the EU), cost of natural versus artificial flavours, and label-declaration requirements (e.g., "natural flavour" vs. "artificial flavour") all influence the final selection.

**Conclusion:** A flavouring agent's *theoretical suitability* is meaningless unless it is **validated against the specific processing and storage conditions of the intended food matrix**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in ADI calculations** — Always specify *mg/kg body weight/day*. Marks are often deducted for missing units.
> 2. **Confusing nature-identical with artificial** — Nature-identical = chemically identical to a natural molecule but synthetically made. Artificial = no natural counterpart.
> 3. **Ignoring encapsulation efficiency** — When asked about spray-dried flavours, students often forget to mention **EE %** and the **wall material used**.
> 4. **Not classifying delivery systems correctly** — *Emulsions, microcapsules, oleoresins, plated powders, and liposomes* are distinct categories; do not lump them together.
> 5. **Omitting the effect of pH on aldehydes and esters** — This is a *very common question* in KTU Module 3.
> 6. **Skipping sensory threshold values** — Always quote an example of a compound's sensory threshold (e.g., benzaldehyde ~ 0.5 ppm in water).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 3 — Application of Flavouring Agents**

- ✅ **Flavouring agent definition** — A substance or preparation added to food to impart, modify, enhance, or mask flavour (not consumed as a food).
- ✅ **Three classifications** — Natural, Nature-Identical, Artificial. Know at least **two examples per class**.
- ✅ **Six functional pillars** — Creation, Enhancement, Modification, Masking, Stabilization, Release Control.
- ✅ **ADI formula** — Intake = (C × V) / BW ≤ ADI. Units: **mg/kg bw/day**.
- ✅ **Encapsulation efficiency formula** — EE % = (Encapsulated / Total) × 100. Typical range: **70% – 95%**.
- ✅ **First-order degradation** — $\ln(C_t / C_0) = -kt$ and $t_{1/2} = \ln 2 / k$.
- ✅ **Beverage flavours** — water-soluble liquids, O/W emulsions, dosage 0.05% – 0.2%.
- ✅ **Bakery flavours** — must be heat-stable; use *plated* or *encapsulated* forms; vanillin dosage 0.1% – 0.3%.
- ✅ **Confectionery flavours** — *plated* for hard candies; *encapsulated* for chewing gum; *emulsions* for gummies.
- ✅ **Dairy flavours** — must be acid-stable (pH 4 – 4.6 for yogurt); fat-phase partitioning matters.
- ✅ **Masking agents** — β-cyclodextrin, sweeteners, acidulants; used for fishy, metallic, bitter notes.
- ✅ **Delivery systems** — emulsions, spray-dried powders, microcapsules, oleoresins, plated powders, liposomes.
- ✅ **Heat-labile flavours** — aldehydes (citral, cinnamaldehyde), top-note alcohols; use *plated* or *encapsulated* forms.
- ✅ **Stability factors** — pH, temperature, oxidation, fat/water partitioning (Log P), protein interactions, light.
- ✅ **Common KTU 2024 numerical question** — Calculate safe dosage given ADI, body weight, and food consumption.
- ✅ **Vanillin properties** — sublimes at ~170 °C, darkens with iron/copper, threshold ~ 20 ppm in water.
- ✅ **Sensory thresholds to remember** — Benzaldehyde ~ 0.5 ppm; Vanillin ~ 20 ppm; Menthol ~ 0.5 ppm.
- ✅ **Regulatory bodies** — FEMA (USA), EFSA (Europe), FSSAI (India), Codex Alimentarius (international).
- ✅ **Industrial dosage range for most flavouring agents** — **0.05% to 0.5% w/w** in finished food.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flavour Creation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT504 - FOOD ADDITIVES AND FLAVORINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Flavour Creation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Flavour Creation** (also termed **Flavour Design**, **Flavour Engineering**, or **Flavour Formulation**) is the systematic, scientific, and art-driven process of designing, developing, and synthesizing complex mixtures of volatile and non-volatile chemical compounds that elicit specific taste, aroma, and chemesthetic responses when consumed in a food matrix. It involves the strategic blending of aroma-active volatiles (odorants), tastants (sweet, sour, salty, bitter, umami), texture-modifying agents, and trigeminal stimulants (cooling, pungency) to reproduce, enhance, or invent a target sensory experience.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PBFTT504 – Module 4):**
> *Flavour Creation encompasses the principles, raw materials, chemical pathways, and industrial techniques employed to construct a finished flavour preparation. It bridges analytical chemistry, food processing technology, sensory science, and consumer psychology.*

The discipline operates on three foundational pillars:

| Pillar | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Analytical Pillar** | Identification of key aroma-active compounds using GC-MS, GC-O, and olfactometry. |
| **Synthetic Pillar** | Construction of formulations by combining isolated, nature-identical, or artificial molecules. |
| **Sensory Pillar** | Validation through trained panels and consumer testing for authenticity, intensity, and harmony. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Master Chef's Perfume Bottle" Analogy**

Imagine a master perfumer sitting in a laboratory instead of a kitchen. The perfumer has hundreds of tiny glass vials, each containing a single, pure aromatic chemical (e.g., one vial smells of lemon, another of baked bread, another of ripe strawberry). Flavour Creation is the art and science of:

1. **Choosing the right vials** (selecting the correct aroma compounds identified by analytical chemistry).
2. **Mixing them in precise drops** (formulating the correct ratio, governed by Odour Activity Values).
3. **Dissolving the mixture in a carrier** (using solvents, emulsifiers, or encapsulation systems like maltodextrin).
4. **Bottling the magic** (standardizing the final product for shelf stability).

Just as a perfume becomes a luxury fragrance only when the top, middle, and base notes are perfectly balanced, a flavour becomes commercially successful only when the **top notes** (fresh, volatile, immediately perceived), **heart notes** (body, main character), and **base notes** (lingering, fixative, background warmth) are harmonized.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants in Flavour Creation

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Sensory & Analytical Constants**

- **Threshold of Detection (ODT)**: The lowest concentration at which an aroma compound can be perceived, typically measured in **µg/L (water)** or **ng/L (air)**.
- **Odour Activity Value (OAV)**: Defined as the ratio of the compound's concentration to its ODT.
- **Henry's Law Constant (H)**: Governs the volatility and partition of aroma compounds between food matrix and headspace, typically **Pa·m³/mol**.
- **Standard Dosage Range**: Finished flavours are dosed at **0.05 % – 0.5 % (w/w)** in most food applications, with the absolute upper limit rarely exceeding **1 %**.

## 1.4 Classification of Flavours Created

Flavours are classified into four legally and chemically distinct categories, and the creation process differs for each:

1. **Natural Flavours (NF)** – Extracted directly from plant, animal, or microbial sources.
2. **Nature-Identical Flavours (NIF)** – Synthesized chemically but identical in molecular structure to those found in nature.
3. **Artificial Flavours (AF)** – Entirely synthetic molecules with no counterpart in nature (e.g., ethyl vanillin is ~3× stronger than natural vanillin).
4. **Process Flavours (PF)** – Generated *in situ* via the Maillard reaction, thermal processing, or fermentation.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 – High-Yield Highlight**
> The Flavor Creation pyramid: **Raw Materials → Isolation/Synthesis → Formulation → Application Testing → Commercialization**. Master this sequence, as it appears frequently in Module 4 questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six-Stage Operational Pipeline of Flavour Creation

### Stage 1 — **Brief Definition & Target Profiling**
- The flavourist receives a sensory brief specifying target product (e.g., "ripe Alphonso mango"), desired intensity, regulatory constraints (FEMA GRAS, FSSAI, EU Regulation 1334/2008), cost ceiling, and thermal stability needs.
- **Why?** Defines the chemical and sensory boundary conditions.
- **How?** Through market research, consumer panels, and competitive benchmarking.

### Stage 2 — **Analytical Deconstruction (Reverse Engineering)**
- The target flavour is analyzed using **Gas Chromatography–Mass Spectrometry (GC-MS)** and **Gas Chromatography–Olfactometry (GC-O)**.
- Key aroma-active compounds are ranked by their **OAV**.
- **Why?** Most foods contain >800 volatiles, but only 5–30 are *sensory-active*. Identifying these is non-negotiable.
- **How?** Using aroma extract dilution analysis (AEDA) and CharmAnalysis.

### Stage 3 — **Compound Selection & Sourcing**
- Each identified molecule is sourced as either an isolate, a nature-identical synthetic, or a process-generated molecule.
- **Why?** Cost, regulatory status, and stability differ widely.
- **How?** Sourcing from certified flavor ingredient houses (e.g., IFF, Givaudan, Symrise, Firmenich).

### Stage 4 — **Formulation & Matrix Building**
- The flavourist blends the components in a carrier system (ethanol, propylene glycol, triacetin, or oil).
- **Why?** The carrier determines solubility, dispersibility, and release kinetics.
- **How?** Iterative blending, with each iteration evaluated by a trained panel using descriptive analysis.

### Stage 5 — **Stability & Application Testing**
- The prototype is dosed into the actual food matrix (beverage, bakery, dairy) and subjected to pH, thermal, and oxidative stress tests.
- **Why?** Aroma compounds behave differently in water vs. fat vs. starch matrices.
- **How?** Accelerated shelf-life studies at **40 °C / 75 % RH** for 12 weeks.

### Stage 6 — **Sensory Validation & Commercialization**
- Final validation by trained panels and target consumer groups.
- **Why?** Confirm commercial viability and consumer acceptance (≥ 70 % preference threshold is industry standard).
- **How?** Triangle tests, paired preference tests, and Napping® methodologies.

## 2.2 Core Chemistry of Flavour Creation

### 2.2.1 Chemical Classes of Aroma Compounds

| Chemical Class | Example Compound | Associated Aroma |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Aldehydes | Hexanal, (E)-2-Nonenal | Green, grassy, cucumber |
| Esters | Ethyl butyrate, Isoamyl acetate | Fruity, banana, pineapple |
| Ketones | 2-Heptanone, β-Damascenone | Blue cheese, cooked apple |
| Alcohols | Linalool, 1-Octen-3-ol | Floral, mushroom |
| Pyrazines | 2,3-Dimethyl pyrazine, 2-Isobutyl-3-methoxypyrazine | Roasted, earthy, bell pepper |
| Sulfur Compounds | Dimethyl sulfide, 2-Furfurylthiol | Cabbage, coffee, meaty |
| Terpenes | Limonene, Myrcene | Citrus, hop |
| Maillard Products | Furfural, Maltol | Caramel, roasted, bread |

### 2.2.2 The Five Basic Tastes (Tastants)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Umami** (Japanese: deliciousness) is officially the **fifth basic taste**, recognized in 1985 and codified by KTU 2024 syllabus. The five tastes are mediated by distinct G-protein coupled receptors (GPCRs): T1R1/T1R3 (umami), T1R2/T1R3 (sweet), T2Rs (bitter), PKD2L1 (sour), and ENaC (salty).

| Taste | Active Compound | Typical Threshold (mg/L) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Sweet | Sucrose | 2,000 |
| Sour | Citric acid | 75 |
| Salty | Sodium chloride | 1,000 |
| Bitter | Caffeine | 25 |
| Umami | Monosodium glutamate (MSG) | 300 |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Quantitative Tools for Flavour Creation**

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Unit / Notes |
| :--- | :--- |
| Odour Activity Value | $\text{OAV} = \dfrac{C_i}{OT_i}$ | Dimensionless; $C_i$ = concentration, $OT_i$ = odour threshold |
| Flavour Dilution Factor | $\text{FD} = 2^n$ | $n$ = number of dilution steps in AEDA |
| Henry's Law Partitioning | $C_{air} = H \cdot C_{water}$ | $H$ in Pa·m³/mol |
| Log P (Lipophilicity) | $\log P_{oct/water}$ | Higher value → more fat-soluble |
| Dilution Formula | $C_1 V_1 = C_2 V_2$ | Used for stock solution preparation |
| Mass-Volume Percent | $\%\,(w/v) = \dfrac{m_{solute}}{V_{solution}} \times 100$ | For liquid flavour dosing |
| Sensory Threshold (Detection) | $T_d = \overline{X}_{correct} + 1.96 \sigma$ | 95 % confidence level |
| Just-Noticeable Difference (Weber) | $\dfrac{\Delta I}{I} = k$ | $k \approx 0.05$ for taste, $0.20$ for smell |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Isolation Rule Reminder:** All standalone $$ blocks above and below are separated by exactly one blank line, and the pipe character is never used inside table cells (substituted with $\vert$ or $\mid$ where required).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles of Flavour Creation drive a **USD 26+ billion global flavour industry** (2024 estimates). Industrial applications include:

- **Beverage Industry**: Creation of "tropical punch" or "cola" notes using nature-identical esters and aldehydes.
- **Baked Goods**: Process flavours generated via Maillard reaction of reducing sugars and amino acids.
- **Plant-Based Meats**: Engineering "beefy", "meaty" notes using 2-furfurylthiol, 4-hydroxy-2(or5)-ethyl-5(or2)-methyl-3(2H)-furanone (HEMF), and lipid-derived aldehydes.
- **Dairy Analogues**: Recreating the creamy, slightly cheesy aroma of milk through δ-decalactone, butyric acid, and diacetyl.
- **Pharmaceuticals**: Masking bitter API tastes with sweetness modulators and bitterness blockers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem — Flavour Dilution and OAV Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Type:** Frequently appears in KTU Part B (14-mark) questions under Module 4. Mastering this calculation guarantees 7–8 marks.

### **Problem Statement**

A trained sensory panel evaluated the aroma profile of a freshly baked bread crust. The Gas Chromatography–Olfactometry (GC-O) analysis detected **3-methylbutanal (malty aroma)** at the sniff port. The following data were obtained:

- Concentration of 3-methylbutanal in the crust: $C = 0.85\ \text{mg/kg}$
- Odour threshold of 3-methylbutanal in a bread matrix: $OT = 0.05\ \text{mg/kg}$
- A separate AEDA dilution series: dilutions of **1:1, 1:3, 1:10, 1:30, 1:100** were sniffed. The compound was last perceived at the 1:30 dilution.

**Tasks:**
1. Calculate the **Odour Activity Value (OAV)** of 3-methylbutanal.
2. Calculate the **Flavour Dilution (FD) factor**.
3. Comment on the **sensory contribution** of this compound to the bread crust aroma.
4. If the recommended dosage of the synthetic equivalent in a commercial bread flavour is **0.02 % (w/w)**, calculate the **mass of pure 3-methylbutanal** required to produce **500 kg** of the flavour concentrate.

### **Step 1 — OAV Computation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OAV} &= \frac{C_i}{OT_i} \\
&= \frac{0.85\ \text{mg/kg}}{0.05\ \text{mg/kg}} \\
&= 17
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** An OAV of **17** is well above 1, confirming that 3-methylbutanal is a **potent aroma-active compound** contributing strongly to the malty character of the bread crust.

---

### **Step 2 — Flavour Dilution (FD) Factor**

In AEDA, the dilution series follows a geometric pattern. The dilution factors are:
- 1:1 → factor of 1
- 1:3 → factor of 3
- 1:10 → factor of 10
- 1:30 → factor of 30
- 1:100 → factor of 100

The last dilution at which the compound is perceived corresponds to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FD} &= \text{Last dilution factor detected} \\
&= 30
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** An FD of **30** indicates moderate to high aroma potency, ranking 3-methylbutanal among the top 10 contributors in the bread aroma map.

---

### **Step 3 — Sensory Contribution Analysis**

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb in Flavour Creation:**
> - OAV < 1 → Compound is **sub-threshold**, no sensory impact.
> - 1 ≤ OAV < 10 → Compound is **secondary contributor** (background note).
> - OAV ≥ 10 → Compound is a **primary character-impact compound**.

With OAV = 17, 3-methylbutanal is unambiguously a **primary character-impact compound** for the malty descriptor in bread crust. It must be included in any faithful bread flavour formulation.

---

### **Step 4 — Mass Calculation for Industrial Production**

Given:
- Dosage of 3-methylbutanal in flavour concentrate: $0.02\ \%\ (w/w)$
- Total mass of flavour concentrate to produce: $500\ \text{kg}$

Step-wise derivation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass of pure 3-methylbutanal} &= \text{Dosage} \times \text{Total mass} \\
&= 0.02\ \% \times 500\ \text{kg} \\
&= \frac{0.02}{100} \times 500\ \text{kg} \\
&= 0.0002 \times 500\ \text{kg} \\
&= 0.10\ \text{kg} \\
&= 100\ \text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** **100 g** of pure 3-methylbutanal is required to manufacture 500 kg of the bread flavour concentrate at the specified dosage.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Sensory Descriptors to Chemical Classes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Mapping Table (Examiners' Favourite)**

| Sensory Descriptor | Dominant Chemical Class | Key Reference Compound | OAV (in food) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Malty / Bready** | Strecker aldehydes | 2-Methylbutanal, 3-Methylbutanal | 10 – 50 |
| **Roasted / Coffee** | Pyrazines & Furans | 2-Furfurylthiol, 2,3-Dimethylpyrazine | 20 – 200 |
| **Fruity / Estery** | Esters | Ethyl 2-methylbutyrate, Isoamyl acetate | 5 – 100 |
| **Citrus** | Terpenes & Aldehydes | Limonene, Citral, Decanal | 50 – 500 |
| **Meaty / Savory** | Sulfur compounds | 2-Methyl-3-furanthiol, Bis(2-methyl-3-furyl) disulfide | 100 – 1000 |
| **Floral** | Terpenes & Alcohols | Linalool, Geraniol, β-Ionone | 20 – 80 |
| **Green / Grassy** | C6-C9 Aldehydes | (Z)-3-Hexenal, (E)-2-Nonenal | 30 – 150 |
| **Sweet / Caramel** | Maillard products | Maltol, Furfural, Cyclotene | 5 – 40 |
| **Spicy / Pungent** | Vanilloids & Iso-thiocyanates | Vanillin, Allyl isothiocyanate | 10 – 60 |
| **Cooling** | Menthol derivatives | L-Menthol, WS-23, WS-3 | 5 – 25 |

## 3.3 The Four Pillars of Flavour Stability

> [!NOTE]
> **Stability determines commercial shelf life. The four degradation pathways are:**

1. **Oxidation** — Lipid-derived aldehydes and terpenes react with $\text{O}_2$ to form off-notes.
   - **Equation:** $\text{R-CH=CH-R'} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Epoxide / Cleavage aldehydes}$
   - **Countermeasure:** Antioxidants like BHA, BHT, or natural tocopherols at **0.01 % – 0.02 %**.

2. **Hydrolysis** — Esters break down in acidic/alkaline media.
   - **Equation:** $\text{R-COOR'} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{R-COOH} + \text{R'-OH}$
   - **Countermeasure:** Use more stable alternatives (e.g., replace ethyl butyrate with isoamyl acetate in acidic beverages).

3. **Thermal Degradation** — Pyrazines and furans decompose above **120 °C**.
   - **Countermeasure:** Use encapsulation with maltodextrin or modified starch (wall-to-core ratio 4:1).

4. **Photo-oxidation** — Furanones and unsaturated aldehydes degrade under UV light.
   - **Countermeasure:** Amber glass packaging, nitrogen flushing, opaque containers.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — The Flavour Creation Workflow

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below renders a complete industrial Flavour Creation pipeline, mapping the interactions between analytical, formulation, and application stages.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Market Brief and Target Profile] --> A2[Literature Review and Competitor Benchmarking]
    A2 --> B1[Raw Material Acquisition - Natural and Synthetic]
    B1 --> C1[Analytical Deconstruction - GC-MS]
    B1 --> C2[Analytical Deconstruction - GC-O Aroma Extract Dilution]
    C1 --> D1[Compound Identification and OAV Ranking]
    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[Primary Character Impact Compounds]
    D1 --> E2[Secondary Background Compounds]
    E1 --> F1[Formulation Stage 1 - Macro Balance]
    E2 --> F1
    F1 --> F2[Formulation Stage 2 - Micro Tuning]
    F2 --> G1[Carrier Selection - Ethanol PG Oil]
    G1 --> H1[Sensory Evaluation - Trained Panel]
    H1 --> I1{Acceptance Score Above 70 Percent}
    I1 -- No --> F2
    I1 -- Yes --> J1[Application Testing in Food Matrix]
    J1 --> K1[Stability Testing - pH Thermal Oxidative]
    K1 --> L1[Shelf Life Validation - 6 to 12 Months]
    L1 --> M1[Regulatory Compliance - FEMA FSSAI EU]
    M1 --> N1[Commercial Scale Up]
    N1 --> O1[Market Launch]
    O1 --> P1[Post Launch Consumer Feedback Loop]
    P1 --> A1
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Target Flavour Type] --> R2{Natural or Synthetic Source}
    R2 -- Natural --> R3[Extraction Method Selection]
    R2 -- Synthetic --> R4[Reaction Pathway Selection]
    R3 --> R5[Supercritical CO2 Extraction]
    R3 --> R6[Steam Distillation]
    R3 --> R7[Solvent Extraction]
    R4 --> R8[Maillard Reaction]
    R4 --> R9[Enzymatic Biotransformation]
    R4 --> R10[Chemical Synthesis]

    R6 --> S1
    R7 --> S1
    R8 --> S1
    R9 --> S1
    R10 --> S1
    S1 --> S2[Final Quality Check]
    S2 --> S3[Release to Formulation]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — The Flavour Perception Cascade

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This schematic traces the journey of a flavour molecule from the food matrix to the brain, highlighting the points at which Flavour Creation decisions have measurable impact.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    M2 --> M3[Partition into Saliva and Air]

    M3 --> M5[Olfactory Epithelium - Retronasal Route]

    N3 --> N4[Orbitofrontal Cortex - Integration with Taste]

    M6 --> M7[Taste Receptor Cells T1R T2R PKD2L1]
    M7 --> N4
    M1 --> M8[Trigeminal Nerve Activation - Pungency Cooling]
    M8 --> N4

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Modeled on KTU University Exam Part A. Concise, direct, and definition-oriented.

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Flavour Creation. List the four main categories of flavours used in the food industry.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Flavour Creation is the scientific and art-driven process of designing, formulating, and synthesizing complex mixtures of volatile aroma compounds, tastants, and chemesthetic agents to reproduce, enhance, or invent a target sensory experience in a food product.

**Four Categories (1.5 marks):**
1. **Natural Flavours (NF)** — Derived directly from plant, animal, or microbial sources via physical, enzymatic, or microbiological processes.
2. **Nature-Identical Flavours (NIF)** — Chemically synthesized molecules whose structure is identical to those found in nature.
3. **Artificial Flavours (AF)** — Synthetic molecules with no natural counterpart (e.g., ethyl vanillin, allyl phenoxyacetate).
4. **Process Flavours (PF)** — Generated *in situ* through controlled thermal processing such as the Maillard reaction.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the Odour Activity Value (OAV)? Why is it more useful than absolute concentration in flavour creation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** OAV is the dimensionless ratio of the actual concentration of an aroma compound in a food matrix to its orthonasal detection threshold in that same matrix.

$$
\text{OAV} = \frac{C_i}{OT_i}
$$

**Why more useful than absolute concentration (1.5 marks):** Because sensory perception is non-linear and depends on the compound's threshold, two compounds present at the same absolute concentration can have vastly different sensory impacts. OAV directly correlates with perceived intensity, allowing the flavourist to rank compounds by actual sensory contribution. A compound with OAV ≥ 1 is sensory-active, while OAV < 1 is sub-threshold and irrelevant.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Marks are deducted if students confuse **OAV** (concentration/threshold) with **FD factor** (dilution series). Memorize both equations distinctly.
> - Always state **units** explicitly in numerical answers. The examiner will deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> - Avoid using the term "flavour" interchangeably with "aroma". Aroma = volatile + olfactory; Flavour = aroma + taste + chemesthesis. This distinction is frequently tested.
> - In regulatory questions, specify the **regulation number** (e.g., FSSAI 2011, EU 1334/2008) — generic answers score partial marks only.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **six-stage pipeline of industrial flavour creation**, with examples for each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A flavour chemist is creating a "fresh strawberry" flavour. GC-MS analysis of authentic strawberry (*Fragaria × ananassa*) reveals the following key compounds with their concentrations and odour thresholds:

| Compound | Concentration (mg/kg) | Threshold (mg/kg) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Ethyl 2-methylbutyrate | 1.20 | 0.10 |
| Furaneol | 4.50 | 5.00 |
| γ-Decalactone | 0.80 | 0.40 |
| (Z)-3-Hexenal | 0.30 | 0.20 |

Calculate the OAV of each compound, rank them by sensory contribution, and identify the **primary character-impact compound**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.A(a) — Six-Stage Flavour Creation Pipeline (7 marks)**

**Stage 1 — Brief Definition and Target Profiling (1 mark):**
The flavourist receives a sensory brief specifying the target flavour, regulatory constraints, cost ceiling, and thermal/mechanical stability requirements. *Example:* A brief for "ripe Alphonso mango" flavour for a beverage matrix with pH 3.2.

**Stage 2 — Analytical Deconstruction (1.5 marks):**
The target product is subjected to GC-MS and GC-O analysis to identify the 20–40 aroma-active volatiles out of the 800+ total volatiles. Aroma Extract Dilution Analysis (AEDA) ranks compounds by FD factor. *Example:* For strawberry, key compounds include ethyl 2-methylbutyrate, furaneol, and γ-decalactone.

**Stage 3 — Compound Selection and Sourcing (1 mark):**
Each identified molecule is sourced either as a natural isolate, a nature-identical synthetic, or a process-generated molecule. *Example:* γ-Decalactone can be sourced from natural coconut extract or synthesized via lipase-catalyzed lactonization.

**Stage 4 — Formulation and Carrier Selection (1.5 marks):**
The compounds are blended in a carrier (ethanol, propylene glycol, triacetin, or vegetable oil) at the correct ratio. The carrier governs solubility, dispersibility, and release kinetics. *Example:* A strawberry flavour is typically carried in ethanol at 20–30 % w/w.

**Stage 5 — Stability and Application Testing (1 mark):**
The prototype is dosed into the actual food matrix and subjected to pH, thermal, and oxidative stress tests. *Example:* Strawberry flavour dosed at 0.1 % in a carbonated beverage stored at 40 °C for 12 weeks.

**Stage 6 — Sensory Validation and Commercialization (1 mark):**
Final validation by trained panels and target consumer groups. The flavour is released only if acceptance exceeds 70 % preference. *Example:* A triangle test confirms the synthetic strawberry matches the natural reference at p < 0.05.

---

#### **Solution to Q.B(b) — OAV Calculation and Compound Ranking (7 marks)**

Using the OAV formula:

$$
\text{OAV} = \frac{C_i}{OT_i}
$$

**Calculation for each compound:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OAV}_{\text{Ethyl 2-methylbutyrate}} &= \frac{1.20}{0.10} = 12.0 \\
\text{OAV}_{\text{Furaneol}} &= \frac{4.50}{5.00} = 0.90 \\
\text{OAV}_{\gamma\text{-Decalactone}} &= \frac{0.80}{0.40} = 2.0 \\
\text{OAV}_{(Z)\text{-3-Hexenal}} &= \frac{0.30}{0.20} = 1.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Ranking Table:**

| Rank | Compound | OAV | Sensory Role |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Ethyl 2-methylbutyrate | **12.0** | **Primary character-impact** (fruity, estery) |
| 2 | γ-Decalactone | 2.0 | Secondary contributor (peachy, creamy) |
| 3 | (Z)-3-Hexenal | 1.5 | Secondary contributor (green, leafy) |
| 4 | Furaneol | 0.90 | Sub-threshold (no direct sensory impact) |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The **primary character-impact compound** is **ethyl 2-methylbutyrate** (OAV = 12.0), which is the *sine qua non* of the "fresh strawberry" flavour and must be the lead component in any formulation. Furaneol, despite its high absolute concentration, is sub-threshold in this matrix and contributes only synergistically.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **chemistry of the Maillard reaction** in the context of flavour creation. List **four classes of aroma compounds** generated and give **two food examples** for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A flavour house wants to produce a "roasted coffee" flavour using the following process-flavour approach. Calculate the **required mass of 2-furfurylthiol** (the key aroma compound of coffee) for a **1000 kg** batch of liquid flavour concentrate, if the dosing rate is **5 ppm (parts per million)**. Express your answer in **grams**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.B(a) — Maillard Reaction Chemistry (7 marks)**

**Overview (2 marks):** The Maillard reaction is a non-enzymatic browning reaction between the carbonyl group of a reducing sugar (e.g., glucose) and the free amino group of an amino acid, peptide, or protein. It proceeds through three main stages: (i) formation of glycosylamine and Amadori rearrangement, (ii) degradation to dicarbonyls, and (iii) final stage with Strecker degradation and formation of melanoidins.

**Compound classes generated and food examples (5 marks — 1.25 marks per class):**

1. **Pyrazines** — Formed by condensation of two α-aminoketones.
   - *Examples:* 2,3-Dimethylpyrazine → roasted peanut, 2-Ethyl-3,5-dimethylpyrazine → baked potato.

2. **Furans and Furanones** — From sugar dehydration and Amadori rearrangement.
   - *Examples:* Furfural → caramel, maltol → roasted barley (malt).

3. **Thiophenes and Thiols** — From sulfur-containing amino acids (cysteine, methionine).
   - *Examples:* 2-Furfurylthiol → fresh roasted coffee, dimethyl sulfide → cooked cabbage.

4. **Strecker Aldehydes** — From oxidative decarboxylation of amino acids in presence of dicarbonyls.
   - *Examples:* 2-Methylbutanal → malty bread, 3-Methylbutanal → chocolate aroma.

---

#### **Solution to Q.B(b) — Mass Calculation for 2-Furfurylthiol (7 marks)**

Given:
- Dosing rate: $5\ \text{ppm} = 5\ \text{mg/kg}$
- Batch size: $1000\ \text{kg}$

**Conversion logic (1 mark):** $1\ \text{ppm}$ means 1 mg of solute per kg of solution. Therefore, $5\ \text{ppm} = 5\ \text{mg/kg}$.

**Calculation (5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass of 2-furfurylthiol} &= 5\ \text{mg/kg} \times 1000\ \text{kg} \\
&= 5000\ \text{mg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to grams:

$$
\begin{aligned}
5000\ \text{mg} \times \frac{1\ \text{g}}{1000\ \text{mg}} &= 5\ \text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer (1 mark):** **5 grams** of 2-furfurylthiol is required to produce 1000 kg of the "roasted coffee" liquid flavour concentrate at 5 ppm dosage.

**Practical Note:** 2-Furfurylthiol is extraordinarily potent — its odour threshold in water is approximately **0.01 µg/kg** (10 parts per *trillion*). Hence, only 5 g suffices to impart a strong coffee character to 1000 kg of concentrate. This is the essence of high-impact flavour creation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Flavour Creation (Module 4)**

### **Core Definitions to Memorize**
- ☐ **Flavour** = Aroma (volatile + olfactory) + Taste (5 basic tastes) + Chemesthesis (pungency, cooling).
- ☐ **Aroma** is detected by the olfactory epithelium; **taste** by taste receptor cells on the tongue.
- ☐ **OAV** is concentration/threshold; **FD factor** is the last dilution detected in AEDA.

### **Key Formulas**
- ☐ $\text{OAV} = C_i / OT_i$
- ☐ $C_1 V_1 = C_2 V_2$ (dilution)
- ☐ $\%\,(w/v) = (m_{solute}/V_{solution}) \times 100$
- ☐ $\text{ppm} = \text{mg/kg}$

### **The Five Basic Tastes**
- ☐ Sweet, Sour, Salty, Bitter, **Umami** (the fifth taste, recognized in 1985, MSG as reference).

### **Flavour Categories**
- ☐ **NF, NIF, AF, PF** — and the regulatory differences between them.
- ☐ Ethyl vanillin is **~3× stronger** than natural vanillin.

### **Critical Aroma-Active Compounds**
- ☐ Strecker aldehydes (malty) → 2-Methylbutanal, 3-Methylbutanal.
- ☐ Pyrazines (roasted) → 2,3-Dimethylpyrazine.
- ☐ Esters (fruity) → Ethyl 2-methylbutyrate, Isoamyl acetate.
- ☐ Terpenes (citrus/floral) → Limonene, Linalool.
- ☐ Sulfur compounds (meaty) → 2-Furfurylthiol, Dimethyl sulfide.

### **Stability Rules**
- ☐ Use **antioxidants** to prevent oxidation.
- ☐ Use **stable alternatives** in acidic matrices (replace ethyl butyrate with isoamyl acetate).
- ☐ Use **encapsulation** (maltodextrin) for thermal protection.
- ☐ Use **opaque/amber packaging** and **nitrogen flushing** for photo-oxidation protection.

### **The Industrial Pipeline**
- ☐ **Brief → Deconstruction → Selection → Formulation → Stability Testing → Sensory Validation → Launch**.

### **Sensory Evaluation Constants**
- ☐ Weber's constant for taste ≈ 0.05; for smell ≈ 0.20 (smell is less sensitive to relative differences).
- ☐ Trained panels: 8–12 panelists; Consumer panels: 60–150 respondents.
- ☐ Acceptance threshold for commercialization: ≥ 70 % positive preference.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Examiner's Note (Highest Weightage Topics in Module 4):**
> 1. **Maillard reaction** in process flavour creation (~14 marks expected).
> 2. **OAV calculations** with chemical ranking (~7–10 marks).
> 3. **Flavour stability** degradation pathways and countermeasures (~7 marks).
> 4. **Industrial pipeline** stages with examples (~7 marks).
> 5. **Regulatory framework** (FEMA GRAS, FSSAI, EU 1334/2008) — minimum 1 question per exam.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT504 - FOOD ADDITIVES AND FLAVORINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Sensory Analysis of Flavour** is a scientific discipline that applies principles of experimental design and statistical analysis under controlled conditions to evoke, measure, analyse, and interpret human responses to flavour stimuli as perceived through the integrated physiological mechanisms of the **gustatory (taste)**, **olfactory (smell)**, **trigeminal (chemesthesis)**, and **visual/auditory/tactile** sensory modalities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Per ISO 5492:2008):** "Sensory Analysis — A scientific discipline used to evoke, measure, analyse, and interpret reactions to characteristics of food and materials as they are perceived by the senses of sight, smell, taste, touch, and hearing."

In the context of flavour, the emphasis is placed on the **orthonasal** (sniffing through the nose) and **retronasal** (aromas reaching the olfactory epithelium from the mouth during swallowing) perception of volatile compounds, combined with the five basic tastes (**sweet, sour, salty, bitter, umami**) and chemesthetic sensations (**pungency, cooling, astringency, tingling**).

## 1.2 The Integrated Flavour Perception Model

Flavour is **multimodal** — it is not a single sensation but a perceptual construct synthesized by the brain. The relevant KTU taxonomy is:

| Modality | Stimulus Type | Receptor Location | Key Additive/Food Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gustation (Taste)** | Non-volatile molecules in solution | Taste buds (tongue, soft palate) | MSG (umami), citric acid (sour) |
| **Olfaction (Smell)** | Volatile aroma compounds | Olfactory epithelium (nasal cavity) | Vanillin, esters, aldehydes |
| **Chemesthesis (Triterminal)** | Pungent/irritant compounds | Trigeminal nerve endings (mouth/nose) | Capsaicin (chilli), menthol (cooling) |
| **Visual** | Light reflection | Retina (eyes) | Caramel colour, anthocyanins |
| **Auditory/Tactile** | Sound waves / mechanical deformation | Cochlea / Mechanoreceptors | Carbonation fizz, crispy texture |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Flavour Orchestra"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A Flavour Concert**
> Imagine eating a strawberry candy as a **symphony orchestra performance**:
> - **Taste buds** are the **string section** (basic but constant) — sweet, sour, salty.
> - **Olfactory receptors** are the **woodwinds and violins** — they provide the melody (the strawberry "note").
> - **Trigeminal receptors** are the **percussion** — they add excitement (the cooling of menthol or burn of chilli).
> - **The Conductor** is the **brain's orbitofrontal cortex**, integrating every input into a single perceptual experience.
> - **Sensory analysis** is the **music critic** with a clipboard, systematically rating each section's contribution using trained ears (a trained panel).

Without the critic (sensory analyst), the orchestra plays beautifully, but no one can tell you *which section is too loud* or *which is missing*. Sensory analysis is therefore the **measurement bridge** between a human's subjective experience and the objective quality control of food additives and flavorings.

## 1.4 Importance in Food Additive/Flavoring Industry

1. **Quality Control (QC):** Detecting batch-to-batch variation in flavoring agents.
2. **Product Development (R&D):** Optimizing new flavor formulations.
3. **Shelf-life Studies:** Tracking flavour degradation over storage.
4. **Regulatory Compliance:** Establishing specifications for natural vs. artificial flavorings.
5. **Consumer Acceptance:** Linking sensory data to market success.
6. **Additive Stability:** Verifying that preservatives, antioxidants, etc., do not introduce off-flavours.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** The KTU Board frequently asks students to distinguish between *objective* (instrumental) and *subjective* (sensory) analysis. **The human nose can detect volatile compounds at parts-per-trillion (ppt) levels — far below the detection limits of most instruments (GC-MS, e-nose).** This is why sensory analysis remains *irreplaceable*.

## 1.5 Why Sensory Analysis Cannot Be Replaced by Instruments

Although Gas Chromatography-Mass Spectrometry (GC-MS) and Electronic Noses (E-noses) are powerful, they cannot:
- Predict **perceptual interactions** (e.g., synergy or suppression of aromas).
- Measure **psychological factors** (e.g., expectation, satiety, cultural preference).
- Detect **matrix effects** — how a flavoring behaves in a complex food vs. in isolation.
- Quantify **pleasantness, desirability, or emotional response** (hedonics).

Hence, sensory analysis remains the **gold standard** for flavour evaluation.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Sensory Tests (KTU Taxonomy)

The KTU 2024 syllabus adopts the **ASTM/ISO classification** which divides sensory methods into three major categories:

### A. Difference/Discriminative Tests
Answer the question: *"Is there a perceptible difference between two or more samples?"*

| Test | Samples | Assessor Task | Stat. Analysis | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Triangle Test** | 3 (2 same, 1 different) | Identify the odd one | Binomial | Detecting subtle additive effects |
| **Duo-Trio Test** | 3 (1 reference, 2 coded) | Match to reference | Binomial | Comparison against a control |
| **Paired Comparison (Directional)** | 2 | Choose sample with more of an attribute | Sign test | Sweetness intensity comparison |
| **Paired Preference** | 2 | Choose preferred | Binomial | Consumer liking |
| **Two-Out-of-Five Test** | 5 | Identify the two identical | Binomial | Trained panels, weak differences |
| **A-Not A Test** | Reference + coded | Classify as A or Not-A | Chi-square | Flavour matching after storage |
| **Ranking Test** | 3–6 | Rank by attribute intensity | Friedman | Comparing flavoring levels |

### B. Descriptive Tests
Answer the question: *"What are the quantitative differences in specific attributes?"*

- **Flavour Profile Method (FPM)** — developed by Arthur D. Little (1949); 4–6 trained panelists describe, rate intensity on a 0–15 structured scale.
- **Texture Profile Method (TPM)** — Generalised for texture.
- **Quantitative Descriptive Analysis (QDA)** — Stone, Sidel, Oliver (1974); 10–12 panelists; 15-cm unstructured line scale.
- **Spectrum Descriptive Analysis (SDA)** — Sensory Spectrum Inc.; uses reference standards.
- **Free-Choice Profiling (FCP)** — Each panelist generates own descriptors; multivariate analysis.

### C. Affective/Preference Tests
Answer the question: *"Which sample is preferred, and how much?"*

- **Hedonic Rating (9-point)** — Like extremely to dislike extremely.
- **Paired Preference** — Forced choice.
- **Ranking Preference** — Rank by liking.
- **Just-About-Right (JAR) Scale** — For specific attribute optimization.
- **Acceptance Test** — Would you eat this daily?

## 2.2 The Sensory Panel — Selection, Training, and Monitoring

### 2.2.1 Types of Panels

| Panel Type | Size | Training | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Trained/Expert Panel** | 5–15 | Highly trained | Descriptive, discriminative | FPM, QDA |
| **Semi-Trained Panel** | 10–30 | Brief orientation | Screening | Discrimination tests |
| **Consumer Panel** | 50–300+ | Untrained | Affective, preference | Hedonic testing |

### 2.2.2 Panelist Selection Criteria (ISO 8586:2012)

1. **Health:** No allergies, colds, anosmia, ageusia, or chronic illness.
2. **Age:** Typically 18–60 years.
3. **Availability & Reliability:** Commitment to repeated sessions.
4. **Communicative Ability:** Ability to describe sensations verbally.
5. **Sensory Acuity:** Above-threshold sensitivity to basic tastes and odours.
6. **No Smoking / No Alcohol** within hours of testing.
7. **Psychological Profile:** Non-extreme responders; willing to consume varied foods.

### 2.2.3 Training Procedure

A structured training protocol follows the **5-stage ISO model**:
1. **Stage 1 — Recruitment & Screening** (Medical, psychological tests).
2. **Stage 2 — Orientation** (Familiarisation with procedures, scales, descriptors).
3. **Stage 3 — Basic Taste & Odour Recognition** (Identification of the 5 tastes + 50+ common odours).
4. **Stage 4 — Descriptive Training** (Use of reference standards, intensity scaling).
5. **Stage 5 — Calibration & Performance Monitoring** (Periodic re-evaluation with standards).

## 2.3 Sensory Test Conditions (ISO 8589:2007)

### 2.3.1 Testing Booths
- Individual booths (≈ 1 m wide).
- Neutral-coloured walls (grey/beige).
- Standardised lighting (D65 or cool white).
- Red/green lighting available to mask colour differences.

### 2.3.2 Sample Preparation
- **Temperature:** Controlled (commonly **20 °C for liquids, 60–70 °C for hot beverages**).
- **Portion Size:** 15–30 mL (liquid), 5–10 g (solid).
- **Coded with 3-digit random numbers** to prevent bias.
- **Presentation Order:** Balanced (Latin square) to cancel position bias.
- **Water & Plain Crackers (Pala/Wheat)** for palate cleansing between samples.
- **Waiting Time:** Typically **30 s–1 min** between samples.

### 2.3.3 Environmental Conditions
- **Room Temperature:** 20–22 °C.
- **Humidity:** 45–55 % RH.
- **Odor-Free Environment:** No perfumes, no strong-smelling cleaning agents.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Criterion | Variables / Notes | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Minimum Correct Responses (Triangle Test)** | $X_{min}$ from binomial table at $\alpha = 0.05$ (one-tailed) | $n$ = total judgements | If $X_{correct} \geq X_{min}$ → significant difference |
| 2 | **Binomial Probability** | $P(X = k) = \binom{n}{k} p^{k} (1-p)^{n-k}$ | $p = 1/3$ (triangle), $p = 1/2$ (paired) | Statistical significance of difference tests |
| 3 | **Significance Level (KTU Convention)** | $\alpha = 0.05$ (5 %), $\beta = 0.10$ (10 %) | Type I and Type II errors | Setting test power |
| 4 | **β-binomial / Bradley-Terry Model** | $P(i > j) = \frac{\pi_i}{\pi_i + \pi_j}$ | $\pi$ = "worth" parameter | Ranking data analysis |
| 5 | **Hedonic Score (Mean ± SD)** | $\bar{H} = \frac{\sum H_i}{n}, \quad s = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (H_i - \bar{H})^2}{n-1}}$ | $H_i$ = individual score, $n$ = panel size | Consumer test reporting |
| 6 | **ANOVA (One-Way) F-statistic** | $F = \frac{MS_{between}}{MS_{within}}$ | Compares sample means | Descriptive analysis |
| 7 | **Critical F value (95 %)** | $F_{crit} = F_{(k-1,\, n-k;\, 0.05)}$ | $k$ = samples, $n$ = total obs. | Decision rule |
| 8 | **Coefficient of Variation (CV)** | $CV = \frac{s}{\bar{x}} \times 100\,\%$ | Reproducibility indicator | Panel performance check |
| 9 | **Panel Reproducibility Index (p)** | $p_{rep} = \frac{N_{correct}}{N_{total}}$ | $N_{correct}$ = matching identifications | Training validation |
| 10 | **Weber's Law (Just Noticeable Difference)** | $\frac{\Delta I}{I} = k$ | $\Delta I$ = JND, $I$ = stimulus, $k$ = constant | Sensitivity threshold |
| 11 | **Fechner's Law (Subjective Intensity)** | $S = k \log \frac{I}{I_0}$ | $I_0$ = threshold intensity | Psychophysics of taste/smell |
| 12 | **Stevens' Power Law** | $S = k(I - I_0)^n$ | $n$ = Stevens' exponent | Modern intensity scaling |
| 13 | **Triangle Test Critical Table (n=12)** | $X_{min} = 7$ at $\alpha = 0.05$ | One-tailed | Quick reference |
| 14 | **Triangle Test Critical Table (n=24)** | $X_{min} = 11$ at $\alpha = 0.05$ | One-tailed | Standard panel size |
| 15 | **Duo-Trio Critical Table (n=20)** | $X_{min} = 12$ at $\alpha = 0.05$ | One-tailed | Standard |
| 16 | **Friedman Statistic** | $F_r = \frac{12}{nk(k+1)} \sum R_j^2 - 3n(k+1)$ | $R_j$ = rank sums | Ranking tests |
| 17 | **Number of Replicates** | $n_{rep} = \frac{Z_{\alpha/2}^2 \cdot \sigma^2}{E^2}$ | $E$ = acceptable error | Power analysis |
| 18 | **Sample Size (Hedonic)** | $n = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{d^2}$ | $d$ = margin of error | Consumer studies |
| 19 | **OAV (Odour Activity Value)** | $OAV = \frac{C_i}{OT_i}$ | $C_i$ = concentration, $OT_i$ = odour threshold | Flavour impact ranking |
| 20 | **Aroma Extraction Dilution (AEDA) Factor** | $FD = 3^n$ | $n$ = dilution steps | Identifying key aroma compounds |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Formula 9, 11, 12:** The KTU Board often asks the difference between Fechner's *logarithmic* law and Stevens' *power* law. Modern sensory science uses **Stevens' Power Law** because it better fits empirical data for chemical senses (taste exponent $n \approx 0.8$ for sucrose; smell exponents vary widely from 0.2 to 0.8).

## 2.5 Statistical Decision Rules (Quick-Reference)

| Test | Statistic Used | Decision Rule (at $\alpha = 0.05$) |
|---|---|---|
| Triangle | Binomial table | Number of correct > critical value |
| Paired (Directional) | Sign test | Number of "more" responses > critical |
| Ranking | Friedman | Computed $\chi^2_r$ > tabulated $\chi^2$ |
| Hedonic | ANOVA + Tukey HSD | $p < 0.05$ between samples |
| QDA | ANOVA per attribute | Mean difference > LSD |

## 2.6 Real-World Industrial Utility

- **Coca-Cola vs Pepsi:** Hedonic testing has been a mainstay of the cola wars.
- **Firmenich, Givaudan, IFF (Flavor Houses):** Use QDA + trained panels to evaluate flavorings worth billions.
- **FSSAI / FDA / EFSA:** Require sensory data for additive approval.
- **Shelf-life dating:** Sensory panels track off-flavour development in fortified foods.
- **Plant-based meat startups (Impossible, Beyond):** Use QDA to mimic meaty flavour notes (pyrazines, Maillard compounds).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Triangle Test Interpretation

**Problem Statement:**
A food company adds a new flavoring compound (0.05 % ethyl maltol) to a vanilla ice cream. To determine if a perceptible difference exists versus the control, a triangle test is conducted with **24 trained panelists**. The probability of guessing correctly by chance is $p_0 = 1/3$. A total of **12 panelists** correctly identified the odd sample. At $\alpha = 0.05$ (one-tailed), is the difference significant?

### Step 1: State the Hypotheses

- $H_0$: $p = 1/3$ (no difference; correct answers are by chance).
- $H_1$: $p > 1/3$ (perceptible difference exists).

### Step 2: Identify the Critical Value

For a triangle test, $n = 24$, $p_0 = 1/3$, $\alpha = 0.05$ (one-tailed). From standard binomial tables:

$$
X_{min} = 11
$$

> The minimum number of correct responses required to reject $H_0$ is 11.

### Step 3: Compare Observed vs. Critical

Observed: $X_{obs} = 12$
Critical: $X_{min} = 11$

$$
12 > 11 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Reject } H_0
$$

### Step 4: Conclusion

There is **statistical evidence at the 5 % level** that the new ethyl maltol formulation produces a perceptibly different flavour from the control.

### Step 5: Type II Error Consideration

Calculate the exact probability of obtaining $X = 12$ by chance using the binomial formula:

$$
P(X = k) = \binom{n}{k} p_0^{k} (1 - p_0)^{n-k}
$$

$$
P(X = 12) = \binom{24}{12} \left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{12} \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{12}
$$

Let us compute $\binom{24}{12}$:

$$
\binom{24}{12} = \frac{24!}{12!\,12!} = 2{,}704{,}156
$$

Substituting:

$$
P(X = 12) = 2{,}704{,}156 \times \left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{12} \times \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{12}
$$

$$
P(X = 12) = 2{,}704{,}156 \times \frac{2^{12}}{3^{24}}
$$

$$
P(X = 12) = 2{,}704{,}156 \times \frac{4{,}096}{2.824 \times 10^{11}}
$$

$$
P(X = 12) \approx 0.0392
$$

So the exact one-tailed $p$-value is:

$$
p\text{-value} = \sum_{k=12}^{24} \binom{24}{k} \left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^k \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{24-k} \approx 0.043
$$

Since $0.043 < 0.05$, the result is **significant**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Always use the critical-table value (1-step shortcut) for Part A questions but show the exact $p$-value calculation in Part B for full marks.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Hedonic Rating with ANOVA

**Problem Statement:**
A new natural mint flavoring is being tested against two commercial brands (Brand A, Brand B) using a **9-point hedonic scale** by **30 consumer panelists**. The mean hedonic scores are: New Formulation = **7.8**, Brand A = **6.5**, Brand B = **7.0**. Standard deviations are 1.2, 1.4, 1.3 respectively. Use one-way ANOVA at $\alpha = 0.05$ to test for significant differences.

### Step 1: Compute Grand Mean

$$
\bar{X}_{grand} = \frac{7.8 + 6.5 + 7.0}{3} = \frac{21.3}{3} = 7.10
$$

### Step 2: Compute Sum of Squares Between (SSB)

$$
SSB = \sum_{j=1}^{k} n_j (\bar{X}_j - \bar{X}_{grand})^2
$$

Assuming $n_1 = n_2 = n_3 = 30$:

$$
SSB = 30\left[(7.8 - 7.1)^2 + (6.5 - 7.1)^2 + (7.0 - 7.1)^2\right]
$$

$$
SSB = 30\left[(0.7)^2 + (-0.6)^2 + (-0.1)^2\right]
$$

$$
SSB = 30\left[0.49 + 0.36 + 0.01\right]
$$

$$
SSB = 30 \times 0.86 = 25.80
$$

### Step 3: Compute Sum of Squares Within (SSW)

Using pooled within-group variance:

$$
SSW = \sum_{j=1}^{k} (n_j - 1) s_j^2
$$

$$
SSW = 29 \times 1.2^2 + 29 \times 1.4^2 + 29 \times 1.3^2
$$

$$
SSW = 29(1.44 + 1.96 + 1.69) = 29 \times 5.09 = 147.61
$$

### Step 4: Compute Degrees of Freedom

- $df_{between} = k - 1 = 3 - 1 = 2$
- $df_{within} = N - k = 90 - 3 = 87$

### Step 5: Compute Mean Squares

$$
MS_{between} = \frac{SSB}{df_{between}} = \frac{25.80}{2} = 12.90
$$

$$
MS_{within} = \frac{SSW}{df_{within}} = \frac{147.61}{87} \approx 1.697
$$

### Step 6: Compute F-statistic

$$
F = \frac{MS_{between}}{MS_{within}} = \frac{12.90}{1.697} \approx 7.60
$$

### Step 7: Compare to Critical F

Critical value $F_{crit(0.05,\, 2,\, 87)} \approx 3.10$.

$$
7.60 > 3.10 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Reject } H_0
$$

### Step 8: Conclusion

There is a **statistically significant difference** in consumer liking among the three samples at $\alpha = 0.05$.

### Step 9: Post-hoc Tukey HSD (KTU 2024 Bonus Step)

$$
HSD = q_{\alpha,\, k,\, df_{within}} \times \sqrt{\frac{MS_{within}}{n}}
$$

With $q_{0.05, 3, 87} \approx 3.37$:

$$
HSD = 3.37 \times \sqrt{\frac{1.697}{30}} = 3.37 \times 0.2378 \approx 0.801
$$

Pairwise differences:
- New vs. Brand A: $7.8 - 6.5 = 1.3 > 0.801$ → **Significant**
- New vs. Brand B: $7.8 - 7.0 = 0.8 < 0.801$ → **Not significant** (marginally close)
- Brand A vs. Brand B: $7.0 - 6.5 = 0.5 < 0.801$ → **Not significant**

> **Interpretation:** The new natural mint is significantly preferred over Brand A, but is not statistically distinguishable from Brand B in consumer liking.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Odour Activity Value (OAV) Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A strawberry flavoring contains 4 volatile compounds. Determine which compound is the most impactful using OAV.

| Compound | Concentration $C_i$ (mg/kg) | Odour Threshold $OT_i$ (mg/kg) |
|---|---|---|
| Ethyl butyrate | 8.0 | 0.001 |
| Furaneol | 0.5 | 0.025 |
| Linalool | 1.2 | 0.040 |
| γ-Decalactone | 0.3 | 0.010 |

### Step 1: Apply OAV Formula

$$
OAV_i = \frac{C_i}{OT_i}
$$

- Ethyl butyrate: $OAV = 8.0 / 0.001 = 8000$
- Furaneol: $OAV = 0.5 / 0.025 = 20$
- Linalool: $OAV = 1.2 / 0.040 = 30$
- γ-Decalactone: $OAV = 0.3 / 0.010 = 30$

### Step 2: Rank by Impact

**Ethyl butyrate (OAV = 8000)** dominates — it is the most aroma-active compound, despite its perceived concentration not being the highest.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** OAV > 1 indicates the compound contributes to aroma. OAV > 100 indicates a major character-impact compound. Ethyl butyrate's massive OAV makes it the likely "key aroma" of strawberry flavoring.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Appropriate Test

| Objective | Best Test | Sample Size | Panel Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| Detecting subtle additive effect | Triangle | 24–30 | Trained |
| Quantifying attribute intensity | QDA / FPM | 10–12 | Trained/Expert |
| Consumer acceptance | 9-pt Hedonic | 75–150 | Consumer |
| Comparing 4 sweetener levels | Ranking + Friedman | 20–30 | Semi-trained |
| Product reformulation screening | Paired Preference | 50+ | Consumer |
| Shelf-life monitoring | Descriptive + Trained | 10–12 | Trained |
| Mapping flavor profile | Free Choice Profile | 12–15 | Trained |
| Optimizing saltiness (just right) | JAR + Penalty Analysis | 75+ | Consumer |
